title: The problem with polarization research in an age of extremism
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Forums & Communities; News Media; Academic Literature
Tags: political psychology; social conflict; democratic values
abstract: "I have been gravely disappointed with the white moderate. I have almost reached the regrettable conclusion that the Negro's great stumbling block in his stride toward freedom is not the White Citizen's Counciler or the Ku Klux Klanner, but the white moderate, who is more devoted to "order" than to justice; who prefers a negative peace which is the absence of tension to a positive peace which is the presence of justice"-Martin Luther King, Jr., Letter from Birmingham Jail (1963) Over the past decade, political polarization has become one of the most studied topics in the behavioral and social sciences. A search for "political polarization" in the Web of Science database (conducted on 23 October 2025) yielded 13,387 results, and more than 60 per cent of which were published since 2020. During the same period, authoritarian and anti-democratic movements have gained strength across diverse contexts. A Friedrich-Ebert-Stiftung (2023) study reported that support for a dictatorship led by a "strong leader" in Germany increased, and endorsement of social-Darwinist statements such as "some lives are worthless" more than doubled compared with previous years. Similarly, the Leipzig Authoritarianism Study (Decker et al., 2024) reported renewed increases in xenophobia, chauvinism, and the downplaying of Nazism. In Germany, national reports indicate that extremist offenses have increased year after year (Reuters, 2024). Similar patterns are also observable in the United States and Brazil where not only national surveys reveal an increased openness to military coups and one-party rule (Pew Research Center, 2024a;2024b) but also extremists have attempted to overturn electoral outcomes by storming their respective congresses (European Parliament, 2023).Authoritarian, conservative, and reactionary networks have been among the principal drivers of the surge in distrust, conspiracism, and anti-scientific sentiment (Backhaus et al., 2023;Benegal et al, 2022;Cologna et al., 2025;Dakin et al., 2023;Pavlovic et al., 2022;Philipp-Müller et al., 2022;van Bavel et al, 2022;van der Linden, 2020;Vlasceanu et al., 2024;Voelkel et al., 2024). Coordinated reactionary movements couple conspiratorial and antiscientific narratives as a central element of their political strategy, which, in the recent decades, has been successful in undermining pluralist opponents, galvanizing reactionary followers, shifting blame away from authoritarian sources, and weakening democratic values and institutions (Ren et al., 2022). Through national and international political campaigns, these networks have attempted to replace societal trust and factual knowledge with alternative belief systems that frame them both as victims of ideological attacks and as opponents of a corrupt establishment against the "will of the people" (Golder, 2016;Mudde, 2007;Rothmund et al., 2020;2022;Van Hauwaert et al, 2018;2020). By portraying democracy and science as corrupt, elitist, or ideologically captured establishments, these actors erode public trust in civic society, empirical inquiry, and in the institutions that sustain both (van der Meer et al., 2026). Moreover, in framing pluralist democracy and scientific expertise as nefarious, they reframe their quest for political power and dominance as a project of "renewal" and "social progress".foot_0 This rather paradoxical-and populist (Golder, 2016;Mudde, 2007)-framing has been a potent tool spreading and legitimizing hostility towards pluralist democracy and its knowledge-based institutions. Opposition to democracy, science, and institutional expertise is therefore not an accidental feature of anti-democratic populism and authoritarianism, but its defining component (Capraro et al., 2026;Levitsky and Ziblatt, 2019;Sandel, 2022;Snyder, 2021;Stanley, 2020).These developments illustrate that contemporary polarization does not occur in a vacuum. Rather, it is embedded within a wider ideological shift towards authoritarianism marked by the normalization of violence, systematic dis/misinformation campaigns, and hostility toward scientific expertise (Azevedo and Jost, 2021;Roozenbeek and van der Linden, 2024;van der Linden, 2023;Van Dijck et al., 2018;Vilanova et al., 2025). Against this backdrop, the widespread assumption of the field that polarization is inherently detrimental warrants re-examination. Notably, most empirical studies conceptualize polarization either in ideological terms-typically as increasing divergence or clustering of citizens' attitudes along an ideological spectrum (Philipp-Müller et al., 2022)-or in affective terms, defined as intensified animus toward political outgroups coupled with ingroup favoritism (Iyengar et al., 2012). While analytically tractable, this approach is nonetheless conceptually naïve and methodologically too coarse for the current political moment. Polarization can reflect (i) agonistic democratic conflict-a constitutive feature of pluralist democracy in which adversaries contest values and policies while accepting democratic norms, rules, and outcomes (Mouffe, 2000)-or (ii) democratically corrosive conflict, in which political identity is asymmetrically organized and strategically exploited into distrustful camps and wielded to justify rule-breaking, delegitimizing of elections and pluralist institutions, and erosion of accountability (Somer and McCoy, 2019). Rather than reflecting a symmetric breakdown of civility, this form of dissension constitutes a well-documented, elite-driven pathway to democratic backsliding (Bermeo, 2016). This framing constitutes a tacit belief that social harmony and dialogue are the ideal state of democratic life, and political antagonism is a failure of civility. In the current moment of rising extremism, not all polarization is equivalent nor undesirable. Historical experience underscores this asymmetry. Normative appeals to civility and harmony, when applied indiscriminately, can obscure the moral distinction between domination and resistance-condemning confrontational opposition even in contexts where democratic survival or basic human dignity is at stake. Under conditions of systematic persecution, conflict is not a pathology of democracy but one of the few remaining mechanisms through which injustice can be resisted. Moreover, opposition between democratic and authoritarian values cannot be treated as symmetrical disagreement. When one side rejects pluralism, electoral legitimacy, or epistemic accountability, then 'reconciliation' and even 'civility' function less as neutral civic virtues than as asymmetric constraints, and they risk unilaterally disarming prodemocracy forces. Indeed, democratically corrosive conflict primarily disciplines democratic defenders while leaving anti-democratic actors free to escalate, delegitimize, and capture institutions. Under such asymmetric conditions, appeals to reduce polarization risk equating resistance to authoritarianism with intolerance.The assumption that polarization must be reduced carries profound epistemic and political consequences for social and political psychology and political science. It shapes research designs, variable operationalizations, and the normative interpretations of findings. Interventions aimed at reducing affective polarization, for example, often promote empathy and perspective-taking across ideological divides without considering whether the underlying attitudes sustain or undermine the very conditions that allow for democratic discourse and evidence-based reasoning (e.g., Gillissen et al., 2025;Santos et al., 2022;Tausch et al., 2024;Voelkel et al., 2024;Warner et al., 2020). When authoritarian and anti-scientific movements are gaining strength, such naïve depolarization efforts risk demobilizing both scientific communities and democratic publics at precisely the moment when active defense of evidence and reasoned inquiry is most needed. Safeguarding the norms that make civil society and empirical inquiry possible requires an explicit commitment to democratic rules, pluralism, and epistemic standards (e.g., electoral legitimacy, institutional accountability, and evidence-constrained claims-making). We therefore propose a distinction between democratic polarization-agonistic contestation among actors who accept multicultural pluralism, democratic institutions and election outcomes, civil and human rights, and epistemic accountability-and antidemocratic polarization, in which conflict is strategically mobilized to delegitimize opponents, erode institutional constraints, and normalize dehumanization, scapegoating, misinformation, antiscientific, and conspiratorial narratives as a route to political power. To clarify this distinction, we introduce a minimal framework specifying when political conflict remains within democratic bounds and when it begins to undermine them. This clarification is necessary because much of the polarization literature treats all forms of conflict as analytically comparable, regardless of their implications for democratic functioning.Under contemporary conditions of authoritarian resurgence, disinformation, and institutional erosion, the meaning of democratic conflict cannot be inferred from intensity, incivility, or ideological distance alone. Research treating polarization as presumptively pathological may misclassify democratic resistance as equivalent to anti-democratic escalation. Indeed, democratic politics is not reducible to civility, consensus, or the absence of conflict. In pluralistic societies, conflict is often unavoidable and sometimes necessary. Political disagreement commonly concerns collective priorities, the allocation of public resources, and the design of institutions that structure social life. But democratic disagreement does not extend without limit. It unfolds within a democratic framework that must secure equal standing, basic rights, and meaningful inclusion for all members of the polity. Polarization researchers must contextualize and interpret political conflict in light of the political reality, including stakeholders' commitments to pluralism, equal citizenship, collective welfare, equitable social and economic opportunities, and the maintenance of democratic institutions.This point is especially important because the boundary between "ordinary" policy disagreement and anti-democratic politics cannot be drawn by separating social and economic arrangements from questions of rights, recognition, and membership. Social and economic arrangements are not external to democratic inclusion: they help determine who has security, health, mobility, voice, and genuine access to collective life. When such arrangements systematically expose some groups to deprivation, precarity, premature death, or political marginalization while insulating dominant groups and concentrated economic power, they cease to be ordinary policy disagreements in any normatively innocent sense. They become mechanisms through which hierarchy is organized and reproduced. For this reason, conflicts over rights, recognition, and membership cannot be cleanly separated from conflicts over economic life, since the latter shape whether democratic citizenship is substantive or merely formal. When such inequalities are re-described as routine political disagreement, domination is recast as policy and anti-democratic projects can pass as normal democratic contestation.Accordingly, not all politically salient disagreements are democratically equivalent. Some conflicts occur within democratic bounds, even when they are intense and adversarial. Others target the very conditions that make democratic life possible. When political actors seek to deny full standing to opponents, restrict who counts as a legitimate member of the polity, erode rights protections, weaponize institutions, or degrade the epistemic conditions of public reasoning, the issue is no longer conflict within democracy alone. It is conflict over democracy's own normative and institutional basis. Under such conditions, domination can be redescribed as policy, exclusion can be redescribed as disagreement, and anti-democratic projects can pass as routine democratic contestation.To tentatively point out this distinction, Table 1 provides a heuristic framework for evaluating when political conflict remains consistent with democratic functioning and when it does not. It is not intended as a rigid classificatory scheme, nor does it aim to resolve ambiguities inherent to sociopolitical realities. Rather, it identifies a set of dimensions along which political conflict may be evaluated. The purpose of Table 1 is to make explicit why polarization cannot be evaluated solely in terms of distance, hostility, or civility, as it is often the case in the published literature. What should matter most, in our view, is the nature and kind of conflict being mobilized, by whom, under what institutional conditions, and toward what ends. Contestation over collective priorities, public resources, and institutional arrangements within a framework that protects equal standing, basic rights, and democratic inclusion.Mobilization directed at domination, exclusion, scapegoating, or the denial of full standing, often while being framed as ordinary policy disagreement.The key distinction is not between "material" and "symbolic" conflict, but between conflict within democratic bounds and conflict targeting democracy's normative basic tenets. Treating social and economic arrangements as if they were innocent or neutral background can obscure the existence of democratically corrosive conflict.Opponents are treated as legitimate adversaries within a shared political community, even amid deep disagreement.Opponents are cast as enemies, traitors, contaminants, or groups whose equal standing should be denied.In unequal systems, claims for equal inclusion are often re-described as divisive or extreme.Commitment to fair representation, meaningful participation, and peaceful transfer of power under substantively democratic conditions.Fraud claims without evidence, voter suppression, intimidation, rule manipulation, or other efforts to entrench minority rule.Procedural compliance alone is insufficient. Elections can be formally lawful yet substantively distorted by gerrymandering, disenfranchisement, or unequal access.Rejection of intimidation, vigilantism, and coercive force as tools of routine political competition.Endorsement, normalization, or strategic toleration of violence, threats, or paramilitary intimidation to secure political ends.Defensive resistance under conditions of authoritarian aggression should not be treated as equivalent to aggression in the service of domination.Disagreement proceeds with some commitment to evidence, accountability, policy evaluation, and truth-seeking institutions, even when values differ.Strategic disinformation, conspiracy narratives, epistemic nihilism, anti-scientific attitudes, or the systematic rejection of evidence when it constrains preferred outcomes.Democratic politics requires agreement and commitment to epistemic infrastructure. Accountable institutions for truth-seeking and policy assessment are necessary while not devolving into technocracy nor preaching technocratic infallibility. Preserves equal citizenship, equal protection, minority rights, opposition rights, and inclusive participation as non-negotiable conditions of democratic life.Treats the rights, recognition, safety, or participation of some groups as contingent, revocable, or subordinate to dominantgroup interests.Formal rights may coexist with substantive exclusion. The issue is whether institutions protect equal standing just formally or de facto. A democracy that protects formal liberties while permitting social and economic arrangements that systematically hollow out equal membership remains democratic only in a thin and unstable sense.Supports and materially sustains the institutions that enable accountability, including independent courts, watchdogs, civil society, electoral administration, and a genuinely free press.Defunds, captures, delegitimizes, or selectively weaponizes accountability institutions in order to avoid scrutiny or entrench power.The performative acceptance of democracy is too weak a standard. Democratic conflict must occur while all actors help sustain democratic accountability infrastructures.Clarifies genuine social conflicts, widens participation, increases accountability, and surfaces injustices that require political resolution.Accelerates democratic erosion, normalizes exclusion, immunizes power from scrutiny, and recasts hierarchy as ordinary disagreement.Whether conflict is agonistic depends partly on background power relations, not merely on tone or intensity. For one example, civil and political liberties cannot be exercised meaningfully in the absence of the social and economic conditions that make equal membership, voice, and participation materially possible.This framework indicates that polarization cannot be adequately evaluated solely in terms of attitudinal distance, hostility, or affect. By distinguishing between agonistic and corrosive forms of conflict, it helps account for why comparable levels of polarization may have different implications across contexts. This distinction, however, introduces challenges for classification and measurement. Simpler models may offer greater clarity, parsimony, and cross-context comparability, but these advantages often involve the omission of distinctions that are normatively and politically relevant. As a result, analytically distinct forms of conflict may be treated as equivalent. These limitations are not only methodological but also epistemic, as they relate to how analyses incorporate asymmetries of power, institutional conditions, and the processes through which political meanings and attitudes are formed. with these dimensions and yet their exclusion does not their it may to that are but conceptually in which forms of domination are as ordinary disagreement and anti-democratic is as equivalent to democratic central is not polarization but the of of polarization research to conflict as presumptively pathological while distinguishing democratically contestation from democratically corrosive under conditions of and epistemic it is not that efforts toward depolarization are inherently nor that all research on polarization treats polarization as undesirable. title: Geochemical Insights into Rare Earth Elements Distribution patterns in Granites of Nasarawa State, North-Central Nigeria
authors: Mohammed Salihu; Emmanuel Joseph; Adamu N. Baba-Kutigi
Tags: Geochemistry; Environmental Science; Resource Exploration
abstract: Purpose: To investigate the distribution of rare earth elements (REEs) in granites from selected locations within Nasarawa State, North-Central Nigeria, in order to evaluate their geochemical characteristics and assess their mineralization potential. Methodology: Fourteen (14) granite samples were systematically collected and analyzed using Instrumental Neutron Activation Analysis (INAA) at the Centre for Energy Research and Training, Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria, Nigeria. Trace element concentrations and REE patterns were examined, and geochemical discrimination diagrams were employed to determine tectonic settings and source characteristics. Findings: The trace element data show elevated concentrations of U (6.79 ppm), Co (64.81 ppm), Rb (266.89 ppm), Sb (2.04 ppm), Cs (43.83 ppm), and Ta (219.90 ppm) relative to Upper Continental Crust values, indicating moderate to extreme enrichment. REE patterns are dominated by light rare earth elements (LREE; average 58.44 ppm) over heavy rare earth elements (HREE; average 18.22 ppm). Geochemical discrimination diagrams indicate that the granites were emplaced in multiple tectonic settings, including syn-collisional, volcanic arc, and within-plate environments, and were primarily derived from the upper continental crust. Comparative analysis shows general depletion of most REEs, except for dysprosium (Dy), which exhibits elevated concentrations, suggesting potential Dy mineralization. Unique Contribution to Theory, Practice and Policy: This study provides baseline geochemical data for REE distribution in granites of Nasarawa State and contributes to the understanding of crustal evolution and tectonic settings in the region. It highlights the potential for dysprosium mineralization, offering practical insights for targeted REE exploration and resource development. The findings can inform mineral exploration strategies, guide investment decisions, and support policy formulation aimed at sustainable exploitation of REE resources in Nigeria.
Keywords: Rare Earth Elements; Geochemical Analysis; Granites; Nigeria; Mineralization Potential; Tectonic Settings
affiliations: Niger State Polytechnic; Federal University Dutsin-Ma

title: A Unified Compliance Aggregator Framework for Automated Multi-Tool Security Assessment of Linux Systems
Tags: Unified Compliance Aggregator; Security Tools; Automation; Assessment; Open-source
abstract: Assessing the security posture of modern computing systems typically requires the use of multiple specialized tools. These tools focus on different aspects such as configuration compliance, file integrity, and vulnerability exposure, and their outputs are often difficult to interpret collectively. This paper introduces the Unified Compliance Aggregator (UCA), a framework that integrates several open-source security tools into a single composite score representing overall system security. The proposed framework combines outputs from Lynis, OpenSCAP (STIG and CIS profiles), AIDE, Tripwire, and Nmap NSE. A normalization process converts heterogeneous outputs into a consistent 0 to 100 scale, followed by weighted aggregation. We also introduce a logarithmic scoring model for file integrity measurements to address limitations observed in prior linear approaches. Experiments were conducted on Ubuntu 22.04 across different hardening levels and environments. Results show consistent improvement in composite scores as systems are hardened, while also revealing contrasting behavior between compliance and file integrity tools. Two case studies, a basic web server and a DVWA-based system illustrate how the framework can be applied in practical scenarios.
Keywords: Security Assessment; Framework; Linux Systems; Compliance; Vulnerability

title: Ethical AI in Cybersecurity
venue: Advances in Computational Intelligence and Robotics
Tags: Artificial Intelligence; Cybersecurity Ethics; Compliance Evaluation; Evidence-based Governance
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence into cybersecurity systems introduces significant ethical challenges that require systematic and measurable evaluation. Although prior studies propose lifecycle-based ethical AI frameworks, practical methodologies for benchmarking ethical compliance remain limited. This study presents an evidence-driven analytical framework for evaluating ethical integration in AI-driven cybersecurity research. A corpus of peer-reviewed publications was analyzed using a structured pipeline that employs large language models (LLMs) to extract machine-readable JSON evidence, followed by automated scoring across seven ethical dimensions: transparency, explainability, accountability, human oversight, privacy, data protection, and continuous ethical monitoring. The results reveal substantial variability in ethical compliance, with notable deficiencies in human oversight and post-deployment monitoring. The proposed framework is replicable, auditable, and supports evidence-based ethical governance of AI-enabled cybersecurity platforms.
Keywords: Ethical AI; Cybersecurity; AI frameworks; Transparency; Accountability; Privacy

title: Disclosure of Cybersecurity Investments and the Cost of Capital
authors: Taha Havakhor; Mohammad S. Rahman; Tianjian Zhang
Tags: disclosure; trust; risk management; business governance
abstract: Executives often hesitate to disclose their company’s cybersecurity investments, fearing lawsuits or negative reactions from investors. Our research shows that transparency in this area actually pays off. Analyzing SEC filings from nearly 2,000 public firms, we find that companies that disclose cybersecurity investments enjoy a lower cost of capital—cheaper access to debt and equity financing. These benefits are strongest when disclosures are specific rather than boilerplate, when the firm is followed by more analysts and more institutional investors, and when disclosed investments in cybersecurity are substantial. The takeaway is clear: Meaningful disclosure builds trust with investors, who reward transparency by lowering financing costs. For leaders and regulators alike, this finding highlights that openness about cybersecurity readiness is not just good governance—It is smart business in a capital market that increasingly values risk management and resilience.
Keywords: Cybersecurity investments; Cost of capital; Transparency; SEC filings; Investor relations
affiliations: McGill University; Purdue University West Lafayette; California State University, Dominguez Hills

title: Awesome Cybersecurity
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: cybersecurity; aggregate; reference; practice
abstract: Initial release. The goal of this list is to: Aggregate trustworthy and relevant resources. Support learning, research, and practical implementation. Provide a centralized reference for practitioners, researchers, and developers. This release establishes the baseline version for citation, versioning, and long-term maintenance. Licensed under CC0 1.0 Universal.
Keywords: cybersecurity; resources; learning; research

title: CYBERIQ - An AI Based Cybersecurity Awareness System
venue: International Journal for Research in Applied Science and Engineering Technology
Tags: CyberIQ; adaptive training; human factors; digital security
abstract: The rapid expansion of digital infrastructure has intensified cybersecurity threats, making individuals and organizations increasingly vulnerable to phishing, malware, social engineering, and data breaches. Despite advances in technical defenses, human factors remain a critical vulnerability, as a large proportion of successful attacks exploit limited user awareness. This paper proposes CyberIQ, an Artificial Intelligence-based Cybersecurity Awareness System designed to detect threats, personalize user education, and dynamically adapt training content based on individual behavior and risk profiles. CyberIQ integrates Artificial Intelligence techniques such as Natural Language Processing (NLP) and rule-based reasoning to deliver real-time cybersecurity awareness, phishing detection guidance, and interactive learning modules.The system is modeled on an adaptive training framework that assesses user knowledge gaps and updates content intelligently. CyberIQ addresses the critical gap between technical cybersecurity tools and human-centered education by providing a scalable, intelligent, and proactive awareness platform.
Keywords: cybersecurity awareness; AI; phishing detection; user education; NLP

title: THE INFLUENCE OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE ON E-GOVERNANCE AND CYBERSECURITY
authors: Mr.U.Indu Sekhar; Injam Divya Teja; Yadavalli Gopi chand; Mekala Raja Sekhar Reddy; Tagirisa Vamsi Kumar
venue: American Journal of Management and IOT Medical Computing
Tags: Digital Infrastructure; Public Service Delivery; Fraud Detection; Real-Time Threat Detection
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing the way governments deliver services and manage digital infrastructure, significantly impacting e-governance and cybersecurity. In e-governance, AI enhances decisionmaking, automates administrative tasks, and improves public service delivery through intelligent data analysis, predictive modeling, and process optimization. AI-powered systems can process vast volumes of citizen data efficiently, enabling personalized services, fraud detection, and enhanced transparency. In cybersecurity, AI plays a crucial role in detecting, preventing, and responding to cyber threats in real-time by analyzing network traffic patterns, identifying anomalies, and predicting potential attacks. Machine learning algorithms, deep learning models, and intelligent automation allow organizations to strengthen defense mechanisms against increasingly sophisticated cyber-attacks. This paper explores the dual role of AI in promoting effective egovernance while bolstering cybersecurity measures, highlighting its potential to optimize operational efficiency, improve policy implementation, and ensure secure digital infrastructure. The study emphasizes the need for robust ethical frameworks, regulatory policies, and technical safeguards to maximize AI’s benefits while minimizing risks associated with privacy, bias, and system vulnerabilities.
Keywords: Artificial Intelligence; E-Governance; Cybersecurity; Machine Learning; Predictive Modeling

title: CYBERSECURITY TRUST AND RISK MANAGEMENT FOR DIGITAL START UPS
authors: Dr. Mrs. Chidiebere Chukwuma Oparah; Ogwuche Gabriel Shaibu; Dr. Echetama Forstina C.
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; News Media; Corporate Records
Tags: Cyber Threats; Operational Resilience; Stakeholder Trust
abstract: Abstract: In the contemporary digital economy, start-ups face increasing exposure to cyber threats that can compromise business operations, stakeholder trust, and competitive advantage. Digital start-ups, particularly in emerging economies, often struggle to implement robust cybersecurity and risk management practices due to limited resources, technical expertise, and formal governance structures. This study investigates the role of cybersecurity trust and risk management in enhancing the performance, operational resilience, and stakeholder confidence of digital start-ups. A cross-sectional survey research design was adopted, and data were collected from 238 founders, IT personnel, and employees of digital start-ups in Owerri Municipal, Owerri North, and Owerri West in Imo State, Nigeria, using a structured questionnaire titled Cybersecurity Trust and Risk Management Questionnaire (CTRMQ). Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics and Spearman Rank Order Correlation Coefficient. Findings reveal that implementing strong cybersecurity trust practices significantly promotes the adoption of security measures, while structured risk management initiatives effectively enhance the mitigation of cyber threats. The study concludes that integrating cybersecurity trust with proactive risk management equips digital start-ups to safeguard digital assets, strengthen stakeholder confidence, and achieve sustainable growth. It is recommended that start-ups adopt robust security controls, transparent data handling practices, continuous risk assessment, and incident response planning to build resilience and maintain competitive advantage in the digital marketplace.
Keywords: Cybersecurity; Start-ups; Risk Management; Trust; Digital Economy
affiliations: Federal Polytechnic Nekede; Federal University of Technology Owerri; Imo State University; Federal College of Education, Kano

title: Framework for Cybersecurity ROI and business case development in service agencies
Tags: Cybersecurity; ROI; Service Agencies; Risk Management; Strategic Governance
abstract: Cybersecurity is emerging as a tactical priority among service agencies that are operating in more digital and interconnected setting. Although cyber threats and regulatory pressures are on the increase, most organizations find it difficult to defend cybersecurity investments on structured financial models. This integrative review with conceptual framework development review is an empirical, regulatory, sector specific, and governance-based research synthesis which will result in an integrated framework of cybersecurity return on investment (ROI) and business case development specific to service agencies. Drawing on literature across healthcare, financial services, capital markets, and enterprise risk management, this study identifies four core dimensions of cybersecurity investment: economic impact, regulatory compliance, strategic governance alignment, and behavioral decision-making. The findings demonstrate that structured risk reduction, preservation of stakeholder trust, mitigation of regulatory exposure, and strengthening organizational resilience are the primary factors influencing cybersecurity return on investment (ROI) in service-oriented organizations. The suggested framework offers a multi-dimensional method of cyber threat translation into defensible financial and strategic rationale, repositioning cybersecurity from a technical cost center to a resilience-focused capability necessary for providing services in a sustainable manner. Keywords: Cybersecurity ROI, Cyber Risk Management, Business Case Development, Enterprise Risk Management, Risk Quantification, Preventive Investment.
Keywords: Cybersecurity ROI; Cyber Risk Management; Business Case Development; Enterprise Risk Management; Risk Quantification; Preventive Investment

title: A prototype for updating the “things” in IoT: framework for cybersecurity implementation
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Corporate Records; Academic Literature
Tags: IoT; Cybersecurity; Framework; Vulnerability; Device Tracking
abstract: The fast-growing Internet of Things (IoT) devices create major cybersecurity problems because standard security measures remain behind this rapidly spreading connected device network. The proposed research introduces a framework to secure and manage IoT device updates inside IoT systems. The system proposal achieves enhanced cybersecurity through its refined infrastructure for monitoring as well as firmware updates and device management. The project targets three security goals which consist of protecting IoT device communication channels and maintaining current firmware and software systems and defending against unauthorized intrusions. A central control system running in the prototype provides active device observation along with continuous device tracking capabilities. The system implements access policies together with automatic response procedures that both reduce security threats and perform crucial vulnerability remediation tasks. Through encryption protocols combined with authentication mechanisms the framework improves the security position of IoT networks. Archived data about device operating systems through the system enables proper forensic examination and security breach investigation. A thoughtful assessment of the implementation proves the security solution’s ability to prevent unauthorized entry points and keep systems safe. The presented framework adds important knowledge to IoT security studies by establishing an operational approach to safeguarding various IoT networks efficiently. Test results from this prototype become the basis to advance secure IoT design principles which maintain effective protection of IoT systems.
Keywords: IoT Security; Firmware Updates; Device Management; Cybersecurity Framework; Unauthorized Access

title: A Systematic Literature Review of Cybersecurity Risks in Autonomous and Digitalised Maritime Systems
authors: Achema Iyamadi Timothy; A. A. Obiniyi; J. B. Agbogun
venue: African Journal of Management and Business Research
Tags: digitalisation; threat vectors; AI-driven approaches; regulatory challenges
abstract: The maritime industry is experiencing rapid digitalisation driven by the adoption of autonomous vessels, cyber–physical systems, artificial intelligence, Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, and extensive ship–shore connectivity. While these developments enhance operational efficiency, safety, and sustainability, they have simultaneously expanded the cyber-attack surface of maritime systems. This study synthesises contemporary research on cybersecurity risks in autonomous and digitalised maritime environments. Following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 guidelines, 52 peer-reviewed studies published between 2018 and 2025 were analysed from Scopus, Web of Science, IEEE Xplore, ScienceDirect, and ResearchGate. The review categorises the literature into three dominant themes: (i) cybersecurity risk assessment and management frameworks, (ii) vulnerabilities and threat vectors in maritime cyber–physical systems, and (iii) human, regulatory, and validation challenges. Findings reveal increasing reliance on hybrid risk assessment models integrating safety analysis techniques with cybersecurity taxonomies such as Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge (MITRE ATT&CK), alongside emerging AI-driven approaches. However, persistent gaps remain in real-world validation, regulatory harmonisation, and human-factor integration. The study concludes that resilient autonomous maritime operations depend on empirically grounded, human-centred, and standardised cybersecurity frameworks. It further recommends future research focusing on real-world validation, regulatory harmonisation, and maritime-specific cybersecurity training.
Keywords: cybersecurity; autonomous vessels; cyber-physical systems; maritime cybersecurity; risk assessment

title: Operationalising Teaching Presence at Scale: A Design Model for Foundational Cybersecurity Education
Tags: foundational education; online courses; scalable teaching; cybersecurity; educational model
abstract: Online cybersecurity education increasingly serves diverse cohorts, including students with non-technical backgrounds and those balancing their studies with work or family responsibilities. Yet, research on sustaining educational quality while scaling fully online enrolments remains limited, particularly in foundational technical subjects where learning requires both conceptual understanding and professional judgement. This study aims to examine how teaching presence can be operationalised in fully online foundational cybersecurity subjects through inspectable artefacts and routines that remain workable for large cohorts and distributed teaching teams. This paper reports a Scholarship of Teaching and Learning (SoTL) design and transfer case grounded in the Community of Inquiry (CoI) framework. This study examines the redesign of CSE1ICB (Introduction to Cybersecurity) and the transfer of the same design logic to CSE1CPR (Cybersecurity in Practice). The findings identify a coherent four-component design model comprising (1) real-world incident integration, (2) scenario-based learning and interactive checks, (3) structured, layered support, and (4) a predictable communication rhythm across the learning management system (LMS) and email. Across these two subjects, these elements are presented as an integrated system intended to make learning objectives salient, increase opportunities for guided practice in professional reasoning, reduce avoidable friction in practical work, and create consistent instructor visibility through routine communication and support structures. This paper synthesises the approach into nine transferable design principles, mapped to CoI teaching presence dimensions and illustrated through concrete design choices, including incident-framing templates, scenario prompt patterns, layered support resources, formative feedback patterns, and communication routines. Overall, this study shows that teaching presence can be operationalised as a coordinated design system rather than as a set of isolated tactics. This paper contributes a reusable and theory-informed model for educators coordinating foundational cybersecurity subjects delivered online at scale.
Keywords: cybersecurity education; teaching presence; online learning; Community of Inquiry; design principles

title: Determinants of the acceptance of cybersecurity chatbots: an extended protection motivation perspective
authors: Larysa Luzinska; Stefanie Sohn; Oliver Schnittka; Peter Mayer
Tags: cybersecurity; chatbots; user acceptance; trust; threat assessment
abstract: Purpose The purpose of this paper is to investigate the determinants of individuals’ acceptance of cybersecurity chatbots (CSCs). Integrating theories of protection motivation and institutional trust, this study examines the effects of threat appraisals, coping appraisals and trust in the CSC provider on the intention to use CSCs, thereby extending chatbot acceptance research into the cybersecurity domain. Design/methodology/approach The authors empirically test the research model using data from two field studies conducted in Europe and the United States. Data were analyzed using partial least squares structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM). Study 1 tests the role of threat and coping appraisals, while Study 2 replicates the model and incorporates trust in the CSC provider. Findings The findings show that CSC acceptance is shaped by threat appraisals (e.g. perceived susceptibility) and coping appraisals (e.g. self-efficacy), as well as by trust in the CSC provider. Originality/value By conceptualizing CSCs as awareness-supporting tools in voluntary, citizen-level contexts, the study advances cybersecurity awareness research beyond organizational settings and enriches Protection Motivation Theory by introducing institutional trust as a provider-focused appraisal influencing acceptance. This study also extends chatbot acceptance research by demonstrating that CSC acceptance is explained by protection motivation processes beyond traditional technology acceptance factors. It integrates institutional trust as a provider-related determinant, showing that trust in the CSC provider shapes coping appraisals and, in turn, CSC acceptance.
Keywords: cybersecurity chatbots; protection motivation theory; institutional trust; chatbot acceptance; threat appraisal; coping appraisal

title: Layered Control Architectures for AI Safety: A Cybersecurity-Oriented Systems Framework
authors: Young B. Choi; Paul C. Hong; Young Soo Park
Tags: AI; cybersecurity; safety; framework; risk management
abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI) systems become increasingly autonomous, scalable, and embedded in critical digital infrastructure, AI safety has emerged as a significant consideration for cybersecurity, system reliability, and institutional trust. Advances in large language models and agentic systems expand the threat surface to include misalignment, large-scale misuse, opaque decision-making, and cross-border risk propagation, while existing debates remain fragmented across technical, ethical, and geopolitical domains. This paper conducts a structured comparative analysis of AI safety perspectives from ten influential thinkers, examining them across five dimensions and reframing their insights through a cybersecurity lens spanning national governance, industry standards, and firm-level design. Building on this synthesis, the study proposes a layered control architecture that organizes technical safeguards, governance mechanisms, and human oversight into a defense-in-depth structure. The framework is conceptual and theory-building, intended to clarify system-level security reasoning and support future empirical refinement across diverse institutional contexts.
Keywords: AI safety; cybersecurity; systems framework; layered control architecture; governance mechanisms
affiliations: Regent University; University of Toledo; Midwest University

title: The New Reality of Knowledge Work: Impact of Flexible Work Settings on Organizational Cybersecurity
authors: Dennik Baltuttis; Christoph M. Abels; Timm Teubner
Tags: cybersecurity; knowledge work; flexible settings; adaptability; employee productivity
abstract: The rise of flexible work arrangements increases knowledge workers’ autonomy and organizations’ access to talent but exacerbates the cybersecurity threat landscape for organizations. A key tension exists between enabling work flexibility and maintaining robust cybersecurity. Despite the growing prevalence of flexible work, cybersecurity research has largely overlooked its multifaceted nature, particularly beyond the dimension of location. To explore the impact of flexible work arrangements on organizational cybersecurity, we conducted 21 expert interviews with (predominantly young) professionals and academics. Our findings highlight three key dimensions of flexible work — location, time, and device — each presenting unique cybersecurity vulnerabilities. We identify critical tensions between cybersecurity policies and work flexibility, indicating that overly rigid cybersecurity measures can lead to unintended consequences such as employee resistance and non-compliance. We propose an integrative cybersecurity framework that considers technological, human, and organizational factors in flexible work environments, facilitating the optimization of both organizational resilience and employee productivity. Our research highlights the need for adaptive cybersecurity strategies that address the realities of flexible work, enhancing both security and overall value creation. It provides a more holistic understanding of cybersecurity in modern work environments and offers practical guidance for managers.
Keywords: flexible work; cybersecurity; organizational resilience; remote work; employee compliance
affiliations: Technische Universität Berlin; University of Potsdam

title: Cybersecurity in Smart Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) Manufacturing
authors: Ahmad Zaland; Syed Saad; Syed Ammad; Kumeel Rasheed
venue: Artificial General Intelligence and Circular Manufacturing in Industry 5.0
Tags: Artificial Intelligence; cyber risks; manufacturing; security challenges
abstract: Abstract The use of advanced Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) in manufacturing is creating a new wave of smart factories. These systems can learn, adapt and make decisions on their own, which leads to major gains in efficiency and innovation. However, this great power also brings serious new cybersecurity risks. This chapter discusses the unique security challenges of AGI manufacturing. It looks at the dangers of hackers tricking or poisoning the Artificial Intelligence (AI)’s decision-making, which could lead to physical damage, production stops or stolen designs. It also discusses weaknesses from using old equipment, insecure Internet-connected devices and human error. To fight these threats, the chapter recommends strong defence plans. These include building security into the AI from the start, using a ‘never trust, always verify’ approach (Zero-Trust) and securing all network communications. The chapter also covers the need for emergency response plans and the ethical rules that must guide this powerful technology. By understanding and managing these risks, the manufacturing industry can safely unlock the benefits of AGI while protecting its people, property and products.
Keywords: AGI; cybersecurity; smart manufacturing; Zero-Trust; network security
affiliations: CECOS University; Flanders Make (Belgium); Griffith University; Shenzhen University

title: Risk Management in FinTech: Evaluating Cybersecurity Strategies for Financial Institutions
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Government Data; News Media
Tags: Cyber Threats; Financial Institutions; Proactive Measures; Ransomware; Phishing
abstract: Abstract The current paper examines the importance of cybersecurity risk management to FinTech, the issues in the field that require particular attention to financial institutions when it comes to protecting digital assets and confidential customer information. It appraises existing plans, such as technology, regulatory controls, and human aspects, to address the dynamic cyber threats like ransomware, phishing, and insider attacks. Emphasizing the need for integrated and adaptive risk management frameworks, the study highlights how proactive measures and continuous innovation are essential to maintaining trust and resilience in the rapidly digitizing financial ecosystem.
Keywords: Cybersecurity; FinTech; Risk Management; Digital Assets; Customer Information

title: FUTURE SMART TECHNOLOGIES: AI, IOT, CYBERSECURITY, AND DATA ANALYTICS CONVERGENCE
authors: Dr. P. Menaka Dr J Savitha
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: Interconnected Systems; Digital Innovation; Data Privacy; Ethical Considerations; Smart Solutions
abstract: The rapid evolution of digital innovation is transforming the way individuals, organizations, and societies function in the modern world. The convergence of advanced technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), the Internet of Things (IoT), Cybersecurity, and Data Analytics is reshaping industries, redefining business models, and creating new opportunities for sustainable growth and development. This book, Future Smart Technologies: AI, IoT, Cybersecurity, and Data Analytics Convergence, aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of these interconnected domains and their collective impact on the future. In today’s interconnected ecosystem, intelligent systems powered by AI are enhancing decision-making capabilities, while IoT devices are enabling seamless communication between the physical and digital worlds. At the same time, the exponential growth of data has made data analytics an essential tool for extracting meaningful insights and driving strategic actions. However, with increased connectivity and data exchange comes the critical need for robust cybersecurity frameworks to protect sensitive information and ensure trust in digital systems. This book explores the synergy between these technologies, highlighting how their integration leads to smarter solutions in areas such as healthcare, finance, manufacturing, education, and smart cities. It emphasizes not only technological advancements but also the challenges associated with privacy, security, ethical considerations, and implementation complexities. Designed for students, researchers, academicians, and industry professionals, this work provides both theoretical foundations and practical perspectives. Each chapter delves into key concepts, emerging trends, real-world applications, and future directions, offering readers a holistic view of the digital transformation landscape. As we move toward a smarter and more connected future, understanding the convergence of AI, IoT, cybersecurity, and data analytics becomes essential. It is our hope that this book will serve as a valuable resource, inspiring readers to explore, innovate, and contribute to the development of secure, intelligent, and sustainable technological ecosystems.
Keywords: AI; IoT; Cybersecurity; Data Analytics; Digital Transformation; Smart Technologies; Sustainable Growth

title: Cybersecurity Awareness Dataset on HTTPS Usage among Moroccan Web Users and Webmasters
authors: A. Zineddine; Abdeslam Rehaimi; Yousra Belfaik; Yassine Sadqi; Said Safi
Tags: cybersecurity; dataset; webmaster; behavioral analysis
abstract: The dataset contains responses from 440 Moroccan web users and webmasters who completed a structured survey on cybersecurity awareness and HTTPS-related behaviors. The data were collected using Google Forms and distributed widely through active web development and cybersecurity communities on platforms such as Facebook, WhatsApp, and LinkedIn. The dataset includes raw responses, processed data, encoded datasets, clustering outputs, and interpretation reports generated using machine learning techniques and large language models (LLMs). These reports offer detailed assessments and actionable cybersecurity recommendations. This dataset supports the study of real-world HTTPS deployment practices and secure browsing behaviors across different user roles.
Keywords: HTTPS; cybersecurity awareness; Moroccan web users; survey dataset; machine learning; secure browsing
affiliations: Université Sultan Moulay Slimane; Université Ibn Zohr; Cadi Ayyad University

title: Designing Compliance-Driven Cybersecurity Governance Models for Hipaa-Regulated Healthcare Systems
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: cybersecurity; healthcare; governance; risk management; compliance
abstract: Healthcare organizations continue to experience escalating ransomware attacks, third-party breaches, and operational disruptions despite widespread compliance with the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA). This pattern suggests that regulatory adherence alone does not ensure effective cybersecurity risk reduction. This study argues that the persistent gap between formal HIPAA compliance and real-world security outcomes is fundamentally a governance failure rather than a regulatory deficiency. Drawing on breach trend data from the HHS Office for Civil Rights, the Verizon Data Breach Investigations Report, and ransomware impact studies published in JAMA Health Forum, this paper demonstrates that weak executive oversight, diffuse risk ownership, inadequate third-party governance, and slow escalation processes contribute materially to breach severity and operational disruption. In response, the paper proposes a compliance-driven cybersecurity governance model that operationalizes HIPAA safeguards through structured accountability, defined decision authority, continuous oversight, and measurable governance performance indicators. The model integrates board-level risk oversight, executive risk ownership, compliance–security alignment, and operational enforcement into a unified governance system designed for vendor-dependent, clinically sensitive healthcare environments. An evaluation framework is introduced to assess governance effectiveness using behavioral metrics such as risk ownership completeness, escalation timeliness, vendor monitoring coverage, and incident containment performance rather than audit artifact completion. By reframing HIPAA compliance as an enforceable governance system rather than a documentation exercise, this study contributes a structured model for strengthening healthcare cybersecurity resilience and establishes a foundation for future empirical validation of governance-driven risk reduction.
Keywords: HIPAA; cybersecurity governance; healthcare security; ransomware attacks; risk ownership

title: Phishing URL Classification in Cybersecurity Education: A Comparative Study of RF, SVM, DT, and LR
Tags: Phishing Detection; ML Classifiers; Cybersecurity; Education
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) into cybersecurity practice has increased the need for hands-on, data-driven learning in cybersecurity education. This paper presents a classroom-oriented phishing URL classification study using the UCI PhiUSIIL Phishing URL Dataset (2024), a public benchmark with 235,795 instances and 54 features suitable for supervised learning tasks. Four widely used ML classifiers—Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree (DT), and Logistic Regression (LR)—were evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score. The results show that RF achieved the best overall performance with 93.33% accuracy and 93.75% F1-score, while SVM produced the highest precision (93.85%). DT showed strong specificity but lower recall, and LR yielded the weakest overall performance. The study is designed to support cybersecurity education by using interpretable baseline models, standard evaluation metrics, and a reproducible public dataset. It provides a practical workflow for lab activities and course projects while contributing an empirical comparison that complements existing pedagogical frameworks in AI-enabled cybersecurity education.
Keywords: Phishing; URL Classification; Machine Learning; Cybersecurity Education; AI
affiliations: Public Authority for Applied Education and Training; Cardiff University

title: EXPLORING THE SOCIAL DIMENSIONS OF CYBERSECURITY IN SAUDI SOCIETY: TOWARDS A METHODOLOGICAL FRAMEWORK FOR UNDERSTANDING AND ANALYSIS
authors: Hany Mohamed Bahaa El-Din Ali Hegazy, Jaber Hadi F Aldosari, Jasmine Nagy El-Saeed Shabar
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; Government Data; News Media
Tags: social construct; cyber culture; digital practices; trust and privacy; governance
abstract: This study explores the social representations of cybersecurity as a multidimensional social construct in Saudi society and validates a scale for analyzing its cultural, cognitive, behavioral, and institutional dimensions. Most of the literature focuses on technical aspects rather than sociological views. The study was a descriptive cross-sectional design with 755 subjects. Social dimensions were assessed using a scale developed by the researcher. To assess content validity, exploration and confirmatory factor analyses were conducted, and Cronbach’s alpha, split-half reliability, and internal consistency were calculated. The results showed that the five-factor model extracted is: cyber culture and society; cyber awareness and perception; trust and privacy in the digital environment; governance and social support; and digital practices. The results showed that cybersecurity is a socially embedded phenomenon, shaped by values, risk awareness, enacting practices, and governance arrangements. The scale has validity and reliability for research in Arab contexts. The research contributes to the theoretical by investigating social representations in cybersecurity and, methodologically, offering a multidimensional instrument for measurement.
Keywords: cybersecurity; social dimensions; Saudi society; methodological framework; cultural analysis

title: From Events to Conditions: A Systemic and Predictive Framework for Pre-Attack Detection in Cybersecurity
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: anticipatory cybersecurity; system pressure; dynamic behavior; anomaly detection; reactive vs proactive
abstract: This work introduces a condition-based framework for cybersecurity that shifts the focus from event detection to the analysis of system states. Traditional security models rely on the detection and correlation of events, assuming that threats can be identified through anomalous activities and known attack patterns. However, this approach remains inherently reactive and fails to capture the dynamic conditions that enable attacks to occur. The proposed framework is based on the Adaptive Predictive System (APS), which introduces the concept of system pressure as a synthetic indicator of cumulative and evolving system stress. Instead of analyzing isolated events, APS evaluates the dynamic behavior of the system over time, enabling the identification of transient vulnerability states. The study is based on data generated within a simulated enterprise environment and demonstrates that pre-attack conditions are systemic, distributed across multiple assets, and not localized anomalies. The results show that attacks emerge probabilistically rather than deterministically, with pre-attack conditions preceding actual attacks in 61.7% of cases, while 38.3% represent reversible instability phases. Furthermore, measurable lead times—ranging from approximately 2.5 to over 14 minutes—were observed between pre-attack detection and attack manifestation. Vulnerability windows are shown to be transient and dynamically generated, while system pressure increases progressively prior to attack emergence, even during normal operational activity. These findings demonstrate that cyber attacks are not isolated events, but the result of temporary system configurations in which instability conditions converge. The proposed approach challenges the traditional Data–Information–Knowledge–Wisdom (DIKW) paradigm, showing that meaningful insight does not arise from data accumulation alone, but from the analysis of system dynamics over time. This work supports a transition from reactive to anticipatory cybersecurity, where the objective is not only to detect threats, but to identify and govern the conditions that make them possible.
Keywords: pre-attack detection; cybersecurity framework; system dynamics; vulnerability states; predictive model

title: DETECTION OF REAL TIME MALICIOUS INTRUSIONS&ATTACKS IN IOT EMPOWERED CYBERSECURITY
authors: Dr .D.Kalyankumar; Tallapaneni Narasimha Naidu; Beeram Amala; Dorre Gopi; Pamulapati Gangadhar
venue: American Journal of Management and IOT Medical Computing
Tags: IoT; cybersecurity; machine learning; intrusion detection system
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has significantly increased connectivity and automation in smart environments. However, this growth has also escalated cybersecurity risks, making IoT networks vulnerable to malicious intrusions, malware attacks, and unauthorized access. Detecting these threats in realtime is crucial to safeguard sensitive data and maintain system reliability. This study proposes a real-time intrusion detection system (IDS) leveraging machine learning and deep learning algorithms to identify anomalous behavior in IoT networks efficiently. The system captures network traffic, analyzes patterns, and predicts potential threats with high accuracy. Experimental results show that the proposed framework improves detection rates while reducing false alarms compared to traditional methods.
Keywords: IoT security; intrusion detection; machine learning; real-time detection; cybersecurity

title: Building Gen Z's Cybersecurity Resilience on Social Media: A Survey Dataset from Indonesia
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: Cybersecurity; Youth Behavior; Digital Ecosystem; Data Analysis
abstract: Summary: This dataset was collected to investigate the factors influencing the cybersecurity resilience of social media users within Generation Z. The study was conducted specifically in Indonesia, exploring the unique digital ecosystem and user behaviors of Indonesian youth. It examines the relationships between technical knowledge, legal frameworks, social culture, platform policies, and technological standards toward cybersecurity practices on social media. Methodology: Data was obtained through an online survey targeting the Gen Z demographic across various regions in Indonesia. The primary research variables were measured using a 1-5 Likert Scale, defined as follows: 1: Strongly Disagree / Never 2: Disagree / Rarely 3: Neutral / Sometimes 4: Agree / Frequently 5: Strongly Agree / Always Data Structure: The dataset is organized into two main sections: Demographic Information & User Profile: Gender Occupation / Profession Latest Education / Highest Education Level Number of Social Media Platforms Used Most Frequently Used Social Media Platform Daily Social Media Usage Duration Primary Purpose of Using Social Media Monthly Income / Monthly Allowance (in Indonesian Rupiah - IDR) Research Variables (1-5 Likert Scale): X1 (MPK) - Building Knowledge and Capabilities: Technical training, webinars, and discussions. X2 (KHP) - Legal Frameworks and Regulations: Perceptions of Indonesian cyber laws and regulations. Y1 (BMS) - Social Media Culture and Society: Awareness of local and global digital threats. Y2 (KSM) - Social Media Policies and Strategies: Platform-specific security governance. Y3 (ST) - Standards and Technology: Technical verification and detection features. Y4 (PKS) - Security Practices in Social Media: Practical implementation of security measures.
Keywords: Gen Z; Cybersecurity Resilience; Social Media; Indonesia; Survey Dataset

title: Impact of Cybersecurity Awareness on user Behaviour in Digital Banking: A Study on Risk Perception, Preparedness and Financial Implications
venue: International Journal of Creative and Open Research in Engineering and Management
Tags: Cybersecurity; Digital Security; User Education; Banking Security
abstract: The rapid growth of digital banking provides consumers with a high level of access and convenience to their banking services. Unfortunately, it has made them more vulnerable to various types of computer crime such as phishing, OTP fraud and data breaches, which create a real threat to their financial safety and security. This research aims to demonstrate the ways in which awareness about cybersecurity impacts the behaviour of customers who use digital banking. In particular, it examines the relationship between awareness (of cybersecurity), perceived risk, preparedness, the use of protective measures (i.e. protects against potential cybercrimes), and financial impact. A descriptive, analytical (quantitative) research design was chosen to conduct this study. Primary data collection involved 150 participants (users of digital banking) who completed an online survey. According to SPSS, three types of statistical analysis were performed on the data: Independent Samples t-tests, One-way ANOVAs, and Simple and Multiple Regression. The outcomes show there is no significant difference in awareness of cybersecurity based on gender; however, significant differences were found by age and educational level Additionally, cybersecurity awareness was found to be significantly and positively associated with perceived risk and the uptake of protective strategies. The results also showed that while both preparedness (by itself) and awareness affect financial outcomes, the effects of preparedness are substantially greater than the effects of awareness alone (in isolation). The significance of blending awareness training and practical instruction on preparation for using banking technologies is demonstrated by these results; if individuals were better prepared for the possibility of computer crime, they would be less likely to experience financial loss.
Keywords: Cybersecurity Awareness; Digital Banking; User Behaviour; Risk Perception; Financial Implications

title: Cybersecurity Threats and Mitigation Strategies in Healthcare During the Covid-19 Pandemic: A Scoping Review
authors: Itunu Omolade Dave- Agboola; Richard Kayode Alhassan
venue: International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Academic Literature; News Media
Tags: Cyber Threats; Digital Transformation; Pandemic Preparedness; Patient Data Protection
abstract: Introduction The COVID-19 pandemic unexpectedly accelerated the digital transformation of healthcare systems, exposing them to unprecedented cybersecurity risks. During the COVID-19 pandemic, the healthcare system was affected by ransomware, phishing, and other cyber threats which disrupted the delivery of healthcare services, compromised patient data, and challenged public health emergency response. Although the cyberthreats and cyberattacks were documented, there is a limited synthesis of lessons learned for pandemic preparedness.  Methods Following the Arksey and O’Malley framework and PRISMA-ScR guidelines, we conducted a systematic search of peer-reviewed and grey literature published between January 2020 and December 2025 across PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, IEEE Xplore, and Google Scholar. Eligible studies reported on cyber threats, mitigation measures, and impacts on healthcare or public health response during COVID-19. Data were extracted using a standardized template and analyzed descriptively and thematically, categorizing threats, mitigation strategies, and lessons learned.  Results Twenty-six studies were included, comprising scoping/systematic reviews, empirical analyses, case studies, surveys, and policy reports. The most prevalent threats were ransomware and phishing, followed by malware, device/IoMT vulnerabilities, and data breaches. Impacts included service disruption, diagnostic delays, patient safety risks, and widespread data exposure. Mitigation strategies spanned technical (e.g., multi-factor authentication, segmentation, backups), organizational (e.g., incident response planning, workforce training), and policy/governance measures (e.g., reporting systems, cross-sector coordination). Key lessons highlighted the importance of layered socio-technical defenses, workforce preparedness, tested recovery plans, and integrated cyber governance. Major gaps were noted in the evaluation of mitigation effectiveness, and research on cyberbiosecurity and infodemic-related threats.  Conclusions Health systems must adopt resilient, evidence-informed, socio-technical strategies to mitigate cyber threats, maintain continuity of care, and protect patient data. Policymakers should integrate cybersecurity into emergency planning, strengthen reporting and governance frameworks, and support research on intervention effectiveness and emerging threats.
Keywords: Cybersecurity; Healthcare; COVID-19; Ransomware; Phishing; Mitigation Strategies

title: Challenges of 5G Technology: An Analysis of Cybersecurity, Digital Sovereignty, and Geopolitical Conflicts in the Case of Türkiye
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; News Media; Government Data; Academic Literature
Tags: Cybersecurity; 5G Technology; Digital Transformation; Geopolitics; Türkiye
abstract: 5G is widely regarded as the backbone of digital transformation due to its high speed, low latency, and massive connectivity. However, its software-defined architecture, virtualization, and network slicing introduce new cybersecurity challenges. In Türkiye, the transition to 5G, supported by domestic production requirements and new cybersecurity regulations, extends beyond a technical upgrade toward issues of digital sovereignty. This study identifies key literature clusters and highlights a critical gap at their intersection, proposing an analytical framework to assess 5G-related security, governance, and geopolitical implications in the Turkish context.
