Regardless of method, high-quality case study research conveys value through vivid stories while forming a closed chain of evidence, building theory through iteration and comparison with existing literature.The subsequent phase focuses on the formal drafting and composition of the research manuscript, a step that directly reflects the researcher's scientific rigor and academic writing proficiency. Data findings and theoretical frameworks should be presented clearly through well-designed figures and tables, with logical conceptual models strongly recommended to visualize and clarify relationships between key theoretical constructs. When articulating core research propositions, researchers must conduct sufficient dialogue with existing literature to highlight theoretical contributions.The final phase of the process involves research verification and refinement. In this stage, researchers seek targeted feedback and solicit revision suggestions from key case informants and strengthen the discussion section to emphasize practical value. It is worth noting that alternative stage classifications exist within scholarly literature; for instance, some scholars have condensed the case study process into three overarching phases, including preparation, implementation, and dialogue (Chen and Liu, 2010). Researchers should develop comprehensive proposals including title, background, questions, theoretical foundation, design, methods, and references, particularly justifying case selection.To further elaborate the content of case study research, this study selects illustrative cases for concise analysis based on the combination of typicality and appropriateness. Anderski et al. (2024) analyzed athlete brands' role in international marketing of professional sports leagues, using the German Bundesliga's Asian market practices as a single case study. The research addressed two questions: athlete brands' current role and potential in international marketing, and their co-creation pathways toward league brand meaning. For case selection, the Bundesliga's uniqueness in terms of fan majority ownership, emphasis on local youth development, and high stadium attendance distinguishes its brand ecosystem, making it ideal for exploring athlete brands' role in marketing. The league's active expansion into the Asian market adds contextual relevance.The study employed semi-structured interviews with 17 stakeholders including Bundesliga executives, club managers, and broadcast media executives in Asian markets. Inductive thematic analysis across six phases integrated 40 codes through open coding. Following triangulation, theme refinement, and peer review, four themes emerged: athlete brands' international marketing role, league brand meaning co-creation pathways, brand co-creation prerequisites, and influence mechanisms within the brand ecosystem. Theoretically, it supplements understanding of athlete brands within sports brand ecosystems and refines co-creation pathways in cross-cultural contexts; methodologically, it demonstrates standardized application of interviews and thematic analysis; practically, it highlights risks of athlete brands in international marketing.Complementing this, our research team developed a case study protocol on the ATP Shanghai Masters 1000's localized development strategy in China. This longitudinal single case enables tracing the event's evolutionary trajectory over time. Following Yin's (2016) framework, we defined core parameters including research tasks, target groups, questions, and interviewee information. Supporting materials included checklist-based data collection, literature synthesis, and jointly refined interview questions, laying a foundation for subsequent data collection and analysis.As the lead article of this special issue on case-based research in sport marketing, this paper offers a foundational overview of case study methodology for diverse readers. Drawing on scholarly works and representative studies, it presents core definitions and classifications, reveals theoretical value and applicable characteristics, and provides intuitive explanations through typical examples.Admittedly, this paper lacks the detail required for a comprehensive step-by-step guide to rigorous case study research. Nevertheless, we hope this special issue stimulates innovative engagement with case study research in sport marketing and encourages more researchers to adopt this approach.We strongly encourage developing high-quality teaching cases tailored to sport marketing. The Harvard Case Library features rigorous sport-related cases, including Paris Saint-Germain: Building One of the World's Top Sports Brands, FC Barcelona: More Than a Club, More Than Data, Decathlon: Making Sports Accessible to All, Fantasy Sports: A Game of Skill or Chance, John Hancock Sports Sponsorship: 1993–2000 and Beyond, and Negotiating with Data: Analytics FC. These benchmark cases would greatly benefit sport marketing education and business decision-making.
Keywords: case study; sport marketing; methodology; qualitative research; theory building
affiliations: Shanghai University of Sport; University of Georgia

title: Cybersecurity is imperative in robotic arthroplasty
authors: Vaibhav Bagaria; Raju Vaishya; Abhishek Vaish; Sébastien Lustig
Tags: precision orthopedics; surgical ecosystems; cyber threats; operational risks
abstract: Robotic platforms have revolutionized arthroplasty through precision and patient-specific planning, yet introduce cyber-physical vulnerabilities in interconnected surgical ecosystems. Recent incidents, including the 2026 cyber-attack, highlight operational risks despite low direct intraoperative threats. Proactive cybersecurity, via FDA-aligned secure design, institutional audits, and surgeon vigilance, is imperative to safeguard patient safety and trust in precision orthopedics.
Keywords: cybersecurity; robotic arthroplasty; cyber-physical vulnerabilities; patient safety; FDA

title: A review on Cybersecurity Challenges and Threats in modern world
venue: International Journal of Technology & Emerging Research
Tags: cybersecurity awareness; cyber threats; information security; emerging technologies
abstract: Theoretical frameworks and empirical findings consistently underscore the prominence of cybersecurity awareness as a central theme in contemporary cybersecurity research. This review paper aims to gain knowledge about the term ‘cybersecurity’ and the need to analyze and identify various kinds of threats that a person or organization may face in this information era, because as connectivity increases, the devices can interact and share information, and this could lead to a rise in cybersecurity risk. This paper covers various threats, including malware, ransomware, phishing, data breaches, and physical manipulation. These threats carry potential consequences such as financial loss, psychosocial impacts, erosion of trust, and unauthorized disclosure of sensitive data. “This discussion examines AI-enabled defence strategies and procedural frameworks that contribute to strengthening cybersecurity awareness and protection.” This paper is based on data collected from government agencies. This paper defines cybersecurity and discusses various cybersecurity threats like phishing, data breaches, ransomware, denial of service, and advanced persistent threats. The primary goal is to lower expenses brought on by cybersecurity risks.
Keywords: cybersecurity; threats; malware; ransomware; phishing; data breaches; AI defense strategies

title: AI and Cybersecurity Challenges in Born-Digital Companies
venue: Advances in Computational Intelligence and Robotics
Tags: digital security; innovation; cyber risks; privacy issues
abstract: Born-digital companies are businesses that arise in the digital era without a traditional physical presence. They represent some of the most dynamic and rapidly growing organizations today. These companies are agile, scalable, and innovative. They derive their strength from cloud platforms, data-driven strategies, and digital customer engagement. However, their reliance on digital tools also makes them highly vulnerable to cyber risks. Unlike older organizations that slowly digitalize, born-digital firms face cybersecurity threats from the very beginning of their inception. These threats include gaps in cloud security, privacy issues, regulatory challenges, identity theft, and the ongoing struggle to grow without compromising security. This chapter aims to explore how such companies deal with cybersecurity challenges, identify their weaknesses, and outline strategies to help them navigate this complex landscape.
Keywords: born-digital companies; cybersecurity challenges; cloud security; data-driven strategies; identity theft
affiliations: Nirma University; National Institute of Advanced Manufacturing Technology

title: An Interactive Hands- On Cybersecurity Learning and CTF Platform
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: Cyber Warrior; Cybersecurity Education; Interactive Learning; Technical Assistance; Docker Environments
abstract: This report presents the design and implementation of "Cyber Warrior," a cybersecurity training platform developed at Arab International University. It synthesises Natural Language Processing (NLP) and containerisation to address the lack of real-time guidance in traditional Capture The Flag (CTF) environments. Features include: AI-Driven Support: A Rasa-based assistant providing technical hints and command formulation with 0.735 peak accuracy. Automated Lab Isolation: 100% success rate in provisioning isolated Docker hacking environments (e.g., Kali Linux).Secure Full-Stack Architecture: Built with Node.js and React, featuring JWT-based security and BOLA/BFLA mitigation. The project provides a scalable methodology for bridging the gap between theoretical study and professional cybersecurity application. This work was conducted at Arab International University (AIU), Syria. The official website of the university is: https://www.aiu.edu.sy
Keywords: Cybersecurity Training; Capture The Flag; Natural Language Processing; Containerisation; AI-Driven Support

title: Peer Review Report For: Emerging Trends in Cybersecurity: Machine Learning as a Game-Changer in Next-Generation Cybersecurity Applications [version 1; peer review: 2 approved with reservations]
Tags: cybersecurity trends; machine learning applications; research analysis; information security
abstract: Background The relentless surge and growing frequency of cyber threats have indicated that traditional cybersecurity systems are ineffective. The need for more vigorous measures to safeguard information systems has never been more critical. This dilemma underscores the urgent need for advanced, adaptive cybersecurity solutions to detect and proactively counter these sophisticated threats. The study aims to investigate the game-changing role of machine learning in advancing cybersecurity through an in-depth scientometrics and bibliometric analysis. The study aims to map the current research landscape, identify significant contributions, discover emerging trends, and underscore key advancements in machine learning-based cybersecurity practices. Methods The Scopus database was used to conduct bibliometric and scientometric analyses of the machine learning and cybersecurity literature published from 2010 to 2024. Advanced tools were employed for scientometric analysis to evaluate scholarly output, authors’ impact, and the co-occurrence of keywords across geographical, organisational, and thematic indicators. Results The study found that India remains at the top in publication count, with IEEE Access as the leading journal and Princess Nourah Bint Abdul Rahman University as the most productive institution in machine learning and cybersecurity research. The study finds that Alazab, M., and Rao, R. are the most dominant authors. The findings revealed a significant increase in scholarly output since 2013, with intrusion detection, cybercrime prevention, and machine learning techniques identified as the most prominent themes. Conclusions The study highlights the significant role of ML in deriving next-generation cybersecurity solutions. The results could empower practitioners and researchers to establish a proactive, machine-learning-driven cybersecurity infrastructure. Future research should emphasise collaboration with other disciplines, including the social and psychological aspects of cyber threats.
Keywords: machine learning; cybersecurity; scientometrics; bibliometrics; cyber threats

title: Cybersecurity Threats and the Credibility of Digital Media Platforms in Ebonyi State, Nigeria
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; News Media
Tags: cyberattacks; phishing; malware; media credibility; Nigeria
abstract: Background: The rapid adoption of digital media platforms in Nigeria has significantly enhanced communication, information dissemination, and public engagement. However, the increasing prevalence of cyberattacks poses serious threats to the reliability, security, and credibility of these platforms, particularly in emerging digital environments such as Ebonyi State. Objective: This study examined the impact of cyberattacks on digital media operations and public trust among internet users in Ebonyi State, Nigeria. Method: A mixed-methods research design was adopted. Quantitative data were collected from 317 active internet users through structured questionnaires, while qualitative insights were obtained through in-depth interviews with media practitioners and cybersecurity experts. Data were analyzed using descriptive statistics and thematic analysis. Results: Findings revealed that phishing, malware, and hacking are the most prevalent cyber threats affecting digital media platforms. The study further showed that cyberattacks significantly disrupt media operations, including content production and distribution, and contribute to a noticeable decline in public trust in digital information sources. Despite awareness of cybersecurity risks, many media organizations lack adequate protective measures. Conclusion: The study concludes that cyberattacks constitute a major challenge to the efficiency and credibility of digital media platforms in Ebonyi State. The persistence of these threats continues to undermine public confidence and highlights the vulnerability of digital communication systems in developing contexts. Unique Contribution: This study provides empirical insight into the relationship between cyber threats, media operations, and audience trust within a sub-national context. It contributes to the growing body of knowledge on cybersecurity challenges in developing societies and offers practical perspectives for strengthening digital media resilience. Key Recommendation: The study recommends the adoption of advanced cybersecurity technologies, continuous training for media personnel, and sustained public awareness campaigns on safe digital practices. Additionally, stronger collaboration among media organizations, government agencies, and cybersecurity experts is essential to ensure a secure and trustworthy digital media environment.
Keywords: cybersecurity; digital media; Ebonyi State; cyber threats; public trust

title: Exploring Generative AI in Cybersecurity Covering Risks Defences and Ethical Implications
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: AI; Cyber Threats; Defensive Measures; Privacy; Ethics
abstract: Abstract Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) has quickly altered the cybersecurity arena both in terms of powerful features and major threats. In this paper, the author discusses how generative AI can be useful in cybersecurity and how it can be used to improve defensive and offensive activity. On the offensive side, generative AI can be used to develop advanced cyber threats, such as highly personalized phishing, automated malware on the one hand, and deepfake-based social engineering, on the other. Such advancements amplify the magnitude, velocity, and efficiency of cyberattacks as well as reduce the technical obstacles of malevolent entities. On the other hand, general AI can further reinforce cybersecurity defenses by providing high-quality threat detection, anomaly detection, automation in incident response and predicting security systems.The paper also discusses the major threats of generative AI, including advanced attacks, fake news, identity theft, and excessive dependence on artificial intelligence. Also, it indicates key ethical issues, such as the privacy, prejudice, responsibility, and the necessity of regulations. Although it has benefits, generative AI is associated with such challenges as data constraints, model weakness, great computing demands, and interpretability.It can be concluded in this paper that generative AI is a two-sided sword in cybersecurity; however, when implemented responsibly, along with effective governance and constant development, it has a chance to make digital systems more resilient and secure.
Keywords: Generative AI; Cybersecurity; Threat Detection; Ethical Implications; Incident Response

title: THE INFLUENCE OF CYBERSECURITY EFFECTIVENESS ON THE OPERATIONAL STABILITY OF AVIATION BUSINESSES: A CAUSAL MODEL ANALYSIS
authors: Chanatip Jeensoontorn, Vichit U-on, and Nontipan Prayurhong
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: cybersecurity; aviation; risk management; digital practices
abstract: Cybersecurity issues in organizations arise from insufficient risk management and a lack of unified policy direction driven by leadership. In addition, non-standardized digital work practices and unclear system integration create vulnerabilities and delay responses to cyber threats, ultimately reducing operational stability in the aviation industry. The objectives of this research were: (1) to examine the causal factors of risk management, leadership commitment, digital work practices, cybersecurity effectiveness, and operational stability; (2) to analyze the influence of these factors on cybersecurity effectiveness and its impact on operational stability; and (3) to develop a causal model of cybersecurity effectiveness influencing operational stability. A mixed-methods approach was employed, combining qualitative and quantitative data collection. Data were gathered through in-depth interviews and online questionnaires distributed to aviation-related organizations between March 2026 and April 2026, resulting in a total sample size of 580 respondents. The results of the analysis indicate that risk management significantly influences cybersecurity effectiveness and indirectly affects operational stability through cybersecurity effectiveness. Similarly, leadership commitment has a strong positive effect on cybersecurity effectiveness and indirectly enhances operational stability. Digital work practices were also found to significantly influence cybersecurity effectiveness and contribute to operational stability through a mediating effect. Furthermore, cybersecurity effectiveness has a direct and significant impact on operational stability, confirming its critical role in maintaining continuity and resilience within aviation operations. In conclusion, this study highlights the importance of integrating risk management, leadership commitment, and secures digital practices to strengthen cybersecurity effectiveness. The findings provide practical implications for aviation organizations by emphasizing the need for a holistic approach to cybersecurity management. Such an approach not only enhances operational stability but also supports business continuity and strengthens stakeholder confidence in the aviation industry.
Keywords: cybersecurity effectiveness; operational stability; aviation industry; risk management; leadership commitment

title: Cyber Shield on Campus: A Study of Cybersecurity Awareness Among College Students
venue: International Journal of Engineering Research and Science & Technology
Tags: cybersecurity; education; youth awareness; risk management
abstract: In this time of fast digital change, online security has become a major problem for everyone, private companies, and governments. College or university students are among the most active users of online technologies, are particularly at risk to cyber threats such as online scams, stealing personal information, harmful software attacks, and data leaks. This study says to evaluate the level of cybersecurity knowledge among college or university students in India, focusing on key areas such as how to manage a password, correct use of social media, identifying online scams, and data protection methods. A survey was performed among undergraduate students from different courses to understand what is the level of awareness and online activities. The results show that although students have basic knowledge of online security concepts, there is a clear gap when it comes to properly using safe methods. Many students don’t use use important safety steps such as two-step verification, changing passwords regularly, and carefully checking unknown links before clicking or opening them. The study shows that it is necessary to include online security education in academic syllabus and to spread awareness through different types of seminars, workshops, training sessions, and college policies etc. It also suggests useful steps to improve knowledge of cybersecurity and promote safe use of digital technologies among the college or university students
Keywords: cybersecurity awareness; college students; data protection; online security; social media usage

title: Going Beyond Firewalls: Managing Cybersecurity and Privacy in Today's Hyper-Connected World
authors: Mr. Shakil Mubarak Mulla; Seema Shahaji Desai
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: cyber threats; privacy concerns; organizational practices; digital technology
abstract: The rapid growth of digital technologies has significantly transformed communication, commerce, governance, and everyday life. However, this growing digital interdependence has simultaneously built up cybersecurity risks and privacy concerns. This study examines the contemporary cybersecurity environment with three key objectives. First, it analyzes the prevailing types of cyber threats and privacy violations, including malware attacks, identity misuse, phishing, and data breaches. Second, it evaluates weaknesses within current security infrastructures and organizational practices that may expose systems to risk. Third, it examines individuals’ awareness, perceptions, and behavioral responses related to cybersecurity and privacy protection. By integrating technical, organizational, and behavioral perspectives, this research offers practical insights for strengthening cybersecurity governance and enhancing digital resilience in an increasingly interconnected world.
Keywords: cybersecurity; privacy; cyber threats; malware; phishing; data breaches; digital resilience

title: Machine Learning Involved in Explainable Artificial Intelligence in Cybersecurity and Legal Systems
venue: Attacks and Defenses in Explainable Artificial Intelligence
Tags: XAI; Transparency; Trust; Security Analysis
abstract: Machine learning (ML) has a substantial effect on the realms of cybersecurity as well as the legal field, but it often lacks transparency in its decision-making process. This is where explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) comes in to bridge the gap. Here's how ML interacts with XAI in these two fields: cybersecurity and legal system. ML algorithms excel at identifying patterns in vast amounts of data, making them ideal for tasks. In this research, it focuses on malware detection on classifying files as benign or malicious based on features. By challenging with opacity, ML models are especially used in black boxes. Security analysts often struggle to understand why the system flags something as suspicious. With this lack of explainability, some problems can lead to two ways. One of the ways is “alert fatigue,” which represents a constant barrage of unexplained alerts can overwhelm security teams and lead to ignoring important threats. The second way is “difficulty in trusting the system,” which represents if analysts do not understand the reasoning behind detections, they might hesitate to rely on the system's judgment. By incorporating XAI, legal professionals can ensure that ML-driven decisions are fair, unbiased, and understandable, fostering trust in the legal system. The aim of this paper is to show how ML provides the analytical power in cybersecurity and legal systems, while XAI acts as the interpreter, connecting the intricate algorithms to the human comprehension. This combination is crucial for building trust and ensuring responsible decision-making in these critical fields.
Keywords: Machine Learning; Explainable AI; Cybersecurity; Legal Systems; Malware Detection

title: LLM agents security duality: a comprehensive survey of self-security and empowered cybersecurity
authors: Yiwei Xu; Yong Zhuang; Xuanming Liu; Tian Zhang; Bowen Xiao; Xiaoyang Xu; Delong Jiang; Juan Wang; Hongxin Hu
Tags: LLM Agents; Self-security; Empowered Cybersecurity; Cyber Offense-Defense
abstract: Abstract Large language model (LLM) agents are rapidly being integrated into real-world systems. Their autonomy and tool-use capabilities generate substantial value while simultaneously expanding the security attack surface. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of the opportunities and challenges of LLM agents in security, focusing on two core areas: (1) threats to LLM agents themselves and corresponding mitigation strategies (LLM agents self-security), and (2) the role of LLM agents in empowering the cybersecurity lifecycle across offense and defense (LLM agents empowered cybersecurity). We first examine the internal and external attack surfaces of agents, propose a taxonomy organized by threat sources, and analyze associated mitigations and evaluation frameworks. We then investigate how agent capabilities are applied in cybersecurity practice and present, to our knowledge, the first agent-empowerment framework aligned with the full cyber offense–defense lifecycle. By systematically surveying these two areas, we are the first to highlight a positive feedback synergy between LLM agents self-security and empowered cybersecurity, offering new insights for the advancement of both. We further identify current limitations and outline promising directions for future research. The insights provided aim to catalyze the coordinated development of LLM agents self-security and agent empowered cybersecurity, paving the way for more capable and robust agent applications.
Keywords: Large Language Models; Security; Cybersecurity; Attack Surface; Mitigation Strategies
affiliations: Wuhan University; University at Buffalo, State University of New York

title: Do demographic characteristics of directors and commissioners affect the level of cybersecurity disclosure in Indonesian banking?
authors: Arief Rahman; Aditya Pandu Wicaksono; Tiyas Kurnia Sari; Muhammad Fadhly Rizky Octavio; Zahron Abdurrahman; Noorfaiz Athallah Koeswandana; Hanifah Zahra
venue: Journal of Accounting in Emerging Economies
Tags: cybersecurity; banking; governance; Indonesia
abstract: Purpose Corporate boards play a vital role in preventing cyber threats through cybersecurity disclosure policies, strategies and practices. This study examines the influence of the demographic characteristics of corporate boards on cybersecurity disclosure in two-tier governance system companies that include board of directors and board of commissioners. Current research focuses on the banking industry, which is highly exposed to cyber threats. Design/methodology/approach The research examined 46 banks listed on the Indonesia Stock Exchange from 2018 to 2022. Analysis using regression techniques was applied to determine the relationship between board characteristics and cybersecurity disclosure. Additional analysis has been conducted to enrich the findings. Findings The study finds that the average age of board directors and board of commissioners positively and significantly affects cybersecurity disclosure, while tenure of both boards has a negative and significant effect. Furthermore, banks with more board members who have educational backgrounds in information technology tend to have higher levels of cybersecurity disclosure. These findings recommend that regulators should encourage more boards with IT backgrounds to help mitigate cybersecurity risks. Research limitations/implications The measurement of cybersecurity disclosure based on word count and subjective judgment. Future studies may develop more objective indicators for assessing cybersecurity disclosure. The study also focuses on a limited set of demographic characteristics of board members. Practical implications The findings suggest that regulatory bodies should develop guidelines for cybersecurity disclosure to enhance transparency and accountability in the financial sector. Additionally, the study highlights the significance of considering the proportion of IT-educated board members to strengthen cybersecurity oversight. Originality/value This is one of the few papers investigate cybersecurity disclosure and its relationships with board demographic factors. The research is conducted in Indonesia, where two-boards system applied and cybersecurity disclosure is voluntary.
Keywords: cybersecurity disclosure; corporate boards; demographic characteristics; Indonesian banking; information technology

title: Securing responsible AI: Integrating cybersecurity and AI governance at NovexTech solutions
venue: Journal of Information Technology Teaching Cases
Tags: AI; cyber risk; governance; IT services; NovexTech Solutions
abstract: This postgraduate-level teaching case explores the governance challenges that arise when artificial intelligence becomes embedded within enterprise cybersecurity and operational decision-making. Set in a mid-sized European IT services firm, NovexTech Solutions, the case examines how a ransomware incident affecting AI-enabled analytics systems exposes limitations in existing governance arrangements. While the organisation successfully restores services using established incident response and business continuity procedures, post-incident reviews reveal deeper concerns related to AI model integrity, accountability, and oversight across the AI development and operational lifecycle. As regulatory expectations intensify under frameworks such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Network and Information Systems Directive 2 (NIS2 Directive), and the European Union Artificial Intelligence Act (EU AI Act), NovexTech faces growing pressure from clients, partners, and broader societal stakeholders to demonstrate coherent and defensible governance. Students are invited to analyse governance fragmentation, evaluate trade-offs between control and innovation, and assess how organisations can structure responsible and resilient governance in AI-enabled environments. The case culminates in a board-level governance dilemma requiring senior leadership to determine whether to maintain parallel cybersecurity and AI governance structures or redesign governance into an integrated framework capable of managing AI-enabled cyber risk while sustaining innovation and regulatory accountability.
Keywords: AI governance; cybersecurity; ransomware; incident response; regulatory compliance

title: Cybersecurity Awareness as a Mediating Variable in the Relationship between ICS and AIS in Iraqi State Banks
authors: Younis Ahmed Al-Mohammedi; Ahmed S. Tarkh; Hamzah N. Al-Jumaili; Nedhal Aziz Mahdi
Tags: information security; internal control; cybersecurity; banking; data protection
abstract: Research Objective This research aims to study the relationship between internal control and accounting information security, while exploring the role of cybersecurity awareness as a mediating variable that can contribute to strengthening this relationship. Research Significance This research gains its significance from bridging the knowledge gap in accounting literature concerning the integration of internal control systems with information security in the face of cyber challenges. Research Methodology and Tools Considering the nature of the problem and the research objectives, this study follows the steps of the descriptive-analytical method. The aim is to determine the role of cybersecurity awareness as a mediating variable in strengthening the relationship between internal control systems and accounting information security. This includes analyzing data and information related to enhancing the importance of cybersecurity awareness in the local environment, both theoretically and empirically. The analysis was conducted through a survey examining the relationship between internal control systems and accounting information security, mediated by cybersecurity awareness among employees at different administrative levels in the banks included in the study sample. The PLS Smart software was used to process and analyze data. The research found that internal control systems are closely linked to the security of accounting information, with the mediating factor being cybersecurity awareness. This finding indicates a positive and significant relationship. This demonstrates the crucial and positive role of control in protecting data when cybersecurity awareness is raised. Recommendations The researchers recommend that banks adopt best practices for information security protection and implement strong and clear policies and procedures for cybersecurity awareness.
Keywords: cybersecurity awareness; internal control systems; accounting information security; Iraqi state banks; cyber challenges
affiliations: University Of Fallujah; University of Baghdad

title: Circumventing the Norm: Adaptive Human-Centric Cybersecurity Responses for Persistent AI Infrastructure
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: AIVORIX OS; cybersecurity responses; human-centric
abstract: Research paper exploring adaptive governance-centric cybersecurity approaches and persistent AI infrastructure resilience models associated with AIVORIX OS™.
Keywords: cybersecurity; AI infrastructure; adaptive governance; resilience models

title: AI-Driven Cybersecurity and Data Protection: Modern Approaches, Challenges, and Emerging Solutions
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: AI; Cybersecurity; Data Protection; Automated Threat Detection; Adversarial Learning
abstract: Artificial intelligence is transforming cybersecurity and data protection, enabling automated threat detection, intelligent incident response, and proactive vulnerability identification that were previously impossible with rule-based systems alone. iously impossible with rule-based systems alone. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of AI-driven approaches to cybersecurity and data protection, examining both the opportunities and challenges that AI introduces to the security landscape. We analyze the dual-use nature of AI in security contexts, where the same techniques that enable advanced threat detection can also be weaponized by a dversaries to launch more sophisticated attacks. The paper reviews state-of-the-art AI security techniques across threat detection, malware analysis, vulnerability assessment, and privacy protection domains. We identify key challenges including adversarial attacks on AI models, data quality issues, interpretability requirements, and the evolving regulatory landscape. Based on our analysis, we propose a framework for responsible AI deployment in security applications.
Keywords: AI; Cybersecurity; Data Protection; Threat Detection; Vulnerability Assessment

title: AI-Powered Cybersecurity for Financial Transactions using Anomaly Detection and Graph Neural Networks
authors: Mathankumar Selvaraj; Sivakumar C; Thamilpriya A; Dr. Sudhakar K; Princy J; Keerthana E; Dr. G. Jenitha; Dr. Monica Apte
venue: International Journal of Drug Delivery Technology
Tags: Cyber Threats; Machine Learning; Financial Security; Graph Models; Real-Time Detection
abstract: The rapid digitalization of financial systems has significantly increased the vulnerability of financial transactions to cyber threats, fraud, and sophisticated attack patterns. Traditional rule-based and statistical fraud detection systems are no longer sufficient to handle complex, dynamic, and large-scale transaction environments. This research paper proposes an AI-powered cybersecurity framework leveraging anomaly detection techniques integrated with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to enhance fraud detection accuracy and real-time decisionmaking. By modeling financial transactions as graph structures, the proposed approach captures relational dependencies among entities such as users, accounts, devices, and transactions. The study explores supervised, unsupervised, and hybrid anomaly detection models, emphasizing GNN-based architectures, including temporal and heterogeneous graphs. Experimental insights from recent studies demonstrate improved detection rates, reduced false positives, and enhanced interpretability. The paper also provides a comprehensive literature review of over 30 studies, identifies research gaps, and outlines future directions in AI-driven financial cybersecurity.
Keywords: AI; Cybersecurity; Anomaly Detection; Graph Neural Networks; Fraud Detection; Financial Transactions
affiliations: Department of Biotechnology; KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology; Orthopaedic Research Group; AMET University; Spicer Adventist University

title: Consumer awareness, trust and behavior toward cybersecurity risks that banks in Bahrain face
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; News Media; Forums & Communities
Tags: cyber awareness; online banking; consumer behavior
abstract: This research examines the level of awareness consumers have towards cybersecurity risks that banks face in Bahrain and how trust and behavior are impacted. The field of banking has become more technologically advanced with the passing of time. These developments offer more valuable and effective services, but they also bring with them cybersecurity issues that need to be resolved (Alsmadi & Zarour, 2020). These challenges include the rising threats of cybercrimes like phishing and vishing (Rao, 2019). Cybercrimes can have a negative, long-lasting impact on consumers and banks. For consumers, it affects their trust, finances, and behaviors, while for banking institutions, it threatens their operations and reputation (AlBenJasim et al., 2023).The study found that consumers in Bahrain have moderate trust, awareness, and cybersecurity behavior while undertaking online banking transactions. Although most rely on online banking services, active behavior and awareness of cybersecurity threats are low. Age was the only demographic factor that was statistically associated with cybersecurity behavior. Awareness was found to be positively related to trust and behavior, reflecting its contribution to developing secure banking practices. Most of the consumers believe banks should improve in communicating with them on protective security measures, especially via in-app guidance and additional security features.All in all, this research gathers information from the Bahraini population to assess their level of awareness with the overall hopes of increasing trust and promoting positive online banking behaviors.
Keywords: cybersecurity; consumer trust; banking; phishing; Bahrain

title: AN ENSEMBLE MACHINE LEARNING FRAMEWORK FOR AUTOMATED CYBERSECURITY INCIDENT CLASSIFICATION USING STRUCTURED METADATA
authors: Samuel Adeolu Ogunbiyi; Oludele Adeleke; O. Osunade
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: automated classification; cyber threats; machine learning framework; decision-making; incident response
abstract: This study addresses the growing need for accurate and automated cyber incident classification systems to support timely decision-making in response to increasing cyber threats. It develops and evaluates an ensemble machine learning framework for multi-class classification using structured cybersecurity incident metadata. A dataset of 93,144 incidents, categorized into six attack types, was analyzed using four supervised learning algorithms: Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forest, and Extra Trees. To address class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) was applied. The dataset was split into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing, and model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, alongside confusion matrix and feature importance analysis. Results show that ensemble models outperform non-ensemble approaches, with the Extra Trees classifier achieving the highest performance (95.4% accuracy and 95.2% macro F1-score), followed by Random Forest (94%), Decision Tree (88%), and KNN (81%). Performance remained consistently high across all attack categories, with most misclassifications occurring between closely related classes. Feature importance analysis revealed that over 80% of predictive performance was driven by variables such as user agent, source IP, destination IP, and temporal features. The study concludes that lightweight and interpretable ensemble models can achieve high classification accuracy comparable to more complex methods while remaining computationally efficient. The proposed framework offers a practical and scalable solution for real-world cybersecurity incident classification and supports improved incident response strategies.
Keywords: cyber incident classification; machine learning; ensemble models; cybersecurity; data analysis

title: The Adversarial Discount - AI, Signal Correlation, and the Cybersecurity Arms Race
Tags: Cybersecurity; AI; Adversarial Models; Information Aggregation; Resource Allocation
abstract: We study a contest-theoretic model of adversarial investment in which an attacker and a defender allocate resources to AI-augmented capabilities across multiple attack surfaces. The attacker's investment operates through two channels: it amplifies offensive potency unconditionally and erodes defensive effectiveness conditionally, generating an adversarial discount that deepens endogenously with the defender's own investment. We derive a closed-form arms race ratio decomposing the relative marginal effectiveness of offensive and defensive investment into six structural primitives and establish equilibrium uniqueness and global convergence under a continuous best-response dynamic. The central result concerns signal cross-correlation, the degree to which threat intelligence on one surface informs detection on another. With full cross-correlation, the arms race ratio is independent of the number of attack surfaces: the attacker's structural advantage from surface proliferation is completely neutralized. Under the benchmark full-dilution case, without cross-correlation, per-surface defense effectiveness vanishes as the attack surface grows. Extending the analysis to heterogeneous defenders facing an attacker who targets by expected value, we argue that the model points to a dual inefficiency: overinvestment in private defense (a zero-sum redirective externality) and underinvestment in shared signal correlation (a public good). These formal results, together with public-good reasoning outside the base model, characterize when collective information aggregation can dominate private capability investment as the decisive margin in adversarial contests.
Keywords: Adversarial Investment; Cybersecurity Arms Race; AI-Augmented Capabilities; Signal Correlation; Threat Intelligence

title: Analyse Existing Wireless Network Forensic Techniques and Identify their Limitations in Supporting Effective Cybersecurity Decision-Making
authors: Mudambi Geoffrey; Davis Matovu; Andrew Lukyamuzi; Semwogere Twaibu; Odongotoo Godfrey; Andrew Alunyu E.
venue: International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology
Tags: network security; forensic techniques; cyber threat; intelligent analysis; real-time monitoring
abstract: The rapid growth of wireless communication technologies has significantly increased the complexity and vulnerability of network environments, making them prime targets for sophisticated cyberattacks. Wireless network forensics plays a crucial role in investigating and responding to such incidents; however, existing forensic techniques often fall short in supporting effective and timely cybersecurity decision-making. These limitations include inadequate real-time analysis, poor scalability in handling large volumes of traffic data, limited automation, and insufficient integration with intelligent decision-support systems. This study aims to analyse existing wireless network forensic techniques and identify their limitations while proposing an intelligent traffic analysis framework to enhance cybersecurity decision-making. The research adopts a mixed-methods approach, combining qualitative analysis of current forensic tools and techniques with quantitative evaluation of network traffic datasets. The study further integrates machine learning algorithms and data analytics methods to develop models capable of detecting, classifying, and predicting anomalous network behaviour in wireless environments. The proposed framework emphasizes real-time traffic monitoring, intelligent anomaly detection, and automated forensic analysis to improve the accuracy, speed, and reliability of cyber incident investigations. Experimental results are expected to demonstrate that the integration of intelligent traffic analysis significantly enhances threat detection capabilities, reduces response time, and supports proactive and evidence-based cybersecurity decisions. This research contributes to the field of network forensics by bridging the gap between traditional forensic methods and intelligent analytical approaches. It provides a scalable and adaptive solution tailored to modern wireless network challenges and offers practical recommendations for improving forensic readiness and cybersecurity resilience in dynamic environments.
Keywords: wireless network forensics; cybersecurity decision-making; anomaly detection; machine learning; traffic analysis

title: ZERO-DAY VULNERABILITIES: ANATOMY, EXPLOITATION LIFECYCLE, AND PROACTIVE DEFENSE FRAMEWORKS IN MODERN CYBERSECURITY
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: vulnerabilities; cyber threats; defense strategies; attack campaigns; economic markets
abstract: Zero-day vulnerabilities represent one of the most critical and elusive threats in the contemporary cybersecurity landscape. Unlike known vulnerabilities with available patches, zero-days are unknown to vendors at the time of exploitation, granting attackers an asymmetric advantage. This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the zero-day vulnerability lifecycle — from discovery and weaponization to exploitation, disclosure, and remediation. We examine the economic and intelligence-driven markets that fuel zero-day trading, including both gray-market brokers and state-sponsored acquisition programs. Drawing on documented cases including the Stuxnet worm, EternalBlue, and the FORCEDENTRY exploit chain targeting Apple iOS, we illustrate how zero-days are integrated into sophisticated attack campaigns. Furthermore, we evaluate existing mitigation frameworks such as threat intelligence platforms, anomaly-based intrusion detection, exploit mitigation technologies (ASLR, DEP, CFI), and bug bounty programs. Our findings suggest that a multi-layered proactive defense strategy — combining technical hardening with organizational processes and coordinated vulnerability disclosure — offers the most resilient approach to managing zero-day risk. This work is aimed at both academic researchers and security practitioners seeking a structured understanding of the zero-day threat domain.
Keywords: zero-day vulnerabilities; cybersecurity; exploitation lifecycle; proactive defense; threat intelligence

title: Human-Centric Approaches To Cybersecurity: Insider Threat Mitigation And Social Engineering Risk In Digital Business Environments
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; News Media; Forums & Communities
Tags: Cybersecurity; Risk Mitigation; Digital Business
abstract: The rapid digital transformation of modern organisations has fundamentally altered the cybersecurity landscape. Businesses increasingly rely on interconnected digital platforms, cloud infrastructure, and remote workflows, thereby expanding the attack surface available to adversaries. While considerable investment has been directed toward technical security controls such as firewalls, encryption protocols, and intrusion detection systems, empirical evidence consistently demonstrates that the human element remains the most exploited vulnerability in organisational security architectures.
Keywords: Insider Threat; Social Engineering; Cybersecurity; Digital Transformation; Human Element
affiliations: Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam Technical University

title: Knowledge-Augmented Large Language Models for Automated Characterization of Cybersecurity Vulnerabilities
authors: Elijah Needham; Denis Ulybyshev; Ayesha Dina
Tags: Cybersecurity; NVD; CVEs; Automated Characterization; LLM
abstract: The US National Vulnerability Database (NVD) is a public repository of software and hardware vulnerabilities maintained by the National Institute of Standards and Technology, which also introduced the Vulnerability Description Ontology (VDO) to standardize vulnerability characterization. Despite advances in secure software development and techniques for detecting vulnerabilities, the number ofsoftware vulnerabilities registered in the NVD continue to increase, making accurate characterization essential for selecting effective defense strategies and reducing cyber risk. However, manual labeling is costly and time-consuming, and traditional machine learning approaches often require large labeled datasets. This paper proposes an LLM-driven framework for Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs) characterization guided by VDO. The framework includes two agents: (1) a Context Enrichment Agent that augments sparse descriptions of CVEs with relevanttechnical information from external sources, and (2) an Ontology Guided Characterization Agent that performs structured multi-label classification using VDO definitions and N-shot prompting. This design addresses the limited details in the official CVE text, the complexity and imbalance of VDO labels, and generalization to newly disclosed vulnerabilities. We evaluated the framework on a VDO labeled benchmark dataset and on a newly created dataset of 125 recently disclosed CVEs from 2024 to 2025 labeled by our research project team. Experiments with GPT 4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Llama 3.1 405B show consistent gains from context enrichment and N-shot prompting. GPT 4o achieves macro F1 scores up to 0.81, 0.91, 0.90, 0.87, and 0.83 on the benchmark for Context, Impact Method, Attack Theater, Logical Impact, and Mitigation, respectively, and reaches up to 0.95 macro F1 for Impact Method on the 2024 to 2025 dataset. Our source code is available: https://github.com/ayeshasdina/KA-LLM-CVE.
Keywords: Vulnerabilities; Machine Learning; Context Enrichment; Ontology; Classification

title: A Risk-Based Framework for Prioritising Cybersecurity Controls Using MITRE ATT&CK with Empirical Validation via Adversary Emulation
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Government Data; News Media
Tags: Cybersecurity; Risk Management; Threat Modeling
abstract: This research proposes a risk-based framework for prioritising cybersecurity controls using the MITRE ATT&CK matrix and adversary emulation methodologies. The study aims to improve cybersecurity decision-making by integrating attacker behaviour, quantitative risk scoring, business impact, threat likelihood, and control maturity into a unified prioritisation model. The research addresses the limitations of traditional static and compliance-driven security approaches by introducing a threat-informed methodology that aligns defensive controls with real-world adversarial tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). To validate the proposed framework, practical adversary emulation scenarios were conducted using MITRE Caldera within a simulated enterprise lab environment supported by Sysmon and Wazuh telemetry analysis. The study contributes to the fields of cybersecurity risk management, detection engineering, and threat-informed defence by providing a scalable and measurable approach for reducing organisational cyber risk exposure and improving strategic security control implementation.
Keywords: MITRE ATT&CK; Cybersecurity Controls; Risk-Based Framework; Adversary Emulation; Threat-Informed Defence

title: Specific features of legal regulation of countering cybercrime in the transport sector: the experience of the european union and international cybersecurity management practices
authors: I. V. Bratko; O. S. Kaspruk Ekmen
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Government Data; Academic Literature
Tags: cybersecurity; transportation; legal regulation; EU; international cooperation
abstract: Introduction.Throughout the development of international transportation, major transport corridors have emerged, linking not only key cities but also multiple countries and facilitating crossborder passenger and cargo movement.Transport relations of the modern period are not limited only to relations within a certain country, but have an international character.As A. Haque rightly noted, "Globalization was invented by birds that have been flying around the world since time immemorial" (Hacke, 2005).The international nature of modern transport relations determines the involvement of national sections of transport corridors of a certain country in international transport corridors.Therefore, cybercrimes in the transport sector are a problem not only of one country, but of the world community.Information and communication technologies actively target the transport sector in multiple countries and disrupt international transport links.The number of cyberattacks on the transport systems of various countries has increased significantly in recent years and continues to grow every year.Researchers have devoted very little attention to cybersecurity issues and the legal regulation of countering cybercrime in the transport sector.There are still no effective international legal and national legal mechanisms for effective countering cybercrimes in the transport sector.In the context of regulating cybercrime in the transport sector, the European Union plays a key role in establishing EU-wide cybersecurity standards.EU regulations harmonize the national legal systems of member states, set mandatory requirements for the protection of critical transport infrastructure, and create mechanisms for intergovernmental cooperation to counter cyberattacks.Principles of private international law play a crucial role in regulating cybercrime, particularly in cases of transnational cyber threats.These principles define mechanisms for determining jurisdiction, assigning liability, and coordinating legal procedures between states, enabling the alignment of national legal norms and ensuring effective prosecution of offenders across borders.Applying private international law strengthens national legal frameworks and lays the foundation for coordinated cybersecurity measures while preventing conflicts between the laws of different countries.Materials and methods.The scientific works of domestic researchers and foreign scientists became the theoretical basis of the research.Among them: I.
Keywords: cybercrime; transport sector; EU regulations; cybersecurity; international law

title: Securing Digital Corridors Under Hybrid Threats:Extending SDAF to Cybersecurity Governance of Critical Maritime Infrastructure in Geopolitical Flux
OSINT_Source_Types: Government Data; News Media; Public Records
Tags: cyber threats; maritime security; geopolitics; digital logistics; vendor risk
abstract: Maritime ports—now deeply digitalized andinterdependent—face escalating cyber risk amid hybridgeopolitical pressures, complex vendor ecosystems, andwidening social dependence on uninterrupted trade flows.Situated at the intersection of the Belt and Road Initiative andthe Trans-Caspian International Transport Route, the CaspianBasin exemplifies both the promise of data-driven logistics andthe vulnerability of fragmented cybersecurity governance. Thisstudy extends the Strategic Data Alignment Framework(SDAF), originally designed to align corporate strategy withdata governance, into a cybersecurity governance model forcritical maritime infrastructure under hybrid threat conditions.Using comparative policy analysis and benchmarking againstcontemporary global standards (e.g., NIS2-style obligations,maritime cyber guidelines, and digital trade principles), thestudy identifies systemic weaknesses in harmonization,institutional capacity, supply-chain assurance, and resilienceplanning. It reconceptualizes cyber-resilience as a strategicresource and proposes a five-step roadmap combining regionalthreat-intelligence sharing, vendor risk controls, standardsalignment, AI-enabled detection, and stress-tested recovery.The findings underscore urgent needs for coordinated action tosafeguard digital corridors and the societies they serve.
Keywords: cybersecurity governance; critical maritime infrastructure; hybrid threats; strategic data alignment; resilience planning

title: PhD Thesis – Western Cybersecurity: A New Entente – Federated Trust, Ethical AI and the Governance of Digital Sovereignty (Draft Version)
Tags: Cyber Defences; Democratic Resilience; Privacy-Preserving Models; Cyber Entente; Accountable Collaboration
abstract:
Statement on Ethical and Defensive Intent
This conceptual work is theoretical in nature and was undertaken exclusively for defensive cybersecurity research and policy development, in alignment with ethical AI principles, human rights standards and international law. The frameworks, models, and systems described herein are designed to strengthen resilience, transparency and cooperative security among democratic institutions. All concepts, names and abbreviations mentioned throughout this work are provided solely for educational purposes. This research does not support, enable or endorse offensive cyber operations or the misuse of digital technologies. Any interpretation or use of this work for harmful, offensive or adversarial purposes is strictly rejected and falls outside the scope, intent and permitted use of this research.
This preprint PhD thesis is also available on ResearchGate.net, at: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/396497919_PhD_Thesis_-_Western_Cybersecurity_A_New_Entente_-_Federated_Trust_Ethical_AI_and_the_Governance_of_Digital_Sovereignty_Draft_VersionAbstract: In the twenty-first century, the security of democratic societies depends not only on technological superiority but on the ethical legitimacy of their digital defences. This dissertation develops the concept of Federated Trust as the normative and technical foundation of a new Cyber Entente — a cooperative, privacy-preserving, and auditable cybersecurity framework for the Western alliance. The project argues that the resilience of democratic networks requires both distributed technological capability and shared moral governance: security without legitimacy cannot sustain public trust, while legitimacy without security cannot protect it.Drawing on political philosophy, AI ethics, and cyber-policy studies, the thesis synthesises a multidimensional framework that links digital sovereignty, ethical artificial intelligence, and federated learning architectures. It integrates the OECD AI Principles, NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0, ENISA Threat Landscape analyses, and NATO CCDCOE doctrines to propose a unified defensive model based on transparency, interoperability, and human oversight. The research employs a design-science methodology, combining conceptual analysis with a working prototype of a Federated Cyber Defence Infrastructure built using Flower (federated learning), PyTorch, and Opacus differential privacy. Complementary governance mechanisms — including Data-Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA), data-sharing Memoranda of Understanding (MOU), provenance logging, and cryptographic signing — demonstrate how ethical and technical compliance can co-evolve within a federated ecosystem.At its core, the thesis introduces the concept of a “Cyber-Iron Dome” — a federated, AI-enabled defensive network designed to detect, correlate, and mitigate cyber threats collaboratively across allied infrastructures. This system operates through secure aggregation, differential privacy, and human-in-the-loop oversight, ensuring lawful interoperability without compromising national autonomy. The “Cyber-Iron Dome” embodies deterrence through trust: a democratic model of collective resilience that strengthens the West’s digital sovereignty through ethical cooperation, transparency, and accountability.Simulation results using synthetic data confirm that privacy-preserving models can achieve effective anomaly detection while maintaining differential-privacy guarantees. The findings suggest that federated AI can reinforce trans-national cyber resilience without compromising individual rights or national sovereignty. Conceptually, Federated Trust reframes Western cybersecurity from a deterrence-based paradigm to a cooperative, ethical, and auditable alliance of systems and institutions. Practically, the Cyber Entente provides a blueprint for implementing trustworthy, law-aligned AI defences that respect democratic values while countering systemic cyber threats.Beyond the theoretical synthesis, the project advances into operational realisation through a fully developed Cyber Entente Developer Pack. This implementation includes modular code for federated nodes, aggregators, and trust dashboards; a provenance-based ledger architecture; and a Federated Trust Score (FTS) framework quantifying the ethical integrity of networked learning systems. Supporting tools — such as automated documentation generators, integration tests, and differential privacy pipelines — demonstrate that normative governance principles can be embedded directly into executable code and reproducible infrastructures. The inclusion of a Gantt-mapped deployment roadmap, Grafana observability layer, and Prometheus-compatible exporters further translates theoretical governance into a measurable, auditable, and extensible system of cyber-defensive operations.By completing the technical cycle from concept to demonstration, the Cyber Entente validates Federated Trust not merely as a theoretical construct but as an implementable model for democratic cyber resilience. Its transparent metrics, provenance ledgers, and automated reporting workflows establish a replicable standard for ethical AI compliance within secure digital alliances. This convergence of political philosophy, technical architecture, and operational governance marks a paradigm shift: from cybersecurity as secrecy and deterrence, to cybersecurity as accountable collaboration, where legitimacy and protection are mutually reinforcing pillars of digital sovereignty.
Keywords: Federated Trust; Ethical AI; Cybersecurity; Digital Sovereignty; Cooperative Security

title: Cybersecurity vulnerabilities of variable speed limit signs and their effects on accident risk and traffic safety
authors: Maryam Samaei; Mostafa Ameli; Samaneh Yazdanipour; Reza Arani
Tags: VSL; Cyberattacks; Traffic Management; Safety Assessment
abstract: Abstract Variable Speed Limits (VSL) are primarily designed to enhance traffic safety on hazardous roadways by dynamically adjusting speed limits based on real-time traffic and weather conditions. Most previous studies have focused on using VSLs to improve safety, mitigate congestion, and reduce environmental impact, assuming a securely connected VSL network. But what would be the impact of cyberattacks on VSL systems on traffic safety and network efficiency. This paper investigates the impact of intentional disruptions on VSL signs within traffic networks. We present a threat model to identify vulnerabilities in the VSL communication network and potential access points for attackers. An analytical accident model was developed, and various VSL disruption scenarios were simulated using a case study of Highway 1 in British Columbia, Canada, in SUMO (Simulation of Urban MObility) to assess the potential for crashes and safety problems resulting from VSL disruptions. Surrogate Safety Measures (SSM) were employed as key measures of safety concerns. Our findings highlight notable risks, including up to 56 $$\%$$ additional conflicts, posed by these intentional disruptions. A robustness assessment using Monte Carlo simulation runs of a scenario with heterogeneous driver behaviors demonstrated that the safety impact is consistent and not random seed-dependent. Across runs, intentional disruptions still yielded significant increases in conflicts, with an average rise of 24.95%, confirming the stability of the results. Various mitigation strategies are discussed to enhance traffic safety and resilience against VSL manipulation.
Keywords: Variable Speed Limits; Traffic Safety; Cybersecurity; Disruption Scenarios; Accident Risk
affiliations: Université Gustave Eiffel; University of California, Berkeley; University of Toronto
