Tags: DDoS detection; Educational IoT; AI model; hybrid approach; network security
abstract: The widespread adoption of electronic learning has made contemporary educational environments increasingly dependent on interconnected devices, enabling smart classrooms and remote monitoring systems. However, this technological advancement has also introduced substantial cybersecurity risks due to the vulnerabilities of unprotected, heterogeneous, and networked devices. Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks are among the most disruptive threats, as they can interrupt online classes, data access, and other educational services by flooding educational networks with illegitimate traffic. Existing signature-based DDoS detection mechanisms remain inadequate for Internet of Things (IoT) environments because of the dynamic nature of modern attacks and the resource limitations of connected devices. To address this challenge, this study proposed a hybrid deep learning model integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) units, attention mechanisms, and incremental learning to enhance DDoS detection and prevention in Educational Internet of Things (EIoT) networks. The CNN layers were used to extract spatial features from network traffic, while the LSTM units captured temporal patterns and the attention mechanisms identified relevant traffic behavior. The incremental learning component employed geometric metrics to enable the model to capture previously unknown attack patterns. Experimental evaluation using the CIC-IoT2023 and BoT-IoT datasets showed that the proposed model achieved 99% detection accuracy for both known and emerging attacks in EIoT scenarios, supported by real-time traffic filtering and Software-Defined Networking (SDN)-based mitigation as prevention strategies. The model demonstrated strong adaptability, reduced false positives, and advanced AI-hybridized capabilities for DDoS detection and prevention in EIoT environments. These findings indicate that the proposed model is effective for detecting and preventing DDoS attacks against EIoT infrastructures and contributes to the development of adaptive cybersecurity solutions for technology-enhanced educational systems.
Keywords: DDoS; IoT security; deep learning; CNN; education; cybersecurity; traffic filtering

title: HIDDEN THREATS OF THE DIGITAL WORLD: WHY IS CYBERCRIME INCREASING RAPIDLY?
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: digital threats; cybersecurity measures; Uzbekistan
abstract: This article provides an in-depth analysis of the sharp rise in cybercrime amid the rapid development of digital technologies and the internet. It examines the causes, main forms, global and national statistics, and consequences of cybercrime. Particular attention is paid to artificial intelligence (AI), ransomware, phishing, deepfakes, and other threats. The paper highlights the 11-fold increase in cybercrimes in Uzbekistan, trillions of soums in losses to citizens, and state measures. Practical recommendations for strengthening cybersecurity are offered.
Keywords: cybercrime; AI; ransomware; phishing; deepfakes; cybersecurity

title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND SOCIETY: OPPORTUNITIES, RISKS, AND TRANSFORMATIVE IMPACTS ON ECONOMY, EDUCATION, AND HUMAN BEHAVIOUR
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: AI; Society; Transformation; Economic Impact; Cybersecurity Risks; Ethics
abstract: Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a transformative force influencing economic systems, governance, healthcare, education, defense, and human behaviour in the twenty-first century. This paper critically examines the growing expansion of AI technologies with particular emphasis on India’s evolving technological landscape. The establishment of OpenAI’s operational presence in India reflects the country’s increasing strategic importance within the global AI ecosystem. The study explores AI’s contributions to economic growth, robotics, medical innovation, and digital entrepreneurship, while also analyzing emerging risks such as misinformation, cybersecurity threats, predictive warfare, labour displacement, and youth vulnerability. Special attention is given to AI’s impact on education, where intelligent tutoring systems and generative tools are reshaping learning practices, yet raising concerns about cognitive dependency. Scholarly perspectives from Daniel Oppenheimer, Shiri Melumad, Geoffrey Hinton, Peter Kirchschlager, and Yuval Noah Harari are incorporated to provide a balanced analytical framework. The paper argues that while AI presents unprecedented opportunities for productivity and innovation, its long-term societal implications depend on ethical governance, regulatory foresight, and responsible technological deployment. Sustainable integration of AI requires a human-centered approach that harmonizes technological advancement with democratic accountability and social responsibility. Keywords: Artificial Intelligence, Digital Economy, Robotics, AI in Education, Cybersecurity, Ethical Governance.
Keywords: Artificial Intelligence; Digital Economy; Robotics; AI in Education; Cybersecurity; Ethical Governance

title: A Web Security Framework Integrating OTP- Based Multi-Factor Authentication and Cyber Awareness
authors: Ruhee .; Prakash O. S.; Girish Kumar D.
venue: International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology (IJISRT)
Tags: Authentication; Cybersecurity; Web Applications; MFA; User Awareness
abstract: Web applications increasingly handle sensitive personal and organisational information, so security of user authentication essential. Nowadays traditional password methods sare still common, but they are vulnerable to threats like password reuse, brute force attacks, & phishing. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) offers better access control. Thus however, many systems overlook the human factor, which plays significant role in security incidents. This paper describes the design and implementation of a one time based multi-factor authentication system that includes a cyber awareness chatbot, voice assistant and spam checker. The proposed system improves authentication by combining password validation with email-based one-time password (OTP). It also educates users about common cybersecurity risks through an interactive chatbot. Using the Flask web framework and a MySQL database, the system's experimental evaluation shows stronger resistance to unauthorised access and increased user awareness. This suggests that merging authentication with education can create the more effective security for web applications.
Keywords: Web Security; Multi-Factor Authentication; Cyber Awareness; User Education; One-Time Password

title: Strengthening Data Security Systems in the Cyber Notary to Ensure Legal Certainty
venue: Nusantara: Journal of Law and Islamic Law
Tags: Indonesia; Data Protection; Cybercrime; Electronic Transactions; Notary Law
abstract: The implementation of cyber notary practices in Indonesia currently encounters profound regulatory fragmentation among the Law on Notary Public Office, Electronic Information and Transactions Law, and Personal Data Protection Law. This fundamental normative vacuum triggers significant legal uncertainty in securing clients' electronic data. This normative legal research aims to comprehensively analyze the interconnection of positive legal instruments regarding privacy protection within the digital notarial ecosystem. Utilizing statutory, conceptual, and comparative approaches, this study critically evaluates the obligations of notaries who now transform into personal data controllers bearing absolute liability. The research findings demonstrate that the absence of uniform information security standards substantially elevates the risks of system hacking and digital identity breaches. Therefore, this research concludes the urgency for regulatory harmonization that strictly mandates the implementation of privacy by design principles alongside the standardization of encrypted information security systems. This legal measure constitutes an imperative directive to mitigate cybercrime threats.
Keywords: Data Security; Cyber Notary; Legal Certainty; Privacy Protection; Regulatory Harmonization

title: From Traditional to Digital: Assessing the Impact and Adoption Challenges of Digital Accounting Software among Business Entities
venue: Journal of Economics, Business, and Commerce
Tags: digital transformation; accounting software; business impact; Santiago City; Philippines
abstract: This study focused on the migration of the conventional accounting systems to digital accounting systems of corporate business entities based in Santiago City, Philippines. The city is witnessing tremendous economic expansion but uneven levels of technological proficiency. This study is anchored with Resource-Based Theory and Institutional Theory, which frame the adoption of digital accounting software as a function of both internal resource capabilities and external institutional pressures. The aim of this study was to evaluate the impact of digital accounting software in terms of operational efficiency, financial reporting, and compliance, and to determine the main obstacles faced during the transition phase. The study was conducted utilizing a quantitative research design using a self-developed questionnaire, which was tested for dependability. The study was carried out on 132 respondents holding managerial and staff positions in accounting and audit departments of 50 corporate business entities. The results demonstrate that the use of digital accounting software is crucial in terms of operational efficiency. The importance of the influence on financial reporting and compliance was seen as moderate, with a weighted mean of 3.41 and 3.36, respectively, and respondents highlighted improvements in a number of areas. Challenges such as high ICT costs, lack of technical capabilities, and cybersecurity risks have been noted. The findings show that the benefits of digital accounting software are ultimately restricted by variations in organizational resources and workforce skills.
Keywords: digital accounting; ICT challenges; operational efficiency; financial reporting; cybersecurity risks

title: Assessing Crime Disclosure Patterns in a Large-Scale Cybercrime Forum
authors: Raphael Hoheisel; Tom Meurs; Jai Wientjes; Marianne Junger; Abhishta Abhishta; Masarah Paquet-Clouston
venue: Proceedings of the 18th ACM Web Science Conference 2026
Tags: cybercrime; online communities; law enforcement; text analysis
abstract: Cybercrime forums play a central role in the cybercrime ecosystem, serving as hubs for the exchange of illicit goods, services, and knowledge. Previous studies have explored the market and social structures of these forums, but less is known about the behavioral dynamics of users, particularly regarding participants’ disclosure of criminal activity. This study provides the first large-scale assessment of crime disclosure patterns in a major cybercrime forum, analysing over 3.5 million posts from nearly 300k users. Using a three-level classification scheme (benign, grey, and crime) and a scalable labelling pipeline powered by large language models (LLMs), we measure the level of crime disclosure present in initial posts, analyse how participants switch between levels, and assess how crime disclosure behavior relates to private communications. Our results show that crime disclosure is relatively normative: one quarter of initial posts include explicit crime-related content, and more than one third of users disclose criminal activity at least once in their initial posts. At the same time, most participants show restraint, with over two-thirds posting only benign or grey content and typically escalating disclosure gradually. Grey initial posts are particularly prominent, indicating that many users avoid overt statements and instead anchor their activity in ambiguous content. The study highlights the value of LLM-based text classification and Markov chain modelling for capturing crime disclosure patterns, offering insights for law enforcement efforts aimed at distinguishing benign, grey, and criminal content in cybercrime forums.
Keywords: cybercrime forums; crime disclosure patterns; LLM-based classification; user behavior; grey content

title: AI Cybercrime Detection, Digital Forensics, and Governance: Scoping Review with ☸️SAIMSARA
Tags: AI in Cybersecurity; Digital Forensics; Cybercrime Governance; Automated Detection; Evidence Handling
abstract: To map and synthesize original studies addressing AI cybercrime, with emphasis on how AI is used to enable cybercrime, detect or investigate cybercrime, preserve digital evidence, support victims and law enforcement, and shape governance, adoption, and real-world risk. The review uses 162 references and builds its evidence map from 183 original studies with 61140123 total participants/sample observations (topic-deduplicated ΣN). This scoping review suggests that AI cybercrime research is dominated by a dual-use dynamic in which AI simultaneously accelerates offending and strengthens defense, with AI-powered attacks reportedly surging 238% while defensive systems achieve high study-level accuracies across phishing, fraud, and forensic tasks. The most recurrent signal indicates that AI is effective for automating detection and forensic triage, including reductions in manual investigation time and improved evidence handling through blockchain-backed integrity mechanisms. However, the evidence highlights that performance claims rest on non-comparable datasets and limited adversarial testing, so trustworthy deployment will require explainable, legally admissible, and adversarially robust systems integrated with human oversight. Practically, this supports prioritizing XAI-enabled forensic and fraud-detection tools embedded in governed workflows rather than autonomous adjudication. Future research should establish shared benchmarks and prospectively validate AI forensic outputs against courtroom admissibility and evolving offender adaptation.
Keywords: AI; Cybercrime Detection; Digital Forensics; Governance; Evidence Preservation

title: The Rise of AI-Driven Cybercrime
venue: Advances in Computational Intelligence and Robotics
OSINT_Source_Types: Dark Web; Public Records; Forums & Communities
Tags: cybersecurity; AI; threat analysis; Incident Response
abstract: This chapter examines the emergence of AI-driven cybercrime and its transformation of the digital threat landscape. It traces the evolution from traditional, manually executed cyberattacks to autonomous and semi-autonomous systems powered by machine learning, deep learning, generative adversarial networks, and reinforcement learning. By distinguishing between AI-enhanced and AI-generated crimes, the chapter clarifies key conceptual and legal boundaries shaping contemporary threat analysis. It explores predictive cyberattacks, automated phishing, synthetic media manipulation, and autonomous malware evolution within Crime-as-a-Service ecosystems operating across dark web markets. The discussion highlights the dual-use dilemma, where defensive AI innovations are rapidly repurposed for offensive exploitation. Attention is given to attribution challenges, evidentiary complexities, and jurisdictional fragmentation that complicate investigation and prosecution. The chapter concludes by emphasizing the need for proactive detection systems, adaptive incident response protocols.
Keywords: AI-driven cybercrime; machine learning; predictive cyberattacks; automated phishing; synthetic media manipulation

title: Understanding Silence: A Sociological Exploration of the Challenges Faced by Elderly Cybercrime Victims in Approaching Formal Reporting Channels in India
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
Tags: sociology; cybersecurity; victimology; digital gerontology
abstract: The Indian society is digitizing at a rapid pace. This development has resulted in new opportunities for senior citizens to utilize technology but, at the same time, has also exposed them to severe cybercrime threats. This paper examines the socio-cultural barriers that prevent senior Indians from coming forward to report their experiences of victimization due to hacking. Previous studies have already pointed out the knowledge gaps, decreased intentions to report, inefficiencies in the reporting system, and vulnerabilities intersecting with digital literacy, gender, and socio-economic factors. We have discovered that senior citizens are struggling to navigate digital environments because of insufficient awareness about online cyber threats and risky online practices, which are further worsened by the hindrances created by institutions to make complaint filing easier. The significance of this paper is threefold: it belongs to the growing literature on digital gerontology and the victimology of cybercrime by highlighting an impediment in the Indian socio-cultural setting. The findings suggest that the need for immediate action to safeguard this vulnerable group in a rapidly digitizing environment is imperative.
Keywords: elderly victims; cybercrime; digital literacy; India; reporting challenges

title: Tracing the Digital Trail
venue: Advances in Computational Intelligence and Robotics
Tags: cyberspace; digital trail; law enforcement; tech advancement
abstract: Cybercrime investigation have historically been viewed as complex, both by practitioners and academics. These investigations oftentimes require collaboration across local, state, and federal agencies. Subsequently, the jurisdictional boundaries and concept associated with the crime scene, physical and digital, have become blurred. Couched within this discussion is the technological advancement shared by both investigative agencies and criminal syndicates. Put simply, as the technology advances so should the techniques and legal interpretations. Therefore, this chapter examines the evolution of cybercrime investigations and legal practices associated with investigation and prosecution of cyber-based crimes.
Keywords: cybercrime; investigation; legal practices; technology; collaboration

title: Letter on ‘Altered Pathogen Spectrum of Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis in Patients Treated With Proton Pump Inhibitors’
Tags: Clinical Study; Microbiology; Antibiotic Resistance; Causation Analysis
abstract: The bicentric retrospective analysis by Kitchen and colleagues [1] reports that proton pump inhibitor (PPI) treatment is independently associated with gram-positive spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in cirrhotic patients (adjusted OR 2.72, 95% CI 1.34–5.51). Their clinical message—that empiric antibiotic choice should account for an enriched gram-positive spectrum in PPI users—is plausible and pragmatically useful. How strongly that inference holds, however, depends on residual confounding the present design does not fully address. First, prior hospitalisation within 90 days was the most strongly imbalanced baseline variable (82.5% PPI vs. 62.0% non-PPI, p = 0.002, table 1). This covariate was not retained in the multivariable model; only the binary nosocomial-SBP indicator was. Recent healthcare exposure is among the strongest documented drivers of gram-positive—particularly Enterococcus and Staphylococcus—colonisation in cirrhotic patients [2]. The nosocomial-SBP variable captures only the index admission, not cumulative 90-day exposure to inpatient antibiotics, indwelling devices or contact-precaution organisms. A model including both nosocomial status and recent hospitalisation would more honestly partition the variance currently attributed to PPI. Second, the 18-year inclusion window (January 2007–July 2025) spans an era of substantial change in both PPI prescribing intensity and SBP microbial epidemiology. Independent of PPI use, large series have documented a secular drift from gram-negative toward gram-positive SBP [3, 4]. If PPI prescription increased over the study period while gram-positive SBP increased for unrelated reasons, the cross-sectional association is partly an era effect. The supplementary stratified analysis (figure S1) acknowledges this but does not enter calendar year or, more appropriately, a pre/post midpoint indicator as a covariate in the principal regression. Third, the non-PPI group is unusual. Only 50 of 227 patients (22%) a proportion well below the published prevalence of PPI use among hospitalised cirrhotics [5] comprise it, and they had numerically worse liver function (Child-Pugh 11 (11–12) vs. 11 (10–11), bilirubin 5.3 vs. 3.0 mg/dL) yet less prior healthcare contact. This pattern is compatible with channelling bias: PPI discontinuation in end-stage disease (palliation, polypharmacy review) may select patients who differ from PPI users in unobserved ways relevant to the gut microbiome. Fourth, the external validation cohort (n = 24 PPI, n = 6 non-PPI) carries insufficient information to validate the primary finding. The single non-PPI patient with gram-positive SBP (1/6 = 16.7%) sits within an exact binomial 95% confidence interval of approximately 0.4% to 64.1%. Presenting this as supporting evidence inflates the apparent reproducibility of the result. The clinical recommendation heightened gram-positive coverage when starting empiric therapy in PPI-treated cirrhotic patients has plausible microbiological grounds and may prove correct. Confirming it as causal, however, will require prospective registry data with cumulative healthcare-exposure metrics, era stratification and a non-PPI denominator larger than 50. Yours sincerely, Maajid Mohi Ud Din Malik Maajid Mohi Ud Din Malik: supervision, formal analysis, software, writing – review and editing, writing – original draft, investigation, conceptualization, methodology, validation, visualization. The author has nothing to report. The author declares no conflicts of interest. This article is linked to Kitchen et al. papers. To view this article, visit https://doi.org/10.1111/apt.70593. Data sharing not applicable to this article as no datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.
Keywords: Proton Pump Inhibitors; Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis; Cirrhosis; Gram-positive Bacteria; Healthcare Exposure
affiliations: Dr. D.Y. Patil Vidyapeeth, Pune; D.Y. Patil University

title: Raoof128/Project-Simurgh: Stage 2.8C/D — Linux Wayland + XWayland + systemd + Ubuntu CI
Tags: Linux Security; Cybersecurity Audit; Open Source; Wayland Protocol; Software Development
abstract: Combined PR #21 + #22 — ships the remainder of Stage 2.8: Wayland portal probing, XWayland partial coverage, browser packaging UX hint, live `display_server_mismatch` enforcement, systemd `--user` lifecycle, Ubuntu CI Rust + mandatory Xvfb + shellcheck. What ships Phase A — Live `display_server_mismatch` enforcement Closes the v0.4.15 P0 follow-up. `createDisplayServerLock` wired into `/api/telemetry`. Gated strictly on `platform === "linux"`. Counters not advanced on rejection. `DAEMON_PROOF_REJECTED` emitted to HMAC audit chain. Bonus regression: mid-session mismatch does not consume telemetry sequence. Phase B — Wayland portal advertised vs active (no consent) `zbus` 4.4.0. New `LinuxScannerSummary` type. `scanner/wayland.rs` uses `NameHasOwner` + `GetProperty<AvailableSourceTypes>` — property read only. Banned-method grep test enforces consent safety (no `CreateSession` / `SelectSources` / `Start` / `OpenPipeWireRemote`). Phase C — XWayland partial coverage `scanner/xwayland.rs` reuses X11 path, maps to `coverage=xwayland_partial` and `xwayland_window_count`. `CurrentScan` renamed to `LinuxScannerSnapshot`. `/status` + signed `/proof` both dispatch on `display_server`. `xwayland_window_count` + `portal_*` flow through the signed proof body. Phase D — `browser_package_hint` UX-only SDK `getDeviceShieldStatus()` returns `browser_package_hint` (default `"unknown"`) + soft `daemon_unreachable_hint`. 8 trust-boundary tests enforce that 5 server-side modules never reference the field. Phase E — systemd `--user` lifecycle `simurgh-daemon-linux.service` (user-scope, hardening directives). Install / uninstall / check / doctor scripts with `--check` + `--dry-run`. No sudo, no eval, no curl-pipe-sh. Phase F — Ubuntu CI Rust + mandatory Xvfb + shellcheck `SIMURGH_REQUIRE_XVFB_TESTS=1` env-var enforcement. Workflow extended with apt deps, Rust stable, Cargo cache, shellcheck, fmt/clippy/test. Timeout 10→20 min. Also fixed a parallel-test race in `proof_endpoint_tests.rs` (three `#[tokio::test]` threads racing over `XDG_STATE_HOME`; serialised via `OnceLock<Mutex<()>>`). Phase G — Combined smoke (16 scenarios A–P) X11, Wayland states, XWayland, browser hint, display lock, non-local DISPLAY, headless, systemd lifecycle, report shape, audit chain. Phase H — Combined cybersecurity audit (16 dimensions, 30 assertions) Wayland consent-safety banned-method grep, XWayland partial-only claim, browser hint 5-source perimeter, systemd hygiene, CI Rust + Xvfb enforcement, recursive raw-field rejection, report/dashboard wording, HMAC audit chain integration, no-overclaim wording. Caught one wording violation during the audit (fixed in the same commit). Phase I–J — Evidence rules + docs + gates `docs/evidence/stage-2-linux/README.md`. Light README/ROADMAP/SECURITY/PRIVACY/AGENT/CHANGELOG. `scripts/check.sh` wired for 5 new gate rows. Verification Node: 327 / 327 pass (43 suites) Rust: 33 / 33 pass (with `SIMURGH_REQUIRE_XVFB_TESTS=1`) `npm audit --audit-level=high`: 0 vulnerabilities `node tools/privacy-audit.mjs`: pass `cargo fmt --check` / `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` / `cargo test`: clean Stage 2.7 smoke + Stage 2.6/2.7 closeout audit: green Stage 2.8A/B smoke + audit: green Stage 2.8C/D smoke (16 scenarios) + cybersecurity audit (30 assertions): green `scripts/check.sh`: 52/52 PASS GitHub Actions Quality Gate: both runs green (2m44s + 3m16s with Cargo cache) Non-claims preserved Research prototype only. No production Linux endpoint deployment, no distro packaging, no system-wide service deployment, no MDM, no hardware attestation, no kernel-level visibility, no universal Wayland surface enumeration, no GPU overlay detection, no automatic misconduct detection.
