title: FRAUD NOTIFICATION DETECTOR TECHNOLOGY ON NEURAL NETWORK MODEL
authors: Larisa Cherckesova; Olga Safaryan; Olga Buryakova; Dmitriy Medvedev
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; News Media; Forums & Communities; Other
Tags: cybersecurity; fraud prevention; machine learning; neural networks
abstract: This article is devoted to a comprehensive analysis of modern threats and vulnerabilities associated with text fraud in the digital environment. Both traditional rule-based signature methods and modern machine learning approaches, including zero-shot classification and hybrid architectures, are considered. The practices of social engineering, phishing, and financial fraud are analyzed to identify key patterns and markers of attackers. A lightweight system has been developed that combines the speed of rules and the semantic depth of neural network models, which has improved the detection efficiency. An important element was the risk assessment methodology, which took into account the content of messages and the context, which reduced the number of false positives to 3%, while maintaining high accuracy. The system architecture is implemented with cascade processing, which ensures optimal use of computing resources. Of particular importance is the mechanism of adaptive redistribution of weights between the components of the system, which allows you to maintain the quality of work with limited resources. A fallback classifier has been implemented to guarantee fault tolerance, maintaining accuracy in the range of 85-90%. The study highlights the need for a multi-layered approach that combines signature detection and semantic analysis. Prospects for the development of the system include expanding the rules, integrating with reputation systems, training to adapt to new fraudulent tactics, as well as creating a distributed infrastructure. Continued research in this area is important to combat evolving threats and create effective commercial solutions to protect against text fraud.
Keywords: text fraud; neural networks; phishing; fraud detection; machine learning

title: GenAI-Driven Threat Detection with Microsoft Security Copilot
Tags: Threat Hunting; AI; Machine Learning; Cybersecurity; Incident Response
abstract: Defending against today's increasingly sophisticated cyberattacks requires security analysts to continuously translate evolving attacker tradecraft into detection logic. This places defenders in a reactive posture, requiring constantly updated expertise across an increasingly fragmented security landscape. We introduce the Dynamic Threat Detection Agent (DTDA), an always-on adaptive agent that continuously investigates security incidents across Microsoft Defender to uncover hidden threats and generate explainable detections when attack-story gaps are found. DTDA combines: (1) a unified activity timeline spanning alerts, events, user and entity behavior analytics, and threat intelligence; (2) versioned LLM prompt contracts with schema validation, grounding requirements, bounded retries, and fail-closed suppression; (3) a planner-executor investigation loop that generates attack-specific hypotheses and gathers supporting and refuting evidence; and (4) dynamic alert generation with a context-relevant title, severity, MITRE mappings, remediation guidance, implicated entities, and natural-language attack description. Integrated into Microsoft Security Copilot and deployed across tens of thousands of Defender customers, DTDA operates continuously at industry scale. In a 120-day online evaluation, DTDA achieves 80.1% precision from customer feedback while generating novel alerts for approximately 15% of investigated incidents. In offline evaluation, DTDA recovers hidden malicious activity with 0.78 F1 using GPT-5.4, improving over GPT-4.1 by 0.12 F1 and outperforming the baseline by 0.26 F1 points. Operationally, DTDA processes single-incident investigations end-to-end in a median of 28 minutes at a median token cost of USD 2.04, with a 0.38% job-level failure rate. These results demonstrate that autonomous agents can identify missed malicious activity at a production scale.
Keywords: Threat Detection; Cybersecurity; Generative AI; Microsoft Defender; Dynamic Threat Detection Agent; Incident Response; Machine Learning

title: Market Tragedy and Sustainability of Small Businesses in Lagos State, Nigeria
venue: International Journal of Applied Research in Business and Management
OSINT_Source_Types: News Media; Government Data; Public Records
Tags: economic vulnerability; disaster preparedness; infrastructure resilience; environmental impact
abstract: The study examines the impact of recurring market tragedies, fire outbreaks, and building collapses on the sustainability of small businesses in Lagos, Nigeria. The study employs a qualitative research methodology grounded in Contingency Theory. Secondary data sources, including scholarly literature, media reports, and government documents, are analysed comprehensively. The study illustrates the complex challenges faced by small enterprises operating within global urban ecosystems through a series of case studies drawn from local and international media coverage. It was found that the high frequency and widespread impact of fire incidents and related disasters significantly disrupt business continuity, resulting in substantial financial losses, which signals a pattern of repeated shocks to market-dependent livelihoods and long-term economic instability for small-scale entrepreneurs. Evaluating these critical issues, this research contributes to the discourse on urban-market challenges and small business sustainability in developing economies, thereby deepening economic vulnerability and providing social safety nets for affected traders. The analysis uncovers deficiencies in urban planning, lax enforcement of building regulations, and inadequate emergency response mechanisms as critical factors contributing to these market tragedies. Severe environmental consequences, including air and water pollution, soil contamination, and the release of toxic substances, are identified. These consequences increase health risks and ecological damage in affected areas. The study recommends enhancing infrastructure resilience, enforcing stricter building codes, and improving disaster preparedness, particularly for the focus city of Lagos, Nigeria.
Keywords: market tragedies; sustainability; small businesses; Lagos; fire outbreaks; urban planning

title: A Systematic Literature Review on Machine Learning for Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Ali Ahmed; Ramy Mostafa; Mahmoud H. Qutqut; Noha Ragab
Tags: Cybersecurity; Intrusion Detection; Machine Learning; Literature Review
abstract: The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in cybersecurity, especially for creating Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), has become increasingly important. These systems are essential for detecting malicious behaviour, identifying network issues, and stopping cyberattacks in real time. Although extensive research has been conducted on various ML and Deep Learning (DL) models for IDS, the current literature remains incomplete. It has many different datasets, methods, and evaluation standards. As cyber threats become more advanced, it is crucial to conduct a thorough analysis of ML techniques for intrusion detection. The goal of this Systematic Literature Review (SLR) is to give a full picture of the most recent academic articles on ML-based IDS. The study addresses important research questions about the most widely used algorithms, the types of attacks and network environments covered, the methodological problems that remain unsolved, and the new trends that should shape future research. Following the PRISMA framework, we conducted a systematic review of peer-reviewed articles published between January 2022 and May 2025. We searched IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, and SpringerLink, yielding 22,558 initial records. After carefully applying strict inclusion criteria, 125 papers were selected for the final analysis. We created a standardised data extraction form (i.e., using MS Excel) to gather bibliographic details, research emphasis, methodological strategies, datasets, evaluation criteria, and recognised constraints. We employed thematic analysis to develop a clear taxonomy. We identified five main research themes in our analysis: (1) ensemble and hybrid learning pipelines focused on performance optimisation (30 papers), (2) context-specific IDS designs for Internet of Things (IoT), cloud, and Software-Defined Networking (SDN) environments (34 papers), (3) data-centric engineering that deals with class imbalance and feature selection (20 papers), (4) deep neural architectures for representation learning (31 papers), and (5) trustworthiness concerns like adversarial robustness, zero-day detection, and Explainable AI (XAI) (10 papers). Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Random Forests are the most commonly used algorithms, often combined. Nonetheless, significant deficiencies remain: about 2% of papers incorporate XAI, only 4% focus on adversarial robustness, and none validate their models in real-world production settings. Denial of Service (DoS) and Distributed DoS (DDoS) are the most common types of attacks in the literature, while Web attacks, ransomware, and advanced persistent threats remain poorly studied. The number of publications grows at an average of 30.2% annually, but the field still relies on legacy benchmark datasets rather than operational validation.
Keywords: Machine Learning; Intrusion Detection Systems; Cybersecurity; Systematic Literature Review; AI; Deep Learning

title: An innovative hybrid ANN inception model for detecting and preventing man-in-the-middle attacks in smart networks for shielding sensitive data
Tags: cyber threats; network security; machine learning; data integrity; smart home security
abstract: Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks represent a significant cybersecurity challenge, particularly within the rapidly growing domain of smart networks and Internet of Things (IoT) environments. These attacks involve an adversary intercepting and potentially altering the communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly with each other. As the deployment of smart devices and autonomous systems increases, the risk of MitM attacks escalates, given the sensitive nature of the data being transmitted and the critical functions these systems perform. This paper presents a novel approach to detecting and preventing MitM attacks by leveraging a Hybrid ANN-Inception Model (HAINet). The proposed model integrates the dynamic feature extraction capabilities of Inception modules with the flexibility of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) to identify and mitigate these sophisticated cyber threats. Using the Smart Home Intrusion Detection Dataset from Kaggle, the methodology involves advanced data preprocessing, feature extraction via AutoEncoders, and optimization through hybrid learning algorithms. The HAINet model executed in Jupyter Notebook and achieved an outstanding accuracy of 98.69%, outperforming traditional models in both detection accuracy and robustness across various network conditions. The results demonstrate the model's efficacy in shielding sensitive data from interception and manipulation, making it a valuable tool for enhancing security in smart networks.
Keywords: MitM attacks; smart networks; Artificial Neural Networks; HAInet; data protection; cybersecurity; IoT
affiliations: Anna University, Chennai; G.B. Pant Institute of Himalayan Environment and Development

title: INTEGRATING DECEPTION MECHANISMS AND MEMORY ANALYSIS IN EARLY-STAGE RANSOMWARE FORENSICS: A COMPREHENSIVE REVIEW
authors: Shivaji Patingrao Patil; Kamalesh V.N.; Kalyan Devappa Bamane
venue: International Journal of Drug Delivery Technology
Tags: ransomware; forensics; deception techniques; memory analysis; cyber threats; healthcare security
abstract: In this paper we tackle this problem by exploring the utilization of deceptive tactics alongside memory analysis in early-stage ransomware forensics to combat this problem of detection of advanced ransomware attacks that evade regular security systems. Then we go on to present a systematic study of existing ransomware behaviors and forensic techniques, focusing on deception technologies. You analyze through the deception mechanisms because you can detect ransomware and have a very large or very small number of different campaigns being impacted, and it can control the environment in which the victim is operating, which indicates that it increases the probability of detection and mitigation via deception. Also, through a combination of deception techniques and memory analysis, the early detection of malicious activities will facilitate the optimization of response time at a threat situation and reduce risks. At the core of the study is that the proposed tactic might have an application within the healthcare environment, and while ransomware attacks are increasingly becoming a major threat to the healthcare sector, as they can access sensitive patient data and vital healthcare organisations’ activities, organizations will benefit by correlating the techniques of deception with forensic analysis to protect from potential information breaches. This work lays out a systematic framework for advanced forensics to be utilized in ransomware protection, and it indicates the direction of future research about cybersecurity and digital forensics.
Keywords: ransomware; forensics; deception mechanisms; memory analysis; cybersecurity; healthcare
affiliations: Gujarat University; Central University of Gujarat; D.Y. Patil University

title: Autoencoder-Based Anomaly Detection for Cyber Security Using Unsupervised Deep Learning
venue: International Journal for Research in Applied Science and Engineering Technology
Tags: cyber threats; intrusion detection; network monitoring; anomaly detection framework
abstract: The increasing complexity and frequency of cyber security threats have created significant challenges for traditional intrusion detection and network monitoring systems. Conventional rule-based and signature-based detection mechanisms are often ineffective against evolving cyber attacks, zero-day exploits, and previously unseen malicious activities because they rely heavily on predefined attack signatures and manually crafted rules. In response to these limitations, anomaly detection using deep learning techniques has emerged as a promising approach for intelligent cyber threat identification. This research paper presents an autoencoder-based anomaly detection framework for cyber security applications using unsupervised deep learning techniques. The proposed framework is designed to learn normal system behavior patterns from cyber activity data and identify anomalous events through reconstruction error analysis. The methodology includes data preprocessing, feature normalization, autoencoder model development, training, threshold-based anomaly classification, and performance evaluation. The autoencoder architecture consists of encoder and decoder layers capable of learning compact latent representations of normal cyber behavior. The model is trained primarily on normal system activity data using Mean Squared Error loss and Adam optimization. Experimental evaluation is conducted using standard classification metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrix analysis, and training-validation loss convergence behavior. The proposed anomaly detection framework achieved an overall classification accuracy of 83.85%, demonstrating strong capability in modeling legitimate cyber behavior while maintaining low false-positive rates. The results indicate that the autoencoder effectively captures underlying patterns of normal network activity and provides reliable anomaly detection performance suitable for real-world cyber security environments. The findings of this study highlight the practical significance of unsupervised deep learning approaches for scalable, adaptive, and intelligent cyber defense systems.
Keywords: autoencoder; anomaly detection; cyber security; unsupervised learning; deep learning

title: ENDOCRINE DISRUPTORS AND HORMONAL DYSREGULATION IN AQUATIC VERTEBRATES: MECHANISMS AND ECOTOXICOLOGICAL CONSEQUENCES
authors: Aisha Siddiqa; Khadeejat ul kubra Nangrejo; Kamran Khan; Muhammad Shahbaz Azhar; Muhammad Hashim
venue: Insights – Journal of Health and Rehabilitation
Tags: ecotoxicology; environmental monitoring; chemical pollutants; biodiversity; toxicology
abstract: Background: Endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) are emerging aquatic pollutants that interfere with normal hormonal regulation in vertebrates. These contaminants enter aquatic ecosystems mainly through industrial discharge, agricultural runoff, pharmaceutical residues, municipal wastewater effluents, and urban pollution. Objective: This review aims to summarize the major sources, mechanisms, and ecotoxicological consequences of EDC exposure in aquatic vertebrates, particularly fish and amphibians. Methods: This narrative review synthesizes current literature on endocrine disruption in aquatic vertebrates, with emphasis on contaminant classes, hormonal pathways, biological effects, biomarkers, adverse outcome pathways, and emerging molecular and computational tools used for endocrine toxicity assessment. Discussion: Evidence indicates that EDC exposure can cause reproductive dysfunction, feminization, intersex conditions, thyroid disruption, developmental abnormalities, altered behaviour, and reduced population sustainability. Amphibians are especially vulnerable because their metamorphosis is strongly dependent on thyroid hormone regulation. Key mechanisms include hormone receptor interference, altered steroidogenesis, oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, epigenetic modifications, and disruption of gene expression. Recent advances in molecular tools, adverse outcome pathway frameworks, zebrafish models, and artificial intelligence-based prediction systems have improved the detection and assessment of endocrine toxicity. Conclusion: EDCs pose a significant threat to aquatic vertebrates by disrupting reproductive, developmental, thyroid, behavioural, and ecological processes. Their long-term effects may contribute to biodiversity loss, population decline, and ecosystem instability. Improved environmental monitoring, wastewater treatment, regulatory control, and advanced toxicity assessment tools are essential to reduce the ecological risks associated with endocrine-disrupting pollutants. Keywords: Endocrine-disrupting chemicals; aquatic vertebrates; hormonal dysregulation; ecotoxicological effects; endocrine toxicity mechanisms; fish; amphibians.
Keywords: Endocrine-disrupting chemicals; aquatic vertebrates; hormonal dysregulation; ecotoxicological effects; endocrine toxicity mechanisms; fish; amphibians
affiliations: Jinnah Postgraduate Medical Center; University of Sindh; Southwest University; Pir Mehr Ali Shah Arid Agriculture University; King Abdulaziz University

title: Securing Mechatronic Control Modules in the Context of Current Cyber Threats
authors: Dănuț-Iulian STANCIU; Ioana PETCU; Vlad MANGHER
Tags: cybersecurity; mechatronics; threat landscape; engineering security; critical infrastructure
abstract: The increasing integration of connectivity, embedded intelligence, and remote management has turned classical mechatronic systems into complex cyber-physical systems (CPS).While this evolution enables greater efficiency, flexibility and autonomy, it also exposes mechatronic platforms to cyber threats capable of producing direct physical consequences.Unlike traditional IT systems, cyber-attacks on mechatronic systems can compromise control loops, manipulate sensor data, or induce unsafe actuator behavior.This article overviews the cybersecurity threats to modern mechatronic systems, presents the threat landscape specific to embedded control environments, and discusses engineering-oriented security mechanisms suitable for real-time and safety-critical applications.Additionally, we include a practical methodology for securing an integrated mechatronic control module of a concentrating solar system that is connected to the Internet and performs firmware updates over the network.At the same time, our study positions cyber security as a fundamental design constraint in contemporary mechatronics and highlights future research directions relevant to critical infrastructures.
Keywords: mechatronic systems; cyber-physical systems; cybersecurity threats; control modules; safety-critical applications; firmware updates

title: The decline of bumblebee populations in plant communities: global threats and pressure on pollinators
authors: Jolanta Bąk-Badowska; Anna Wojciechowska; Umer Ayyaz Alsm Sheikh; Joanna Czerwik-Marcinkowska
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: biodiversity; climate change; agriculture; land use; pollinators
abstract: Abstract Bumblebees (Bombus spp.) are crucial pollinators in temperate ecosystems, playing a pivotal role in enhancing crop yields and maintaining the diversity of wild plants. Despite their ecological and economic significance, bumblebee populations are experiencing severe declines across Europe, driven by factors such as climate change, pesticide exposure, habitat fragmentation, and intensified land use. This study investigates the dynamics of bumblebee populations in the Botanical Garden of Kielce, Poland, over a six-year period (2017–2022). Data from 26 identified species across four distinct plant communities were analyzed to assess spatial and temporal patterns of diversity and abundance. Results revealed significant variability in both species richness and abundance, with fresh meadow and xerothermic grassland habitats exhibiting the highest bumblebee diversity, while ruderal communities recorded the lowest. Temporal trends indicated an initial decline in diversity, followed by a notable recovery in later years. These findings highlight the potential of urban green spaces to serve as refuges for pollinators, though they remain vulnerable to anthropogenic pressures. Comparative analysis with regional and global trends of bumblebee decline underscores the fragility of this recovery, emphasizing the need for coordinated, landscape-scale conservation efforts. This study demonstrates that botanical gardens, when designed with ecological principles and structural diversity in mind, can play an important role in pollinator conservation. However, their success is highly dependent on active management and integration into broader ecological networks.
Keywords: bumblebee decline; pollinator conservation; habitat fragmentation; urban green spaces; ecosystem health

title: Evaluation of Updated Polytrauma Triage Criteria: Persistent Undertriage of Elderly and Female Patients
authors: Felix Alexander Julius Huber; Sebastian Weiss; Jannis Christoffel; Matthias Fröhlich
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: triage criteria; polytrauma; clinical study; emergency medicine; demographic analysis
abstract: Abstract Purpose Accurate trauma team activation (TTA) is critical for survival after severe injury, yet the effectiveness of the revised 2022 German S3 polytrauma triage criteria remains uncertain. This study evaluated triage accuracy in clinical practice, with particular focus on demographic disparities by age and sex, to determine whether the revised guideline improves identification of severely injured patients. Methods A retrospective single-center cohort study was conducted at a German Level I trauma center including all adult trauma patients admitted via emergency medical services between September 2024 and March 2025 (n = 2415). Triage accuracy was assessed using consensus-based post hoc criteria defining the need for TTA. Sensitivity-based measures were used to calculate undertriage and overtriage rates. Associations between triage allocation and demographic factors were analyzed using chi-square and nonparametric tests. Results TTA was performed in 122 patients (5.05%), of whom 69.67% were appropriately activated. Undertriage reached 31.46%, substantially exceeding recommended benchmarks, while overtriage was 30.33%. Male patients were significantly overrepresented among correctly triaged cases, whereas female patients were underrepresented. Patients aged ≥ 70 years were significantly less likely to receive TTA and were overrepresented among those without activation despite potential need. These findings demonstrate reduced accuracy of the updated criteria and identify elderly and female patients as groups at risk of misclassification. Conclusion Despite revision of the S3 guideline, clinically significant undertriage persists and disproportionately affects elderly and female trauma patients—groups at risk of being overlooked in emergency decision-making. The current triage algorithm shows insufficient accuracy for vulnerable populations, underscoring the need for age- and sex-adapted criteria to ensure equitable and accurate trauma team activation. However, a direct comparison of triage performance with previous reports was not feasible, as earlier studies defined undertriage using ISS ≥ 16, whereas our analysis was based on post hoc criteria.
Keywords: trauma triage; undertriage; demographic disparities; elderly patients; female patients

title: inTIME: A Machine Learning-Based Framework for Gathering and Leveraging Web Data to Cyber-Threat Intelligence
authors: Paris Koloveas; Thanasis Chantzios; Sofia Alevizopoulou; Spiros Skiadopoulos; Christos Tryfonopoulos
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; Dark Web; Forums & Communities; Leaked Data
Tags: cybersecurity; threat detection; data acquisition; open-source
abstract: In today’s world, technology has become deep-rooted and more accessible than ever over a plethora of different devices and platforms, ranging from company servers and commodity PCs to mobile phones and wearables, interconnecting a wide range of stakeholders such as households, organizations and critical infrastructures. The sheer volume and variety of the different operating systems, the device particularities, the various usage domains and the accessibility-ready nature of the platforms creates a vast and complex threat landscape that is difficult to contain. Staying on top of these evolving cyber-threats has become an increasingly difficult task that presently relies heavily on collecting and utilising cyber-threat intelligence before an attack (or at least shortly after, to minimize the damage) and entails the collection, analysis, leveraging and sharing of huge volumes of data. In this work, we put forward inTIME, a machine learning-based integrated framework that provides an holistic view in the cyber-threat intelligence process and allows security analysts to easily identify, collect, analyse, extract, integrate, and share cyber-threat intelligence from a wide variety of online sources including clear/deep/dark web sites, forums and marketplaces, popular social networks, trusted structured sources (e.g., known security databases), or other datastore types (e.g., pastebins). inTIME is a zero-administration, open-source, integrated framework that enables security analysts and security stakeholders to (i) easily deploy a wide variety of data acquisition services (such as focused web crawlers, site scrapers, domain downloaders, social media monitors), (ii) automatically rank the collected content according to its potential to contain useful intelligence, (iii) identify and extract cyber-threat intelligence and security artifacts via automated natural language understanding processes, (iv) leverage the identified intelligence to actionable items by semi-automatic entity disambiguation, linkage and correlation, and (v) manage, share or collaborate on the stored intelligence via open standards and intuitive tools. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first solution in the literature to provide an end-to-end cyber-threat intelligence management platform that is able to support the complete threat lifecycle via an integrated, simple-to-use, yet extensible framework.
Keywords: machine learning; cyber-threat intelligence; data analysis; web data collection; integrated framework

title: The Significance of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Techniques in Cybersecurity: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Maad Mijwil; Israa Ezzat Salem; Marwa M. Ismaeel
venue: Iraqi Journal for Computer Science and Mathematics
Tags: Cybersecurity; AI; Machine Learning; Deep Learning; Data Privacy
abstract: People in the modern era spend most of their lives in virtual environments that offer a range of public and private services and social platforms. Therefore, these environments need to be protected from cyber attackers that can steal data or disrupt systems. Cybersecurity refers to a collection of technical, organizational, and executive means for preventing the unauthorized use or misuse of electronic information and communication systems to ensure the continuity of their work, guarantee the confidentiality and privacy of personal data, and protect consumers from threats and intrusions. Accordingly, this article explores the cybersecurity practices that protect computer systems from attacks, hacking, and data thefts and investigates the role of artificial intelligence in this domain. This article also summarizes the most significant literature that explore the roles and effects of machine learning and deep learning techniques in cybersecurity. Results show that machine learning and deep learning techniques play significant roles in protecting computer systems from unauthorized entry and in controlling system penetration by predicting and understanding the behaviour and traffic of malicious software.
Keywords: Machine Learning; Deep Learning; Cybersecurity; Data Protection; Artificial Intelligence

title: Use and Abuse of Personal Information, Part I: Design of a Scalable OSINT Collection Engine
authors: Elliott Rheault; Mary Nerayo; Jaden Leonard; Jack Kolenbrander; Christopher Henshaw; Madison Boswell; Alan J. Michaels
OSINT_Source_Types: Social Media; Public Records; Forums & Communities; Other
Tags: Open Source Intelligence; Privacy; Social Science
abstract: In most open-source intelligence (OSINT) research efforts, the collection of information is performed in an entirely passive manner as an observer to third-party communication streams. This paper describes ongoing work that seeks to insert itself into that communication loop, fusing openly available data with requested content that is representative of what is sent to second parties. The mechanism for performing this is based on the sharing of falsified personal information through one-time online transactions that facilitate signup for newsletters, establish online accounts, or otherwise interact with resources on the Internet. The work has resulted in the real-time Use and Abuse of Personal Information OSINT collection engine that can ingest email, SMS text, and voicemail content at an enterprise scale. Foundations of this OSINT collection infrastructure are also laid to incorporate an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven interaction engine that shifts collection from a passive process to one that can effectively engage with different classes of content for improved real-world privacy experimentation and quantitative social science research.
Keywords: OSINT; data collection; personal information; real-time engine; AI-driven

title: Cybersecurity Risk
authors: Chris Florackis; Christodoulos Louca; Roni Michaely; Michael Weber
Tags: financial studies; cybersecurity; risk analysis
abstract: Abstract Based on textual analysis and a comparison of cybersecurity risk disclosures of firms that were hacked to others that were not, we propose a novel firm-level measure of cybersecurity risk for all U.S.-listed firms. We then examine whether cybersecurity risk is priced in the cross-section of stock returns. Portfolios of firms with high exposure to cybersecurity risk outperform other firms, on average, by up to 8.3$\%$ per year. Yet, high-exposure firms perform poorly in periods of high cybersecurity risk. Reassuringly, the measure is higher in information-technology industries, correlates with characteristics linked to firms hit by cyberattacks, and predicts future cyberattacks. Authors have furnished an Internet Appendix, which is available on the Oxford University Press Web site next to the link to the final published paper online
Keywords: cybersecurity risk; stock returns; firm-level measure; cyberattacks; information technology

title: Conceptualizing Cybercrime: Definitions, Typologies and Taxonomies
authors: Kirsty Phillips; Julia C. Davidson; Ruby R. Farr; Christine Burkhardt; Stefano Caneppele; Mary P. Aiken
Tags: cybersecurity; forensic science; literature review; policy recommendations
abstract: Cybercrime is becoming ever more pervasive and yet the lack of consensus surrounding what constitutes a cybercrime has a significant impact on society, legal and policy response, and academic research. Difficulties in understanding cybercrime begin with the variability in terminology and lack of consistency in cybercrime legislation across jurisdictions. In this review, using a structured literature review methodology, key cybercrime definitions, typologies and taxonomies were identified across a range of academic and non-academic (grey literature) sources. The findings of this review were consolidated and presented in the form of a new classification framework to understand cybercrime and cyberdeviance. Existing definitions, typologies and taxonomies were evaluated, and key challenges were identified. Whilst conceptualizing cybercrime will likely remain a challenge, this review provides recommendations for future work to advance towards a universal understanding of cybercrime phenomena as well as a robust and comprehensive classification system.
Keywords: cybercrime; typologies; taxonomies; classification framework; legal implications

title: Working Towards a Security Classification Framework for Research
venue: The Routledge International Handbook of Research Security
Tags: research security; information sharing; academic collaboration; public safety
abstract: The public debate surrounding security in academic research has intensified in recent years, marked by a complex interplay of perspectives. Advocates emphasise the importance of open collaboration and the free exchange of ideas in the academy. The argument is that stringent security measures may hinder scientific progress and innovation. They highlight the historical achievements made possible by unfettered academic inquiry. Conversely, critics caution against potential risks, citing instances of sensitive information falling into the wrong hands and being exploited for malicious purposes. They stress implementing robust security protocols to safeguard intellectual property, prevent espionage, and mitigate the threat of dual-use technologies. A framework for assessing the security classification of research needs to be added to this discourse, especially when, for all intents and purposes, the research in question involves unclassified data. Governments employ national security classification systems to safeguard sensitive information. In the Australian context, these classifications range from unclassified to top secret, depending on the information’s sensitivity and the potential impact of unauthorised release on national security. Access to classified information in government is strictly controlled and requires appropriate clearance levels to ensure the trustworthiness of those handling and using classified information. The system protects critical data and intelligence vital to the nation’s security interests. However, as illustrated by the US government’s National Commission on Terrorist Attacks Upon the United States report on the September 11 terror attacks, this system often unintentionally inhibits the timely sharing of information and knowledge. No such classification system exists in academia, and understanding of the national security impacts of research disclosure is limited. Often, researchers do not understand how aggregation and contextualisation can see the public or covert release of open-source information inadvertently result in national security impacts. Open-source academic research and the development of dual-use technologies present complex national security implications. On one hand, open access to academic research fosters collaboration, innovation, and scientific advancement, benefiting society as a whole. However, some research findings and technologies possess dual-use potential. This kind of research has both civilian and military applications. This duality raises concerns about the unintended proliferation of sensitive knowledge and its exploitation.
Keywords: security classification; academic research; open-source information; dual-use technologies; national security

title: Hierarchical Multimodal Disinformation Detection System for Armed Conflict: Integration of Forensic and Geopolitical Intelligence
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: multimodal analysis; forensic intelligence; decision rule refinement; social media disinformation; video analysis
abstract: Abstract This paper presents a specialized hierarchical multimodal system for detecting disinformation in armed conflict scenarios. The proposed two-layer fusion framework—combining geometric-mean forensic fusion and a validated decision-rule refinement (Layer1: 0.65, 0.55, 0.40; GeoScore ≥ 0.50)—achieves 100% accuracy with 0% false positives on a 30-video benchmark, ensuring zero false accusations. The system addresses a critical gap: general-purpose models experience a 22–44% degradation on military-domain media, whereas our domain-specific architecture is designed to keep degradation minimal. On the benchmark, the method achieves perfect separability, and extended dataset evaluation is planned to quantify real-world domain shift. The 8-component hierarchical fusion framework uniquely integrates: (1) Deepfake Detection Ensemble, (2) DistilBERT Linguistic Analysis with real-world corroboration, (3) LLM Ensemble Verification (Gemini 2.0 Flash and other LLMs, e.g., GPT-4, Claude), (4) AASIST Audio Forensics, (5) CLIP+CLAP Audio-Video Mismatch Detection, (6) Social Media Disinformation Scoring with geopolitical penalties, (7) novel 93-feature Geopolitical Intelligence Module (GeoScore), and (8) Cross-Modal Consistency Verification. Key innovation: Hierarchical two-layer fusion: Layer 1 applies parameter-free geometric mean on six forensic modules; Layer 2 refines via decision tree incorporating audio-video mismatch and geopolitical context. Feature importance analysis reveals forensic detection contributes 71.6%, AV-mismatch 15.2%, and GeoScore 13.3%. Performance on 30-video benchmark: 100% Precision, 100% Recall, 100% Accuracy, F1-Score 1.0. Comparative evaluation across three hierarchical approaches validates optimal architecture design.
Keywords: disinformation detection; armed conflict; hierarchical fusion; deepfake detection; geopolitical intelligence

title: Ambient Journalism after Twitter
Tags: Social Media; Journalism; News Consumption; Algorithmic Influence; AI in News
abstract: The concept of ambient journalism emerged to describe how digital platforms, particularly Twitter, enabled wide and incidental news consumption. I defined it as a broad, asynchronous, lightweight, and always-on communication system that enabled citizens to maintain a “mental model” of news, with Twitter highlighted as a leading example (Hermida, Twittering the News 297). However, the last decade has seen significant changes in the digital media environment. Twitter, now rebranded as X, has significantly declined in importance as a platform for journalists, politicians, celebrities, and the public. In this article, I revisit the theoretical foundations of ambient journalism. It examines how the concept and its connection to Twitter have evolved since 2010, reflecting the rise and fall of platforms, the increase in algorithmic curation, and the fragmentation of public attention. The decline of Twitter as a central hub for journalistic activity, competition from alternative platforms, the spread of mis- and disinformation, and the role of artificial intelligence in news curation have all contributed to a more complex ambient news environment. This development has shifted ambient journalism from a focus on collective intelligence toward an algorithmically driven stream that privileges affective resonance over narrative continuity, often leading to atomised rather than shared mental models. Looking ahead, the rise and rapid diffusion of generative AI raise questions about whether there is now an epistemological turn toward synthetic ambience. Synthetic ambience describes a phenomenon where human-like AI bots and generative AI summaries reshape how information flows. I analyse these changes, arguing that the move to algorithmic and synthetic ambience requires a critical rethinking of how news is experienced, understood, and verified in a post-Twitter world. The Roots of Ambient Journalism With the emergence and growth of social media platforms in the early 2000s, it became common for the public to share, document, and discuss news, experiences, and opinions. Twitter’s architecture was ideal for news, with its reverse-chronological format, immediacy, follower structure, and the ability to connect diverse messages through hashtags (Burgess and Baym 33). The platform thrived on expectations of sharing and public engagement, as shown in retweets, replies, and direct messages. Reports of major events like plane crashes and earthquakes often first appeared on Twitter, frequently from people involved, with the platform acting as “a nervous system for the planet” (Hermida, Tell Everyone 134). Drawing on the concept of awareness systems from ubiquitous computing, I defined ambient journalism as “an awareness system that offers diverse means to collect, communicate, share and display news and information, serving diverse purposes” (Hermida 301). The concept was developed in the context of Twitter as a media environment characterised by immediacy, serendipity, and the blending of professional and citizen journalism (Goode 1288). The original ambient journalism framework marked a departure from traditional, intentional consumption, like reading a newspaper or watching a TV news bulletin. The main features of ambient journalism were immediacy, unexpected discoveries, and the blending of professional and citizen journalism enabled by Twitter. Its architecture, with a reverse-chronological feed, a 140-character limit, and a social graph-based network structure, supported what Axel Bruns called produsage, where publics act as both users and producers of information. In this ambient environment, retweeting was not just an engagement metric but a curatorial act of journalism. This reflects a broader shift in digital journalism research toward the news ecosystem and landscape, including ambient and networked forms of journalism emerging from social media practices (Steensen and Ahva 1). The ambient news landscape has changed as platforms have developed. Algorithm-driven feeds, misinformation, and alternative sites have made the environment more fragmented and unpredictable. Where Twitter once served as a key, real-time hub during breaking news, from natural disasters to elections, now no single platform fully serves that connecting role. Newsrooms that previously relied on Twitter as an informal wire service now need to monitor multiple platforms, each with its own algorithms and norms. For audiences, losing a shared ambient space means that breaking news no longer appears through a common stream but arrives unevenly, reaching different groups at different times. This results in a more scattered, algorithmically curated mosaic that challenges the idea of ambient journalism and calls for a rethinking and redefining of the term. Networked Publics, Uneven Power The concept of a pervasive, participatory news ecosystem enabled by digital platforms, particularly Twitter, was based on users acting as both producers and consumers of news and information. Driven by the socio-technical structures of social networking sites, users formed networked publics where news was co-created and shared (boyd). Scholars have examined Twitter as a public space for networked publics during events like natural disasters, protests, or political instability, from the Arab Spring to the Occupy movement. The activist movements of the early 2010s exemplified the participatory and collective nature of ambient journalism, with Twitter serving as the link that allowed like-minded individuals to connect, communicate, and organise (Hermida, Tell Everyone 187; Papacharissi 52). Over the past decade, research has questioned the participatory concept of ambient journalism. Scholars have challenged the idea of Twitter as a mirror of public opinion (Mellon and Prosser 6; Mitchell and Hitlin par. 13), even though journalists frequently used tweets as a measure of the vox populi (Beckers and Harder 910). They viewed it as a helpful tool for newsgathering (McGregor and Molyneux 607) or to showcase ordinary people’s opinions (Tworek par. 1). While Twitter promoted an ambient awareness system, ambient journalism overlooked the power disparities between those who are seen and heard on specific issues at certain times (Hermida, Tell Everyone 101). Twitter was never a town square representing all voices. Only a minority of citizens used it, and only a minority of those users were highly active (Leetaru et al. par. 7). Accounts managed by journalists, politicians, celebrities, and bloggers were overrepresented among heavy users and dominated attention, giving them outsized influence on the network. A study on COVID-19 news sharing shows that 14 percent drove most circulation, and these creators accounted for 82 percent of false content in the dataset (Castioni et al. 1). The presence of a small group of active and influential users explains how Twitter became a key arena for highlighting important topics and political signals, even if it did not reflect the views of the broader public (Robertson). This concentration created a steady stream of signals such as retweets, replies, and metrics that spread widely beyond the core participants. Ambient journalism explains why Twitter changed how publics matter. Although the wider effects on the public sphere remain debated, this ambient environment provided a platform for networked publics to influence which stories are told and how, especially for place-based news events (Barnard 14; Bruns and Highfield 332). Taken together, these dynamics demonstrate how ambient journalism affected newsroom judgment in real time by making audience signals more immediate and difficult to ignore, without displacing editorial authority. Shifts in Platform Infrastructure Since 2010, the technological infrastructure underpinning ambient journalism has undergone a radical shift. My original conceptualisation was tethered to the affordances of early Twitter. The contemporary social media landscape, including Twitter, is defined by an algorithmic turn. In March 2016, the platform moved to algorithmic curation based on the presumed interests, preferences, and tastes of users, unless people chose to opt out. This shift transformed the ambient experience from a social utility that helps people keep up with friends and news into a relevance engine optimising passive watch time. For ambient journalism, it represented a significant shift. The ambient news in a feed became less about reflecting the priorities of a user’s connections and more about mirroring their individual habits and cues. While algorithms can help surface engaging content, they can also prioritise sensational or polarising stories over those that serve the public interest (Dujeancourt and Garz 264). The lack of transparency around algorithms exacerbates these issues, as people are often unaware of the biases and incentives shaping their news feeds. They amplify some tweets while reducing the visibility of others, playing a crucial yet hidden role in gatekeeping on Twitter (Huszár et al. 1). The mental model of news and events shifts from being based on the collective intelligence of the network (Malone et al. 2) to the social power of algorithms (Beer 1). Elon Musk’s acquisition of Twitter in 2022 and its rebranding as X in 2023 marked a turning point for ambient journalism, as the billionaire reshaped the socio-technical infrastructures underpinning the platform. He sought to reinvent it as a versatile utility, shifting from its original event-based microblogging to accommodate long-form posts and extended videos. Verification, once a symbol of trust for public figures and journalists, shifted to a subscription model based on payment rather than prominence. Musk has used the rhetoric of free speech to justify dismantling trust-and-safety teams and reinstating previously banned extremist and deceptive accounts. His portrayal as a free speech absolutist is mostly self-promotional branding rather than a consistent principle. In practice, his leadership of X has been characterised by selective enforcement, indicating that his priority is to control narratives rather than to foster open dialogue (See). These structural changes triggered cascading effects on the ambient journalism environment. The dismantling of trust-and-safety infrastructure, along with X’s withdrawal from third-party content-monitoring partnerships, undermined confidence in the platform. A more permissive moderation policy has encouraged a cultural acceptance of extreme and violent rhetoric, further degrading the quality of ambient news streams (de Keulenaar et al.). Many left-leaning academics, journalists, and news organisations reduced their presence on X or moved to alternatives like Bluesky and Mastodon (Ng and Ray 1), hollowing out the very network of professional and civic voices that supported ambient journalism on the platform. The overall result was a more unpredictable, volatile, and untrustworthy news and information environment. Collectively, Musk’s actions tore down Twitter as a place where weak ties formed the connective tissue of public conversation and, more broadly, led to “the waning of ideals in relation to the communicative potentials of the open web more generally” (Tkacz and Gehl 1). Fragmentation, Fatigue, and Fabrication The original framework for ambient journalism proposed that ubiquitous information flows would help citizens develop a mental model of public affairs through peripheral awareness of news content. Twitter was one of many ambient systems that created conditions for incidental exposure to news, with people encountering news passively rather than intentionally seeking it out (Schäfer 242). Ambient journalism predicted that news consumption would become ubiquitous, fragmented, and incidental, but it overestimated how much this peripheral exposure would help build knowledge. Instead, a decade of research shows that for many people, constant connectivity leads to less coherent understanding, increased cognitive fragmentation, and psychological stress (Bawden and Robinson par. 3). Since 2010, the relentless flow of updates has intensified, with a proliferation of platforms, smartphones, and push services, let alone major news events from Brexit to COVID-19 to Trump’s elections. Ambient infrastructures have become too successful, leading to a reaction against the very awareness systems I described. When the flow becomes too much, people have sought to limit exposure, actively avoiding updates and further coverage. While news avoidance is a complex and multilayered phenomenon (Andersen et al. 1367), it is partly driven by feelings of news overload, fatigue, and paralysis (Newman et al. 35). Ambient journalism does not create mental models of news but instead increases the cognitive load on audiences. For many, the sheer volume of news feels overwhelming and harmful to their well-being, with the constant focus of newsrooms on conflict and crisis leading to emotional exhaustion (Toff et al. 3). At the same time, the fragmentation of the social media landscape and the splintering of audiences across different platforms with unique algorithmic logics, content norms, and user demographics mean that ambient journalism can no longer be understood through just one platform. Platforms like TikTok, Instagram, Bluesky, and LinkedIn influence much of the casual news consumption, each offering different features that impact how news is presented, encountered, and interpreted. TikTok’s short videos and Instagram’s visual, story-driven format create experiences that are different from the text-based and conversational style of Twitter. The design logics of TikTok and Instagram have altered how people encounter and make sense of news. TikTok centres an algorithmically driven For You stream that learns from viewing behavior and surfaces clips that fit personal interests. This creates an incidental and episodic flow driven by affect and momentary relevance more than by ongoing narratives or social ties. Similarly, Instagram Stories pull content into a visual, short-lived format, creating a highly visual, ephemeral environment. In a high-choice and fragmented media landscape, the lightweight, always-on nature of ambient journalism can hinder rather than help understanding. These pressures intensify during crises. While mis- and disinformation are far from novel, the networked, digital media environment has amplified the scale, speed, and reach of false information (Waisbord 1866). Ambient news streams have become polluted with a chaotic mix of mis- and disinformation, rumors, hate, and emotional outbursts, making it hard to distinguish between credibility and noise. These trends have accelerated since Musk took control of Twitter, increasing the reach of toxic content. While it was once “widely lambasted as a cesspool of vanity and triviality” (Puschmann et al. 425), it is now condemned as a “sewer” polluted by disinformation, harassment, and hate (Hammond-Errey). The shift from cesspool to sewer marks a notable escalation. Early criticisms focussed on the platform’s obsession with triviality, self-promotion, and banality. Now the criticism emphasises a space where disinformation, coordinated harassment, and hate speech not only coexist with news but actively pollute it. The shift to algorithmic curation favours sensational and polarising content over credible reporting (Dujeancourt and Garz 264), while X has lost the institutional checks that once helped moderate the platform’s most extreme issues. The pay-for-verification model has removed the heuristics that helped users distinguish reliable sources from unreliable ones. The result is a fundamentally changed incentive system where news pollution is financially rewarded, algorithmically amplified, and institutionally unchecked (Hermida et al. par. 1). This weakens trust, spurs inaction and news fatigue, and contributes to polarised views (Hasell and Halversen 1448; Tandoc and Kim 1187). Discussion The core of ambient journalism, that news and information would shift from a discrete product to a pervasive environment, has largely occurred as people encounter these through networked, asynchronous, and always-on communication infrastructures. The background flow of updates from various sources, not solely journalists, has become the primary information environment for many, particularly those under 34 years old (Newman et al. 12). For these audiences, news is less an appointment and more an atmosphere. No single item constitutes the whole story. Instead, people encounter continuously updated slivers of information. The evolution of the media environment and news habits highlights the limitations of the initial, more optimistic framing of ambient journalism. Fragmented attention, mis- or disinformation, and news fatigue have undermined the value of peripheral awareness for audiences. What was once considered a tool for collective intelligence now poses risks of anxiety, fragmentation, and news avoidance. The widespread use of algorithmic curation has reshaped ambient flows, prioritising attention over informing the public. The importance of Twitter has diminished with the rise and growing use of competing platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and AI-powered aggregators. Musk’s purchase and the era of X have increased uncertainty about how news is surfaced and consumed, creating a more unpredictable environment where visibility can surpass verification. As many journalists and news organisations reduce their use of X (Ng and Ray 1), its role as a place for ambient awareness, especially during breaking news, becomes more contested and less central than it was in the 2010s. It is even questionable whether X remains a platform for ambient journalism. Algorithmic curation on X now actively de-prioritises quality journalism in favour of content that maximises engagement, while the platform’s financial incentive structure rewards the most attention-grabbing posts regardless of their veracity or public value (Dujeancourt and Garz 264). The dismantling of trust-and-safety infrastructure, the shift to pay-for-verification, and the reinstatement of previously banned accounts have collectively undermined the good-faith assumptions on which ambient journalism relied. In these conditions, what persists on X may be better understood as ambient information noise rather than ambient journalism. In a sense, the epistemological foundations that justified the term journalism have been so thoroughly compromised that the label risks lending unwarranted legitimacy to a deeply polluted environment. X still generates ambient awareness, but whether that awareness constitutes a form of journalism, even in the loose, peripheral sense originally intended, is highly questionable. Ambient journalism has, then, outgrown Twitter, prompting a re-evaluation of the concept to address the complex, algorithmically mediated, and uneven ways in which news is encountered and understood, incorporating affective resonance (Mühlhoff 1001). To account for the socio-technical and cultural changes since 2010, ambient journalism can now be defined as incidentally consumed, algorithmically guided streams of episodic news and information fragments that privilege affective resonance over narrative continuity, fostering atomised rather than collective mental models of public events. This definition recognises an epistemological shift in the infrastructures of news and information. It captures the shift from receiving news and information in fixed editions from authoritative sources to a persistent, pervasive flow of snippets from numerous voices. Outlook Recent and ongoing socio-technical advances in artificial intelligence could ultimately challenge the very concept of ambient news. Here, I introduce the term synthetic ambience, which describes a phenomenon where the ambient news environment is increasingly shaped by AI-generated content that mimics the look and rhythms of human journalism, rather than by human journalists or networked publics. AI-generated signals imitate, compress, and ultimately replace the organic flows that ambient journalism once relied on. Synthetic ambience suggests an epistemological turn, signaling a move from social ambience, rooted in networked publics, to algorithmic ambience, driven by platform curation. What distinguishes synthetic ambience from its earlier concept is the dual processes of fabrication and compression. AI-created bots and automated accounts flood information streams with fake social proof and emotional signals that shape perception. AI-produced summaries and chatbot responses shorten the diverse, multi-source signals that formed ambient journalism into a single, pre-processed synthesis, bypassing traditional human sense-making. Fabrication pollutes the system’s inputs, the signals, while compression interferes with the outputs, the cognitive processes citizens use to piece together meaning from fragments. Together, they reshape an information landscape that maintains the constant, all-pervasive nature of ambient journalism while removing its epistemic core. As a concept, synthetic ambience lies at the crossroads of journalism studies, platform studies, AI ethics, and the epistemology of information. It addresses growing concerns about how generative AI not only transforms content creation but also the very infrastructure of public knowledge, effectively creating ambient layers through which publics develop impressions of the world. The fabrication mechanism is already here. While bots have always been present on Twitter (Carlon et al.), advances in generative AI suggest that platforms like X can expect to be flooded by AI bots that are nearly indistinguishable from real accounts (Yang and Menczer 20). Where earlier forms of astroturfing and coordinated inauthentic behaviour were labour-intensive and easier to detect, generative AI now enables the mass creation of synthetic voices that imitate the tone, cadence, and emotional range of genuine users in ways that are procedurally indistinguishable from organic activity. AI bots on X and other platforms turn ambient journalism into synthetic ambience by polluting streams with a large volume of posts. Emotional triggers and synthetic social proof can create the illusion of consensus or outrage on a much larger scale than previous attempts to influence public opinion. Synthetic ambience risks overshadowing verified reporting through intentional news pollution, diverting attention and mimicking emotion at scale. The result is a reduction in viewpoint diversity and erosion of trust, benefiting those with the resources to use AI extensively for these purposes. The compression mechanism operates through a different but complementary logic. Since Google introduced AI-generated summaries in 2025, such rundowns have become one of the most common ways to interact with generative AI (Simon 57). The immediacy, convenience, and perceived veracity of the summaries are most appealing to audiences (36). Algorithmic compression, through AI-generated summaries or responses from AI chatbots like ChatGPT and Perplexity, collapses the diverse, multi-source signals that make up ambient journalism into a single, synthesised answer, shaped by the AI model’s weighting. Audiences receive a ready-made interpretation influenced by a model, rather than encountering multiple voices and gradually forming their own understanding. Algorithmic compression both models and usurps human editorial authority. AI summaries and chatbot answers offer a pre-assembled model, effectively ending the ambient journalism cycle by taking over the cognitive labour of synthesis and producing a processed sense of events. The risk is not just homogenisation but also epistemic displacement, where publics never experience the diversity of reporting, sourcing, and perspectives that once defined the ambient news environment. This makes it harder to spot gaps, contradictions, or contested claims within the information landscape. Future research could explore whether there is now an epistemological shift toward synthetic ambience. Conceptually, future work should examine whether synthetic ambience operates differently across platforms and cultural contexts, and whether its dual mechanisms of fabrication and compression produce distinct or compounding effects on public knowledge formation. As ambient journalism continues to outgrow the platform that nurtured it, there remains a need to understand how people learn and interpret news within systems shaped by networks, algorithms, and generative AI. The rise of AI-powered synthetic ambience points to an information environment that is persistent, pervasive, and increasingly processed by machine models rather than human minds. If the ambient journalism of the 2010s described how citizens understood the world through the peripheral hum of networked voices, synthetic ambience suggests an emerging situation where that hum is increasingly generated, curated, and condensed by machines, raising questions about what it means to be informed in an age of artificial intelligence. title: Multi-Modal Phishing Website Detection with Real-Time Rendering Signals and TLS Fingerprints
authors: Emily K. Dawson; Sophie L. Cartwright; James R. Whitfield
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; News Media; Leaked Data
Tags: Cybersecurity; Machine Learning; Real-Time Detection
abstract: Abstract Phishing website detection often relies solely on lexical or HTML features, which makes classifiers fragile against obfuscated URLs and template-based page cloning. This study develops a multi-modal detection framework that combines URL lexical embeddings, DOM structural features, page rendering signals (such as font entropy and visual layout similarity), and TLS certificate fingerprints. We build a dataset of 950,000 URLs, including 160,000 confirmed phishing instances collected from browser telemetry and public feeds over nine months. Character-level CNNs encode URLs, while a gradient boosting model integrates DOM and TLS features. A small Siamese CNN compares rendered screenshots with a benign-template bank to capture near-duplicate phishing pages. The framework achieves an AUC of 0.987, recall of 95.4%, and reduces false positives by 19.3% compared with a strong lexical-only baseline. Online experiments in a proxy-based deployment show that median detection latency remains below 20 ms per request. The results indicate that combining transport-layer fingerprints and rendering behavior yields robust, real-time phishing detection suitable for production environments.
Keywords: Phishing detection; TLS fingerprints; URL embedding; DOM features; Rendering signals

title: Cybersecurity challenges in energy harvesting systems
authors: Ravi Kumar; Mohd Anas Wajid; Claudia Camacho-Zuñiga; Akib Mohi Ud Din Khanday; Mohammad Saif Wajid
venue: Intersection of Machine Learning and Computational Social Sciences
Tags: cybersecurity; energy harvesting; IoT security; vulnerabilities; case studies
abstract: This chapter delves into the critical issue of industrial cybersecurity attacks in the context of energy harvesting (EH) technologies. As EH gains momentum in various industrial applications, the integration of smart devices and the Internet of Things (IoT) introduces new cybersecurity challenges. This study comprehensively examines the emerging threats and vulnerabilities associated with EH systems in industrial environments. This chapter analyzes a spectrum of cyberattack vectors that can compromise the integrity, availability, and confidentiality of data and control systems within EH networks. These attack vectors include malware injection, Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks, firmware manipulation, and supply chain vulnerabilities. Moreover, the research explores the potential consequences of successful cyberattacks on EH infrastructure, ranging from production disruptions to equipment damage and financial losses. Understanding these risks is essential for industries to develop robust cybersecurity strategies. To mitigate these threats, the chapter identifies current best practices and effective mitigation measures for safeguarding EH systems. In addition, real-world case studies of cyberattacks on EH systems in industrial settings provide valuable insights into attacker tactics and techniques. The chapter emphasizes the significance of collaboration and information sharing among industry stakeholders, government agencies, and cybersecurity experts to fortify the overall security posture of EH infrastructure. The chapter also highlights the pressing need for proactive and comprehensive cybersecurity measures in industrial EH. It focuses on potential threats and the adoption of effective countermeasures in order to ensure the secure and sustainable growth of energy harvesting technologies while safeguarding critical infrastructures from cyberattacks.
Keywords: energy harvesting; cybersecurity; industrial applications; IoT; cyberattacks; mitigation strategies

title: Machine Unlearning-Enabled Cyber-Resilient AIoT Framework for Privacy-Preserving and Adaptive Smart Pharmaceutics in Personalized Drug Delivery Systems
authors: SAYED ATHAR ALI HASHMI; Pushpendra Tiwari; Naveen Datt Dixit
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: privacy-preserving; healthcare; cyber-resilience; personalized medicine; GDPR compliance
abstract: Abstract The rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) in smart pharmaceutics promises personalized and adaptive drug delivery systems with real-time monitoring and precise dosing. However, these interconnected systems face critical challenges in data privacy, cybersecurity threats, and compliance with “right to be forgotten” regulations such as GDPR and DPDPA. Current AIoT frameworks lack efficient mechanisms to selectively remove sensitive patient data from trained models without full retraining, increasing vulnerability to privacy attacks and model bias. This paper proposes a Machine Unlearning-Enabled Cyber-Resilient AIoT Framework for privacy-preserving and adaptive smart pharmaceutics. The framework integrates edge AIoT sensors, a personalized dosing prediction model, an efficient machine unlearning module, and a distributed digital twin-based cybersecurity layer for real-time detection of insider and external threats. Simulation results on healthcare IoT datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces privacy leakage by up to 92%, maintains over 95% model accuracy after unlearning, and lowers computational overhead by 40–60% compared to complete retraining baselines. It also exhibits strong resilience against common AIoT cyberattacks while ensuring regulatory compliance. This work bridges pharmacy, artificial intelligence, and cybersecurity, offering a scalable privacy-by-design solution for next-generation personalized drug delivery systems.
Keywords: AIoT; privacy; cybersecurity; drug delivery; machine unlearning
affiliations: All India Institute of Medical Sciences Raipur; Chhattisgarh Kamdhenu Vishwavidyalaya; Chhattisgarh Council of Science and Technology; Dalmia Institute of Scientific & Industrial Research

title: Toward Performance-Based Detection of Attacks in Microservice Systems
authors: Andrea Janes; Diaeddin Rimawi; Refat Tahseen Othman; Alberto Avritzer; Raffaela Mirandola; Barbara Russo
venue: Companion of the 17th ACM/SPEC International Conference on Performance Engineering
Tags: performance engineering; cybersecurity; microservices; attack detection
abstract: This poster paper links performance engineering and cybersecurity by presenting an architecture for performance-based attack detection in microservice systems. We define a workflow based on service-level runtime behavior rather than network signatures or logs. The architecture integrates workload generation, attack injection, and continuous collection of CPU, memory, filesystem activity, and response-time metrics. In future work, we will evaluate this architecture on benchmarking applications that reflect realistic architectures reported by practitioners. The goal is to assess whether runtime performance behavior can support service-level attack detection.
Keywords: microservice systems; performance-based detection; attack detection; runtime behavior; cybersecurity

title: Contextual Relationship Feature-Enhanced Steganalysis for Social Texts
authors: Kaibo Huang; Xiaohan Zhang; Wansheng Wu; Yukun Wei; Zhongliang Yang
venue: ICASSP 2026 - 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP)
Tags: steganography; text analysis; feature extraction; cyber defense
abstract: Steganalysis techniques for social media are used primarily to detect potential steganographic content within social networks, contributing to cybersecurity efforts. Most existing research focuses on analyzing individual media features, neglecting the social connectivity aspects that are crucial for identifying steganographic texts in fragmented social media environments. To address this gap, we propose a feature-enhanced linguistic steganalysis model that integrates improved node features, optimized message passing, and robust feature fusion. This model enables more accurate detection of steganographic texts even in complex social media scenarios. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art steganalysis techniques in both accuracy and efficiency.
Keywords: steganalysis; social media; cybersecurity; linguistic features; detection
affiliations: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
