title: Optimization-Driven Multi-Domain Hybrid Framework for Phishing Detection Across URL, Network, and Email Layers Using Deep Learning and Heuristic Intelligence
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: cybersecurity; machine learning; email security; network security
abstract: Abstract Phishing attacks have evolved into click activity that operates across URL, network, and email channels simultaneously, automatic redirecting user, rendering single-domain detection systems insufficient data for real-world deployment. This research presents an optimization-driven multilayer multi-domain hybrid phishing detection framework that incorporates transport-layer TLS communication analysis, URL essential features, and email header, content features within an OSI-aligned three-cluster architecture (C0–Transport, C1–Session, C2–Application). Four available datasets totaling over 762,000 samples are employed, spanning URL lexical features, TLS infrastructure fingerprints, and hybrid multi-domain representations. A feature optimization pipeline based on the Heuristic-Constrained Class-Conditional Feature Selection (HC²FS) algorithm reduces dimensionality from 700 + features into three clusters by 25–50% per cluster while preserving discriminative power. Three deep learning architectures, Transformer, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) are comparatively under controlled adversarial feature-degradation scenarios simulating 20%, 30%, and 50% missing features. Experimental results demonstrate that LSTM achieves the highest overall performance, reaching 99.62% accuracy and AUC = 0.9949 on the TLS infrastructure dataset, at the same time Hybrid architecture provides the most robust performance under adversarial conditions, which reduces the gap in real word deployment. The proposed framework addresses six documented research gaps: dataset fragmentation, inconsistent feature engineering, suboptimal hyperparameter tuning, architectural isolation of detection components, interpretability deficit, and evaluation bias. The Modified Bonobo Optimizer (MBO) is applied for joint hyperparameter co-optimization, achieving F1 > 0.97 and false-positive rate (FPR)
Keywords: phishing detection; deep learning; heuristic intelligence; multi-domain; TLS analysis
affiliations: MIT Art, Design and Technology University

title: Exploring Cybersecurity Awareness Among Street Vendors: A Pathway to Inclusive Growth
venue: Emerging Trends in Smart Computing and Sustainable Communication
Tags: cybersecurity; digital literacy; formal economy; online fraud; education
abstract: Digitalization has globally transformed the business and offered exceptional opportunities for promoting inclusive growth. In today's digital world, cybersecurity has become a crucial issue for not only large companies but also for small business owners, such as street vendors. The Government of India also aims to make vendors a part of the formal economy by launching Digital India and PM SVANidhi Yojana. Street vendors, who form a significant part of the economy, are also increasingly adopting digital tools, especially mobile payments, to expand their businesses. The transformation to a digitalized society is also bringing cybersecurity threats for the stakeholders. A substantial gap lies between awareness of digital options and understanding the cybersecurity threats. Present research aims to explore the cybersecurity awareness among street vendors, focusing on how they interact with digital tools and the various vulnerabilities they face. The study is descriptive in nature, where data have been collected from Street vendors with the help of a questionnaire. The study reveals that street vendors are facing various cybersecurity threats, such as fake payment screenshots, suspicious links, and unauthorized deductions. Findings reveal that there is a significant impact of education on vulnerabilities to cyber threats. Many street vendors are not aware of how to protect themselves from online fraud and identify scams. The study suggests that policymakers, local government, NGOs, and various educational institutions should ensure a digital literacy framework. It is essential to promote cyber safety education among the informal sector so that all groups can benefit from the opportunities created by digitalization.
Keywords: cybersecurity awareness; street vendors; digitalization; inclusive growth; mobile payments

title: Implications of artificial intelligence and cyberspace on risk management capabilities
authors: Hatidža Beriša; Vanja Cvetković; Aleksandar Pavić
venue: International Journal of Disaster Risk Management
Tags: cyber defense; modern warfare; cyber threats; AI implications
abstract: The crisis management system is a key element of a modern, reliable, effective, and sustainable security system. In contemporary settings, management functions are implemented through the capabilities of a command-information system. Consequently, most activities are conducted within cyberspace, meaning that management functions cannot be effectively executed without adequate telecommunications and information technology support. The primary target of hostile cyber activities is precisely the command-information system. Developing an optimal methodology for the use of command-information systems, as well as for prevention and protection against cyber-attacks, is among the most critical tasks for modern defence and security systems. One approach to enhancing risk management is through the application of artificial intelligence (AI). AI should ensure superiority in cyberspace, primarily at the national level, providing the foundation for executing legitimate and lawful cyber activities in a state's defence system. This paper aims to explore the role and significance of artificial intelligence within the realm of cybersecurity by analyzing its implications for risk management in contemporary environments. Command in modern military operations, the nature of modern warfare risks, the protection of command-information systems, the autonomy of artificial intelligence in decision-making, and an understanding of cyber threats are factors through which the challenges of modern command systems can be understood.
Keywords: artificial intelligence; cyberspace; risk management; cybersecurity; command-information systems

title: AI and Digital Evidence in Indian Sexual Assault Cases: A Legal-Ethical Analysis
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Government Data; Academic Literature
Tags: India; Justice System; AI Integration; Digital Evidence
abstract: This research examines the integration of artificial intelligence in India's digital forensic landscape, with particular emphasis on its application in sexual assault inquiries. As India confronts escalating cybercrime rates and increasingly complex digital evidence, forensic laboratories in the country have begun implementing artificial intelligence driven solutions to process and analyse electronic data. The study investigates specific artificial intelligence tools deployed by agencies such as the Central Forensic Science Laboratory and state police cyber cells, including facial recognition systems, automated malware analysis tools, among others. The paper encompasses this analysis in the instances of sexual assault forensics, where substantial backlogs and manual processing methods contribute to delayed justice. It discovers how artificial intelligence models, including automated biological evidence analysis, accelerated DNA matching, intelligent evidence prioritisation systems, and unconventional digital evidence processing, can potentially reduce evidence dispensation time from months to days, enabling faster filing of chargesheets within statutory limits. The research further examines how these technologies can be integrated with existing initiatives such as the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal and the Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C) to create specialised pathways for rape and child abuse cases. Ethical and legal concerns are a major aspect when the usage of AI in forensics is put forward. The paper will sketch how the new Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, deals with this amalgamation of AI and its interaction with India's constitutional framework, the new Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP), and the existing IT Act. Additionally, it analyses recent judicial attitudes toward digital evidence and also compares the usage of AI forensic tools in different countries and how the usage has helped the police and judicial authorise to speed up the justice process. The research concludes by proposing a comprehensive ethical framework tailored to guide the responsible integration of AI in India's digital forensic ecosystem, balancing the benefits of AI, including drastically reduced evidence processing times, heightened detection competencies, and expedited judicial proceedings, with essential protections to algorithmic transparency standards and vigorous data protection safeguards. By potentially reducing investigation timelines from years to months, AI integration offers transformative potential for delivering timely justice to sexual assault survivors, which can resolve key loopholes in criminal investigations.
Keywords: AI; Digital Forensics; Sexual Assault; Cybercrime; Ethical Framework

title: ForenGRAF-AI: An Explainable Graph-Temporal Artificial Intelligence Artifact for Digital Forensic
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: ForenGRAF-AI; Evidential Relevance; Temporal Reasoning; Decision Support System
abstract: Abstract Digital forensic investigation is increasingly constrained by the scale, heterogeneity, volatility, and legal sensitivity of contemporary digital evidence. Cloud infrastructures, mobile devices, endpoint telemetry, encrypted applications, containerized services, and adversarially manipulated logs have transformed digital forensics from a largely artifact-centered discipline into a problem of high-dimensional temporal reasoning under uncertainty. Artificial intelligence offers substantial opportunities to accelerate triage, prioritize evidence, reconstruct event sequences, and discover latent relationships among digital artifacts. However, opaque AI systems may undermine forensic soundness, reproducibility, interpretability, and evidential admissibility if they are not designed around forensic requirements. This article investigates the relationship between Digital Forensic Analysis and Artificial Intelligence and proposes ForenGRAF-AI, a complex yet feasible artifact for forensic investigation. ForenGRAF-AI is an explainable graph-temporal AI application that ingests heterogeneous forensic artifacts, constructs a signed temporal provenance graph, learns suspicious behavioral embeddings through graph neural networks and temporal variational modeling, estimates evidential relevance via Bayesian reasoning, and produces human-verifiable investigative hypotheses. The artifact is developed using the Design Science Research methodology, which is suitable when the research contribution is the construction and evaluation of an artifact intended to solve an identified organizational and technical problem [49]-[51]. The principal contribution is a mathematically grounded, explainable, and forensically constrained AI architecture for digital forensic triage and event reconstruction. Unlike conventional anomaly-detection systems, ForenGRAF-AI explicitly models evidential provenance, uncertainty, temporal causality, artifact reliability, and chain-of-custody requirements. The article presents the theoretical foundation, system architecture, mathematical formulation, data strategy, evaluation design, limitations, and implications for forensic practice. The proposed artifact is positioned not as an autonomous forensic decision-maker but as a decision-support system that assists qualified investigators while preserving transparency, repeatability, and evidential accountability.
Keywords: Digital Forensics; Artificial Intelligence; Explainable AI; Graph-Typical Analysis; Event Reconstruction

title: Research on Incremental Network Intrusion Detection Based on Hunting Algorithm and Class Balance Strategy
authors: Wenqiang Yu; Xianfeng Guo; Xinyue Yan; Xi Chen; Make Mao
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: NIDS; machine learning; cyber attacks; data management
abstract: Abstract With the continuous evolution of network environments and attack techniques, traditional network intrusion detection systems (NIDS) trained in a static manner struggle to effectively adapt to newly emerging attacks, while frequent model retraining incurs substantial computational overhead and maintenance cost. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an incremental network intrusion detection model, namely IDS-We, based on the Herding algorithm and a class-balancing strategy. The proposed method builds an exemplar memory mechanism, selects representative historical samples via Herding, and introduces a class-balanced scheduling strategy during incremental training to jointly manage new and old data. Under a limited storage budget, the method effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting and class imbalance, enabling continual learning of new attack types.We conduct systematic experimental analyses on three public network traffic datasets CIC-IDS2017, UNSW-NB15, and CSE-CIC-IDS2018 under three evaluation settings: in-dataset evaluation, cross-dataset evaluation, and extended evaluation. Experimental results show that the proposed model consistently outperforms existing incremental learning methods in both binary and multi-class tasks, achieving more substantial improvements in metrics such as F1-score and accuracy. In particular, under cross-dataset evaluation, the proposed method exhibits stronger generalization capability and more stable detection performance, validating its effectiveness and scalability for dynamic network environments and long-term intrusion detection deployment.
Keywords: network intrusion detection; incremental learning; Herding algorithm; class balance strategy; cybersecurity

title: The Impact of Darknet and Dark Web Environments on Cyber Sovereignty
venue: Dövlətin kibersuverenliyinin təmin olunmasının elmi-praktiki problemləri respublika konfransı
Tags: Cybersecurity; Digital Transformation; Legal Regulation; International Cooperation
abstract: In the context of digital transformation, the concept of state sovereignty is acquiring new dimensions, with the notion of cyber sovereignty becoming increasingly prominent. The borderless nature of the Internet and its distributed network architecture enable the rapid transmission of information beyond national boundaries, thereby reducing the effectiveness of traditional state control mechanisms. This study analyzes the Internet environment across three layers Surface Web, Deep Web, and Darknet and examines their impact on cyber sovereignty. The findings indicate that these technological characteristics complicate legal regulation and increase risks related to information security, including disinformation, manipulation, cyberattacks, and cyber threats. Consequently, the issue of cyber sovereignty requires a comprehensive approach that integrates legal, technological, and international cooperation frameworks.
Keywords: Cyber Sovereignty; Dark Web; Information Security; Disinformation; Cyber Threats

title: Cybersecurity and the Art of Cyberwar
authors: Dan Shoemaker; Tamara Shoemaker; Amir Jabri
Tags: national security; cyber threats; attack surfaces; security controls
abstract: The art of war is of vital importance to the state. It is a matter of life or death. Hence, it is a subject which can on no account be neglected.—Sun Tzu Why are we calling this war? It’s because the conflict in cyberspace is a matter of national concern, and we are, most assuredly, losing the current struggle. So, what do we do? The person who best understands war is a 2,300-year-old Chinese general who saw conflict as more about process and strategy than fighting. Which, in essence, is the holistic concept. Hence, this book is founded on two principles. First, cybersecurity is a state, not a technical solution. Accordingly, people and organizations must take active steps to both design and sustain a consistently secure operational process. Second, it’s given that you have not attained the desired state if you have failed to deploy all of the controls necessary to achieve it. The catch-all term for such an approach is “holistic.” A holistic control architecture is the only way to achieve adequate protection. Therefore, holistic cyber defenses embody a single, unified system of electronic, behavioral, and physical controls that enforce continuous security across all common attack surfaces. Why does the solution need to be holistic? It is because the cybersecurity function is responsible for securing three distinct and disparate types of attacks – electronic, human, and physical. And just as in war, the adversary doesn’t care which attack surface they breach. They only want your stuff, and they will do whatever it takes to get it. In that respect, then, the only practical approach to cybersecurity is to create an integrated, appropriately tailored defense against any viable threat … electronic, human, or physical. This book describes the lifecycle process for conceptualizing, implementing, and maintaining a holistic control solution. It centers on a well-defined process for planning, building, and ensuring comprehensive, in-depth cyber defense. In that respect, then, we will demonstrate why holistic security is the only way for an organization to identify and address all valid threats to its digital resources.
Keywords: cybersecurity; holistic defense; cyberwar; threat management; security architecture

title: Security of information resources in federal college of education libraries in northwest Nigeria
authors: Kayode Sunday John Dada; Hafsat Aliyu Mohammed; Diana Mallam; Emmanuel Oluwaseun Ajayi
venue: International Journal of Contemporary Security Studies
Tags: library security; cybersecurity awareness; resource protection; policy recommendations
abstract: This study investigates the security of information resources in Federal College of Education libraries in Northwest Nigeria, surveying 74 librarians using a descriptive survey design. A structured questionnaire based on a 4-point Likert scale (Strongly Agree = 4, Agree = 3, Disagree = 2, Strongly Disagree = 1) assessed perceptions of security measures, including policies, training, electronic systems, and physical safeguards. Data were analysed using the average weighted mean, with a benchmark of 2.50 indicating agreement. Findings reveal moderate security implementation, with only physical security measures scoring above the benchmark (M = 2.64). In contrast, security policies (M = 2.35), staff training (M = 2.12), and electronic systems (M = 2.41) were deemed inadequate. Key challenges include insufficient funding, inadequate training, weak policy frameworks, and erratic power supply, which exacerbate vulnerabilities to theft, mutilation, and cyber threats. These align with prior research highlighting resource constraints and low cybersecurity awareness in Nigerian libraries. The study recommends increased funding for advanced security infrastructure, regular training in cybersecurity, development of robust policies, and investment in alternative power sources, such as solar systems, to ensure consistent electronic security. By addressing these gaps, libraries can better protect resources critical for academic advancement. The findings contribute to the discourse on library security in resource-constrained settings, offering actionable insights for policymakers and library administrators to enhance resource protection and align with global standards. Future research should explore user perspectives and the cost-effectiveness of proposed interventions.
Keywords: information security; libraries; cyber threats; Nigerian education; security policies

title: OSINT Report on Current Vulnerabilities in Indonesia
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; Government Data; News Media
Tags: OSINT; vulnerabilities assessment; Indonesia; policy analysis
abstract: This report provides an open-source intelligence assessment of Indonesia’s principal national vulnerabilities as of May 17, 2026. The overall judgment is that Indonesia is not on the edge of systemic collapse. Macroeconomic growth, inflation, and reserve buffers continue to provide a measure of resilience. Its most dangerous vulnerabilities, however, do not sit in a single economic indicator. They lie in the coupled amplification among policy credibility, fiscal space, energy-import pressure, capital-market transparency, social protest, disaster exposure, cyber governance, and resource policy. If a Middle East energy shock, rating or index pressure, implementation failures in large social programs such as free meals, local protests, or major disasters emerge at the same time, Indonesia could move from a zone of manageable pressure into a zone of compound stress. The report divides the vulnerabilities into 10 categories and reviews them through evidence layering, an impact-probability matrix, trigger monitoring, alternative explanations, and scenario analysis. The core conclusion is that over the next 0-18 months, the main issue to monitor is not any isolated event but a chain of events linking declining fiscal credibility, rising foreign-investor risk premiums, expanding energy subsidies, defects in social-policy implementation, and disaster-related emergency fiscal burdens.
Keywords: Indonesia; vulnerabilities; open-source intelligence; cyber governance; fiscal policy; economic resilience
affiliations: Dhurakij Pundit University; The University of Western Australia; Royal Anthropological Institute

title: OSINT Engineering Assessment of Alleged Bribery Risk in Thai State Agencies
OSINT_Source_Types: Public Records; News Media; Government Data
Tags: corruption; transparency; OECD; public sector; investigation
abstract: This report conducts an engineered open-source intelligence assessment of the alleged bribery risk survey concerning Thai state agencies, disclosed by Thailand’s private sector in May 2026. The core source base consists of the original JSCCIB/Zero Corruption/Puean Mai Thon survey page, a sequence of reports by The Nation Thailand, public responses from named agencies, Transparency International CPI/GCB indicators, and OECD/NESDC public information on Thailand’s OECD accession process and its move toward the OECD Anti-Bribery Convention. The report applies strict evidentiary downgrading. The survey results are sufficient to support the judgment that some state-agency interfaces carry elevated perceived bribery-risk in corporate contacts, licensing, regulation, enforcement, and procurement. They are not sufficient, by themselves, to support a legal finding that any specific agency or official has accepted a bribe. The integrated judgment identifies two concentrated risk dimensions. The first is high-contact-frequency risk: highway/traffic police, judicial-process agencies, subdistrict administrative organizations, the Marine Department, and the Department of Highways are reported as having high frequencies of alleged benefit solicitation in corporate contacts. The second is high-amount risk: the Pollution Control Department, the Marine Department, the Excise Department, the Revenue Department, and judicial-process agencies are reported in higher ranges for average alleged amounts. Counter-RAG counterevidence indicates that the Pollution Control Department and the Marine Department publicly disputed the conclusions and requested more granular evidence from JSCCIB. At the same time, the Ministry of Transport initiated a fact review. These counterpoints do not overturn the fact that the survey exists and has created reputational risk.
Keywords: OSINT; bribery; Thailand; risk assessment; state agencies
affiliations: Dhurakij Pundit University; The University of Western Australia; Royal Anthropological Institute

title: Red-Teaming the Feedback Loop: Adversarial Cascade Simulation in Synthetic Primary Care
Tags: cybersecurity; healthcare; AI attack; simulation; robustness assessment
abstract: Administrative artificial-intelligence (AI) pipelines in primary care are vulnerable to cascade amplification when feedback loops are present. Whether the six adversarial attack classes catalogued in our earlier threat taxonomy can be deliberately triggered through realistic perturbations, and whether a distributional monitoring pattern can distinguish adversarial perturbation from accidental drift, has not been tested in silico. We extend our previously published five-node Synthea-based simulation of administrative AI in primary care (1,000 synthetic patients; 100 Monte Carlo iterations per scenario) with eight adversarial scenarios spanning single-node, coordinated multi-node, stealth-ramp, and supply-chain compromise patterns, plus a novel data-poisoning attack using the publicly available Medical-Triage-500 dataset (HuggingFace) validated against 41,470 patient encounters from the MedDialog/HealthCareMagic-100k corpus. Results: the stealth-ramp scenario (1 per cent perturbation magnitude over 100 feedback cycles) is the only configuration crossing the previously reported clinical-relevance threshold for cascade amplification (Cascade Amplification Factor > 1.2), with total error 2.94 versus baseline accidental-drift 0.31. Single-shot adversarial injection at 10 per cent magnitude without feedback reaches total error 0.39, only 23 per cent above baseline, operationally indistinguishable from noise. Coordinated attacks at the Documentation and Coding nodes produce 1.55 times the error of single-node attacks under identical magnitude and feedback. The data-poisoning attack produces an 11.7 per cent undertriage rate at population scale. We argue that adversarial-robustness assessment for clinical AI pipelines must address persistent low-magnitude perturbation through feedback loops, not only high-magnitude single-shot scenarios. An Ontological Intrusion-Detection Architecture as constructive response is developed in a companion paper.
Keywords: adversarial AI; feedback loops; cascade amplification; data poisoning; clinical AI
affiliations: Kingsley House; Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg

title: PhishViT: A Vision Transformer-Based Framework for Phishing Detection from Webpage Screenshots
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: Cybersecurity; Machine Learning; Computer Vision; Phishing
abstract: Abstract Phishing attacks represent one of the most pervasive and economically devastating cyber threats, with conventional detection systems relying primarily on URL lexical analysis, DNS inspection, and HTML source-code heuristics. These text-centric approaches share a fundamental blind spot: they do not examine the visual rendering of webpages as perceived by human users, leaving a critical detection gap exploited by visual-layer spoofing attacks. This paper presents PhishViT , a Vision Transformer- based framework for real-time phishing detection that operates exclusively on webpage screenshots. Unlike methods that analyze URL strings or page source code, PhishViT learns discriminative visual representations directly from rendered webpage images using a fine-tuned Data-efficient Image Transformer (DeiT-Small) architecture. An automated Playwright browser pipeline captures live screenshots which are classified as phishing or legitimate, with interpretable attention rollout heatmaps generated for each prediction. The framework is developed through an iterative three-phase process, starting from an initial prototype (V1: 253 screenshots, 78.95% accuracy), expanding to a balanced dataset (V2: 642 screenshots, 96.91% accuracy), and culminating in a rigorous top-tier evaluation framework (V3) with comprehensive baseline comparison against ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, and ViT-Base; 5-fold cross-validation confirming 85.23% ± 1.18% accuracy ; ablation study validating each design choice; and robustness evaluation under six visual perturbation conditions. V3 evaluation demonstrates that DeiT-Small achieves 91.75% accu- racy , 93.48% precision, 89.58% recall, 91.49% F1-score, and AUC-ROC of 0.9928 at only 5.44 ms inference latency, outper- forming EfficientNet-B0 and ViT-Base while achieving the best efficiency-accuracy trade-off. These results establish PhishViT as a viable, interpretable, and computationally efficient visual-layer phishing detection framework suitable for real-time browser ex- tension deployment.
Keywords: Phishing Detection; Vision Transformer; Webpage Screenshots; Real-time Detection; Visual Spoofing

title: Zero trust architectures and cybersecurity frameworks for mitigating AI-enabled misinformation campaigns
venue: Combating Misinformation in the Age of Generative AI
Tags: Cybersecurity; AI; Misinformation; Public Discourse; Information Integrity
abstract: The endemic artificial intelligence (AI) facilitated misinformation encompassing deepfake videos, fake content, and machine-based propaganda campaigns that pose a huge threat to digital trust, public discourse, and national security. This chapter discusses how Zero Trust Architectures (ZTA) and next-generation cybersecurity models can be an effective building block to counter disinformation in the contemporary world. Though focusing on transformation from the strict trust boundaries to continuous verification, ZTA enables secure content authentication, access management, and behavior monitoring at every layer of information flow. The technical overview of ZTA principles and their integration of AI with detection models, API controls, identity management, and blockchain-based proof-of-origin systems are also covered in the chapter. Apart from this, it aligns the aforesaid technologies with international standards such as the NIST Cybersecurity Framework, MITRE AT&CK, and ISO/IEC standards to counter misinformation. Through case studies and best practices from actual action, this chapter shows how layered, proactive measures are needed to counter synthetic media manipulation and recover the integrity of the information space. This book encourages a multidisciplinary collaboration for the purpose of developing a robust cyber infrastructure to withstand future rounds of AI-driven fraud.
Keywords: Zero Trust Architectures; AI-enabled misinformation; cybersecurity frameworks; digital trust; deepfake; content authentication

title: CEDF-CS: A Record-Level Data CondensationFramework for Resource-Efficient Intrusion Detectionin Industrial IoT and Heterogeneous Network Environments
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: IIoT; Machine Learning; Feature Selection; Network Security
abstract: Abstract Cybersecurity systems increasingly rely on deep learning for intrusion detection, yet such models demand large labeled datasetsand GPU hardware, rendering them impractical for resource-constrained Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) environments. Thispaper introduces the Cybersecurity Data Condensation and Discriminative Feature Extraction Framework (CEDF-CS), amulti-level pipeline that compresses cybersecurity datasets by a factor of 32 while preserving and amplifying their discriminativestructure. The framework applies hierarchical record-pair fusion to label-sorted, Z-score-normalized network traffic data,followed by adaptive correlation-based feature selection, and trains lightweight classifiers (SVM and shallow neural networks)on the compact representation. A central finding is that the 32× compressed dataset serves as a faithful and sufficientrepresentation of the full-scale data: models trained exclusively on the compressed set and evaluated on the entire originaldataset achieve F1 = 0.994 on the WUSTL-IIoT dataset (binary, 630,000 records) and Pearson R = 0.985 on CICIDS2017(six-class, 2.93 million records). These results are obtained through purely linear operations (averaging and correlation) thatproduce feature spaces with discriminative properties typically associated with deep nonlinear architectures. The frameworkoperates without GPUs, reduces training time by over 90%, eliminates 58–59% of features, and preserves minority-classstructure even when the rarest attack class comprises only 0.06% of records.
Keywords: Intrusion Detection; Industrial IoT; Data Condensation; Deep Learning; Cybersecurity

title: Knowledge, Attitudes, and Behavioral Risk Patterns Associated with Cybercrime Involvement Among Nigerian University Undergraduates
authors: ADEDEJI ABDULRAHMON ALAYANDE; ADEBISI OLUWAJUWON ADENIRAN; TAIWO DORCAS ADEBOWALE; DAMILARE ADEOLU OLAWUNI; MARVELLOUS ADEOLA ADEDEJI; HABEEBULAH ABDULLAHI
venue: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
Tags: public health; mental wellbeing; social consequences
abstract: Abstract Background Cybercrime is increasingly perpetrated across all age groups; however, young people remain disproportionately involved, with rising participation over time. This trend presents a growing public health concern, given its associated psychological distress, financial harm, and broader social consequences. Investigating cybercrime among youth is therefore essential for informing preventive strategies and interventions that protect mental and psychosocial well-being. This study assessed the knowledge, attitudes, and practices related to cybercrime, as well as factors influencing its practice among undergraduate students of Osun State University. Methods A descriptive cross-sectional study of 423 undergraduates ages 15–29 years in a tertiary institution, recruited using a multi-stage sampling technique. A self-administered questionnaire was used to collect data from the study respondents. Data collected from the research were processed and analyzed using Statistical Product and Service Solutions (SPSS) Version 23.0. Descriptive statistics using means, SD, frequency tables, percentages, and charts were used to report frequencies of the variables. Chi-square test, Fisher’s exact test, and binary logistic regression were used to determine the factors influencing respondents' involvement in cybercrime. The level of statistical significance was set at a p-value
Keywords: cybercrime; undergraduates; Nigerian universities; youth involvement; preventive strategies
affiliations: University of Ibadan; Osun State University; ITMO University

title: Enhancing Advanced Persistent Threat Detection: An Exploration of Particle Swarm Optimisation and Genetic Programming Techniques
Tags: APT; Cybersecurity; Evolutionary Computation; Threat Detection; Feature Construction
abstract: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are prolonged and carefully orchestrated cyberattacks that use stealth, persistence, and multi-stage techniques to evade detection. Identifying these attacks remains difficult due to limited and imbalanced datasets, subtle behavioural differences between early attack phases and benign traffic, and the complexity of modelling multi-phase behaviours. Traditional approaches often depend on extensive expert-driven feature engineering, which can be costly and difficult to update as threats evolve. This thesis aims to investigate how Evolutionary Computation (EC) methods, in particular Particle Swarm Optimisation (PSO) and Genetic Programming (GP), can be used to support APT detection and classification in the presence of dataset limitations, class imbalance, and evolving attack characteristics. The research explores three complementary directions: using PSO for early-phase APT detection, applying GP for evolutionary feature construction in multi-phase settings, and developing a multi-tree GP framework that evolves separate trees to generate class-specific constructed features. The thesis begins with a detailed analysis of four publicly available APT datasets, examining their feature characteristics, phase coverage, class imbalance, and preprocessing requirements. Baseline Machine Learning (ML) experiments using imbalance-aware metrics highlight how severe class imbalance, limited phase coverage, and feature-quality issues constrain performance, and provide a consistent evaluation framework for subsequent chapters. To address early-phase detection, the thesis proposes a cooperative coevolution PSO-based classifier (CMS-PSOC) that assigns a swarm to each class and optimises a geometric mean (G-mean) objective to improve performance across Benign, Reconnaissance, and Establish foothold traffics. Experiments on the DAPT-2020 dataset show that CMS-PSOC achieves competitive, low-variance performance, demonstrating effective alignment with imbalance-aware objectives. For multi-phase APT detection, the thesis introduces a single-tree GP feature-construction framework (GP–DT), where internal nodes of the evolved tree act as constructed features and are evaluated using a Decision Tree (DT) wrapper and other downstream classifiers. Results on SCVICAPT-2021, DAPT-2020, and Unraveled show that GP–DT often enhances or maintains performance, particularly on SCVIC-APT-2021 multi-class tasks. To further support phase-specific detection, a class-informed multi-tree GP method (FEGPDT) is proposed, evolving one tree per class to construct phase-targeted features. Experiments on SCVIC-APT-2021 demonstrate that FEGPDT achieves competitive balanced accuracy in both binary and multi-class settings and frequently outperforms or matches GP–DT and other evolutionary methods. SHAP-based analyses provide additional insight into the relevance of the constructed features. Across all contributions, the thesis provides a cohesive examination of how EC can complement existing ML approaches by supporting imbalance-aware optimisation, adaptive feature construction, and structured modelling of phase-specific behaviours. The findings offer practical guidance for designing APT detection pipelines and highlight areas where dataset design and evaluation practices can be improved to better support future research.
Keywords: Advanced Persistent Threats; Particle Swarm Optimisation; Genetic Programming; Threat Detection; Machine Learning; Feature Engineering

title: Malice in Agentland: Down the Rabbit Hole of Backdoors in the AI Supply Chain
authors: Léo Boisvert; Abhay Puri; Chandra Kiran Reddy Evuru; Nazanin Mohammadi Sepahvand; Nicolas Chapados; Quentin Cappart; Alexandre Lacoste; Krishnamurthy Dvijotham; Alexandre Drouin; Jason Stanley
venue: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
Tags: Backdoors; Cybersecurity; AI Agents; Vulnerabilities
abstract: While finetuning AI agents on interaction data—such as web browsing or tool use—improves their capabilities, it also introduces critical security vulnerabilities within the agentic AI supply chain.
Keywords: AI Supply Chain; Security Vulnerabilities; Finetuning; Agentic AI
